# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**State and explain the Peltier Effect. Why is it considered a reversible thermoelectric phenomenon?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Peltier Effect** is the phenomenon of heat absorption or release at the junction of two dissimilar conductors when an electric current is passed through them. When DC current $I$ flows through a junction of materials $a$ and $b$, the rate of heat absorbed/released is given by:

$$q_p = \pi_{ab} \cdot I$$

where $\pi_{ab}$ is the Peltier coefficient.

It is considered **reversible** because:
1. Reversing the current direction reverses the hot and cold sides.
2. The same junction can absorb or release heat depending on current polarity.
3. It obeys the **Onsager reciprocal relation** with the Seebeck Effect ($\pi = \alpha T$).
4. It is fundamentally an *entropy transport* effect due to electron flow between materials with different Fermi levels.

*(3 marks — 1 for statement, 1 for explanation, 1 for reversibility logic)*

---

### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**Define the magnetocaloric effect. Name any two magnetocaloric materials and their typical temperature ranges.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Magnetocaloric Effect (MCE)** is the reversible temperature change of a magnetic material when subjected to a change in magnetic field under adiabatic conditions. Mathematically:

$$\Delta T_{ad} = -\int_0^{H} \frac{T}{C_H}\left(\frac{\partial M}{\partial T}\right)_H dH$$

Two MCE materials:

1. **Gadolinium (Gd):** Curie Temperature $T_c \approx 294$ K (21°C) — ideal near room-temperature cooling.
2. **Gadolinium-Silicon-Germanium (Gd₅Si₂Ge₂):** $T_c \approx 276$ K (3°C) — slightly sub-room temperature range.
3. **(Bonus)** Lanthanum-Iron-Silicon (La-Fe-Si) alloys: $T_c \approx 190$ K to 340 K depending on composition.

*(3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for naming materials, 1 for temperature ranges with units)*

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Rule:** *Choose either (a) OR (b) from each question. Each sub-part carries 7 marks.*

---

### QUESTION A — THERMOELECTRIC (14 MARKS)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **heat absorbed at the cold junction** and **heat rejected at the hot junction** of a single-stage Peltier module. Clearly state the assumptions made. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Peltier refrigerator operates between $T_c = 270$ K and $T_h = 300$ K. The thermoelectric properties of the couple are: $\alpha = 210 \times 10^{-6}$ V/K, $R = 0.012 \Omega$, $K = 0.04$ W/K. Find the maximum possible temperature difference $\Delta T_{max}$, the heat absorbed, and the COP when operated at the optimum current. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

Refer to **Section 3.1** of these notes for the complete step-by-step derivation. The final results are:

$$Q_c = \alpha T_c I - \frac{1}{2} I^2 R - K \Delta T$$

$$Q_h = \alpha T_h I + \frac{1}{2} I^2 R - K \Delta T$$

**Assumptions:**
1. Steady-state, one-dimensional heat flow.
2. Thermal and electrical properties constant with temperature.
3. Joule heat splits equally: 50% to cold side, 50% to hot side.
4. No Thomson Effect considered (minor correction).
5. No radiation/convection losses from side surfaces.
6. Perfect thermal contact at junctions.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** Calculate the figure of merit $Z$ from given data.

$$Z = \frac{\alpha^2}{R \cdot K} = \frac{(210 \times 10^{-6})^2}{0.012 \times 0.04}$$

$$Z = \frac{4.41 \times 10^{-8}}{4.8 \times 10^{-4}} = 9.19 \times 10^{-5} \, K^{-1}$$

**Step 2:** Maximum temperature difference (using $T_c = 270$ K):

$$\Delta T_{max} = \frac{1}{2} Z T_c^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 9.19 \times 10^{-5} \times (270)^2$$

$$\Delta T_{max} = \frac{1}{2} \times 9.19 \times 10^{-5} \times 72900$$

$$\boxed{\Delta T_{max} = 3.35 \, K}$$

**Step 3:** Optimum current for given ΔT (operating $\Delta T = 30$ K):

$$I_{opt} = \frac{\alpha \Delta T}{R \left(\sqrt{1 + Z\bar{T}} - 1\right)}$$

Mean temperature: $\bar{T} = (270 + 300)/2 = 285$ K

$$Z\bar{T} = 9.19 \times 10^{-5} \times 285 = 0.02619$$

$$\sqrt{1.02619} = 1.0130$$

$$I_{opt} = \frac{(210 \times 10^{-6})(30)}{0.012 \times (1.0130 - 1)} = \frac{6.3 \times 10^{-3}}{1.56 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$\boxed{I_{opt} \approx 40.4 \, A}$$

**Step 4:** Heat absorbed at cold junction:

$$Q_c = \alpha T_c I - \frac{1}{2} I^2 R - K \Delta T$$

$$Q_c = (210 \times 10^{-6})(270)(40.4) - \frac{1}{2}(40.4)^2(0.012) - (0.04)(30)$$

$$Q_c = 2.289 - 9.793 - 1.2 = -8.704 \, W$$

The negative value indicates that the optimum current derived from $\Delta T_{max}$ formula gives a physically unrealistic scenario because we operated at $\Delta T = 30$ K, very close to the calculated $\Delta T_{max} = 3.35$ K. Therefore **this module cannot achieve 30 K ΔT**; only ~3 K is feasible. Let us recalculate at $\Delta T = 3$ K.

**Recalculate for $\Delta T = 3$ K, $T_c = 297$ K:**

$$I_{opt} = \frac{(210 \times 10^{-6})(3)}{0.012 \times 0.0130} = \frac{6.3 \times 10^{-4}}{1.56 \times 10^{-4}} = 4.04 \, A$$

$$Q_c = (210 \times 10^{-6})(297)(4.04) - \frac{1}{2}(4.04)^2(0.012) - (0.04)(3)$$

$$Q_c = 0.2520 - 0.0979 - 0.12 = 0.034 \, W$$

$$W = \alpha \Delta T \cdot I + I^2 R = (210 \times 10^{-6})(3)(4.04) + (4.04)^2(0.012)$$

$$W = 2.545 \times 10^{-3} + 0.1959 = 0.198 \, W$$

$$COP = \frac{Q_c}{W} = \frac{0.034}{0.198} \approx 0.17$$

**Carnot COP** = $T_c/\Delta T = 297/3 = 99$. So this Peltier module operates at ~0.17% of Carnot efficiency. **Low efficiency is the inherent weakness of thermoelectric refrigeration for low-ΔT, low-power modules.**

---

### QUESTION B — MAGNETIC REFRIGERATION (14 MARKS)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a)** Describe the **Active Magnetic Regenerator (AMR) cycle** with a neat schematic and explain how it produces a larger temperature span than a simple magnetic cycle. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A magnetic refrigeration system uses Gadolinium as the working material. The magnetic field varies between 0 T and 1.5 T. The Curie temperature is 294 K. The adiabatic temperature change of Gd under 1.5 T is approximately 5.5 K. Estimate the **maximum temperature span** the AMR can achieve with a regenerator effectiveness of 0.85, and comment on whether this system can be used for domestic refrigeration. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) AMR Cycle Description (7 marks):**

*(Refer to **Section 2.4** for detailed theoretical description.)*

The AMR cycle consists of four strokes:

| Stroke | Process | Field | Heat Transfer |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Adiabatic Magnetization | H increases (0 → H_max) | Temperature rises |
| 2 | Isofield Cooling | H = H_max | Solid rejects heat to fluid → Hot HX |
| 3 | Adiabatic Demagnetization | H decreases (H_max → 0) | Temperature drops |
| 4 | Isofield Heating | H = 0 | Solid absorbs heat from fluid → Cold HX |

**Why larger temperature span?**

A *simple* magnetic cycle (without regenerator) only achieves ΔT_ad ≈ 5–15 K per material. By introducing a **regenerator** (counter-flow heat exchange with the fluid), the fluid leaving the hot end pre-heats the solid for the next cycle, and the fluid leaving the cold end pre-cools the solid. The **temperature swing of the regenerator fluid** adds to the inherent ΔT_ad of the material, multiplying the effective temperature span by a factor of 3–5. With effectiveness 0.85, the multiplier is approximately:

$$\text{Span Multiplier} = \frac{1}{1 - \varepsilon} \cdot f(\text{flow rate, NTU})$$

For ε = 0.85, the *ideal* span multiplies roughly 4–6×, but real-world multipliers are 2–3×.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** ΔT_ad = 5.5 K, ε = 0.85, T_c ≈ 294 K (Gd's Curie temp)

**Step 1:** Theoretical maximum span (with ideal regenerator):

$$\Delta T_{span, ideal} = \frac{\Delta T_{ad}}{1 - \varepsilon} = \frac{5.5}{1 - 0.85} = \frac{5.5}{0.15} = 36.67 \, K$$

**Step 2:** Real-world span (accounting for practical losses, assume 50% efficiency factor):

$$\Delta T_{span, actual} \approx 0.5 \times 36.67 \approx 18.3 \, K$$

**Step 3:** System capability assessment for domestic refrigeration:

A domestic refrigerator needs $T_c \approx 263$ K ($-10°C$ cold compartment) and rejects heat at $T_h \approx 305$ K. Required span = 42 K.

**Conclusion:** With the calculated span of ~18 K, a **single-stage Gd-based magnetic refrigerator cannot achieve domestic fridge temperatures**. Either:
1. **Cascade two stages** (room-temp stage + cryo stage).
2. **Use a higher-field superconducting magnet** (5 T → ΔT_ad ≈ 14 K).
3. **Use multiple regenerator passes** to enhance effectiveness.
4. **Use Gd-Si-Ge alloys** with higher ΔT_ad.

> **Verdict:** Single-stage magnetic refrigeration is currently suited for **room-temperature niche applications** (cooling electronics, beverage chillers). Commercial domestic use remains under research.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the factor ½ in Joule heating** at the cold junction. ALWAYS write $\frac{1}{2}I^2R$, never $I^2R$.
> 2. **Confusing the three thermoelectric effects** — Peltier (heat ↔ current), Seebeck (temp → EMF), Thomson (single conductor). Examiners explicitly test this distinction.
> 3. **Missing units** in COP, ΔT_max, and figure of merit. Always state K, W/K⁻¹, dimensionless.
> 4. **Not stating assumptions** in derivations — 1–2 marks are deducted if assumptions section is missing.
> 5. **Confusing Curie Temperature** (magnetic ordering transition) with **operating temperature** of the magnetic fridge.
> 6. **In magnetic refrigeration**, students often forget to mention **regenerator effectiveness (ε)** — this is crucial to the AMR concept.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### ✅ Rapid Revision Checklist

**🔹 Thermoelectric Refrigeration (Peltier Effect):**

- [ ] Peltier Effect: Heat absorption/release at junction due to current.
- [ ] Formula: $Q_c = \alpha T_c I - \frac{1}{2}I^2 R - K\Delta T$
- [ ] Formula: $Q_h = \alpha T_h I + \frac{1}{2}I^2 R - K\Delta T$
- [ ] COP: $COP = \frac{Q_c}{W}$, with $W = \alpha \Delta T \cdot I + I^2 R$
- [ ] Max ΔT: $\Delta T_{max} = \frac{1}{2} Z T_c^2$
- [ ] Figure of Merit: $Z = \frac{\alpha^2 \sigma}{k}$
- [ ] Best material: Bi₂Te₃ at room temperature.
- [ ] Joule heating: 50% reaches cold side (factor ½!).
- [ ] Fourier back-conduction: $-K\Delta T$ (parasitic).
- [ ] Applications: CPU cooling, mini-fridges, medical cold chain.
- [ ] Reversibility: Same module can heat or cool.

**🔹 Magnetic Refrigeration (Magnetocaloric Effect):**

- [ ] MCE: Reversible temperature change of magnetic material under field.
- [ ] Formula: $\Delta T_{ad} = -\int \frac{\mu_0 T}{C_H}\left(\frac{\partial M}{\partial T}\right)_H dH$
- [ ] Curie Temperature ($T_c$): Peak MCE temperature.
- [ ] Best material near 294 K: **Gadolinium (Gd)**.
- [ ] Cycle: 4-stroke AMR (magnetize → cool → demagnetize → heat).
- [ ] Regenerator effectiveness (ε) determines span multiplication.
- [ ] Real-world span ≈ 18 K (single stage) vs ~5 K ΔT_ad.
- [ ] No refrigerant, no compressor, no HFC — fully **green**.
- [ ] Applications: Hydrogen liquefaction, cryogenics, future domestic.
- [ ] Carnot COP ceiling: $COP_{Carnot} = T_c/(T_h - T_c)$.

**🔹 Comparative Memory Anchors:**

- [ ] Peltier = *current-driven*; Magnetic = *field-driven*.
- [ ] Peltier = *single junction couple*; Magnetic = *bulk solid bed*.
- [ ] Peltier = *fast response* (ms); Magnetic = *slow* (seconds).
- [ ] Peltier = *commercially mature*; Magnetic = *emerging*.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic — "PICS-MICE":**
> **P**eltier → **I**nduced by **C**urrent → **S**olid-state cooling
> **M**agnetic → **I**nduced by **C**hanging **E**lectromagnetic field
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vapour Compression Systems: Simple Cycle, Representation on T-s and P-h Diagrams, Coefficient of Performance (COP), and Methods to Improve COP (Superheating, Undercooling, Liquid Suction Heat Exchanger, Cascade Systems)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET631 - REFRIGERATION AND AIR CONDITIONING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Vapour Compression Systems: Simple Cycle, Representation on T-s and P-h Diagrams, Coefficient of Performance (COP), and Methods to Improve COP (Superheating, Undercooling, Liquid Suction Heat Exchanger, Cascade Systems)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition — The Vapour Compression Refrigeration Cycle (VCRS)

The **Vapour Compression Refrigeration System (VCRS)** is a closed thermodynamic cycle that uses a working fluid (refrigerant) undergoing phase change to transfer heat from a low-temperature reservoir (cold space) to a high-temperature reservoir (atmosphere). Energy is supplied as mechanical work to the compressor, making the cycle inherently a *work-operated* refrigeration system, distinct from absorption systems (heat-operated).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *A vapour compression refrigeration system is a cyclic process in which a refrigerant circulates through four primary components — compressor, condenser, expansion device, and evaporator — absorbing heat at low temperature (evaporator) and rejecting heat at high temperature (condenser), with the refrigerant undergoing phase change from liquid to vapour and back.*

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Refrigerant Sweat" Analogy

Imagine you are sweating on a hot day. Your body pumps sweat (liquid) to the skin surface. As sweat evaporates, it absorbs heat from your body, cooling you down. The vapour rises away. Now, in nature, that vapour just drifts away. 

In a **VCRS**, we *capture* that vapour before it escapes, mechanically **squeeze** it (compress), and turn it back into a hot liquid by cooling it. We then *recycle* this hot liquid back to absorb more heat. The mechanical squeezing requires a **compressor (electricity)**, and the cooling of the squeezed vapour happens in a **condenser (rejects heat outdoors)**. The expansion back to a cold liquid happens through a **throttle/expansion valve**, and the heat-absorbing phase happens in the **evaporator (inside the room/fridge)**.

**Real-world mapping:** Your home refrigerator, split AC, deep freezer, and cold storage warehouses — all are VCRS applications.

---

## 1.3 The Four Cardinal State Points of a Simple VCRS

| State | Location | Refrigerant Condition | Process Completed |
|-------|----------|----------------------|-------------------|
| **1** | Compressor Inlet | Saturated (or slightly superheated) vapour | Suction from evaporator |
| **2** | Compressor Outlet / Condenser Inlet | Superheated vapour (high P, high T) | Compression (isentropic ideally) |
| **3** | Condenser Outlet / Expansion Valve Inlet | Saturated (or subcooled) liquid | Condensation + possible subcooling |
| **4** | Evaporator Inlet / Compressor Inlet (post-valve) | Liquid-Vapour mixture (low P, low T) | Throttling (isenthalpic) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Favourite:** Always label these four points in sequence on both T-s and P-h charts. A common valuation pitfall is reversing the order or confusing state 2 (compressor discharge) with state 3 (condenser exit).

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Standard COP for domestic refrigerator:** 1.5 to 3.0
- **Standard COP for window/split AC:** 2.5 to 4.0
- **Common refrigerants (modern):** R-134a, R-410A, R-32, R-1234yf
- **Legacy refrigerants (deprecated):** R-12, R-22 (high GWP/ODP)
- **1 Ton of Refrigeration (TR)** = **3.517 kW** = heat removal rate equivalent to melting 1 ton (1000 kg) of ice in 24 hours from 0 °C.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Component-Wise Thermodynamic Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Compressor (State 1 → State 2)
- **Function:** Raises refrigerant pressure from evaporator pressure (P_evap) to condenser pressure (P_cond).
- **Work Input:** The compressor consumes **specific work** $w_c = h_2 - h_1$ (kJ/kg).
- **Ideal assumption:** **Isentropic compression** (s_1 = s_2). Real compressors use **isentropic efficiency** $\eta_c$.
- **Isentropic efficiency formula:**

$$\eta_c = \frac{h_{2s} - h_1}{h_2 - h_1}$$

- **Why isentropic?** KTU examiner expects this idealization as the baseline for COP calculation unless explicitly stated otherwise.

### 2.1.2 Condenser (State 2 → State 3)
- **Function:** Rejects heat from superheated vapour until it fully condenses to (subcooled) liquid.
- **Heat Rejected per kg:** $q_{\text{cond}} = h_2 - h_3$ (kJ/kg).
- **Process type:** Constant pressure heat rejection (with desuperheating + condensation + subcooling zones).

### 2.1.3 Expansion Valve / Throttle (State 3 → State 4)
- **Function:** Sudden pressure drop from P_cond to P_evap.
- **Process type:** **Isenthalpic (throttling)** — $h_3 = h_4$.
- **No work, no heat transfer:** $w = 0$, $q = 0$. Only enthalpy is conserved.
- **Refrigerant partially flashes to vapour** (quality $x_4$ at state 4).

### 2.1.4 Evaporator (State 4 → State 1)
- **Function:** Absorbs heat from the cold space, fully evaporating the liquid.
- **Refrigeration Effect (RE):** $RE = h_1 - h_4$ (kJ/kg).
- **Process type:** Constant pressure heat absorption.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master this table** — every KTU problem in Module 2 is built on these equations.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Boundary/Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-------|----------------|
| 1 | Refrigeration Effect (RE) | $h_1 - h_4$ | kJ/kg | Per kg of refrigerant circulated |
| 2 | Compressor Work (specific) | $h_2 - h_1$ | kJ/kg | Isentropic ideal case |
| 3 | Heat Rejected in Condenser | $h_2 - h_3$ | kJ/kg | $= RE + w_c$ (energy balance) |
| 4 | Throttle (Expansion) Process | $h_3 = h_4$ | kJ/kg | Isenthalpic, no work/heat |
| 5 | COP of VCRS (ideal) | $\dfrac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}$ | dimensionless | Core KTU formula |
| 6 | Mass Flow Rate of Refrigerant | $\dot{m} = \dfrac{Q_e}{h_1 - h_4}$ | kg/s | Where $Q_e$ = cooling load (kW) |
| 7 | Compressor Power Input | $W_c = \dot{m}(h_2 - h_1)$ | kW | For selecting motor |
| 8 | COP (Carnot reference) | $\dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$ | dimensionless | Upper limit (in Kelvin) |
| 9 | COP with Superheating (ideal) | $\dfrac{h_1 - h_4'}{h_2' - h_1}$ | dimensionless | State 1 shifted to superheat |
| 10 | COP with Subcooling | $\dfrac{h_1 - h_4'}{h_2 - h_1}$ | dimensionless | State 3 shifted cooler |
| 11 | LSHX Effectiveness (Energy Balance) | $h_3 + h_1 = h_3' + h_1'$ | kJ/kg | Heat exchange equality |
| 12 | Cascade COP | $\dfrac{(h_1 - h_4)(h_5 - h_8)}{(h_2 - h_1)(h_6 - h_5) + (h_1 - h_4)(h_7 - h_6)}$ | dimensionless | Combined system |
| 13 | 1 Ton of Refrigeration | $3.517$ kW | kW | Conversion constant |

> **Sanity rule:** Always verify $q_{\text{cond}} = RE + w_c$ (First Law energy balance across the cycle).

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pharmaceutical cold chain:** VCRS maintains 2–8 °C vaccine storage; improving COP directly cuts electricity costs.
- **Data centres:** Cooling loads of MW scale use advanced VCRS; a COP improvement from 3.0 to 3.5 can save crores annually.
- **Food processing:** Blast freezers use cascade VCRS for temperatures down to -80 °C.
- **HVAC in skyscrapers:** Chilled water plants use large VCRS chillers (centrifugal compressors) where every 0.1 COP improvement matters.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Application Question Favourite:** "How does superheating affect the COP? Justify with T-s diagram." — Be ready to draw, label, and explain *both* the COP gain and the compressor work penalty.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of COP for a Simple Vapour Compression Cycle

We apply the **First Law of Thermodynamics** to each component, then sum around the cycle.

### Step 1: Compressor (1 → 2) — Energy Balance
Energy input to refrigerant = enthalpy rise (steady-flow, adiabatic, negligible KE/PE):

$$w_c = h_2 - h_1$$

This is the **specific compressor work** (kJ per kg of refrigerant).

### Step 2: Condenser (2 → 3) — Heat Rejection
Heat rejected by refrigerant to surroundings (constant pressure, no work):

$$q_{\text{cond}} = h_2 - h_3$$

### Step 3: Expansion Valve (3 → 4) — Throttling
Throttling is **isenthalpic** — no work, no heat, no change in enthalpy:

$$h_4 = h_3$$

**Quality at state 4** is found from the saturated mixture equation:

$$h_4 = h_f + x_4 \cdot h_{fg}$$

Rearranging for quality:

$$x_4 = \frac{h_4 - h_f}{h_{fg}}$$

### Step 4: Evaporator (4 → 1) — Heat Absorption
The refrigeration effect is the heat absorbed per kg of refrigerant:

$$RE = h_1 - h_4$$

### Step 5: Cycle Energy Balance
Summing energy around the cycle (First Law for a cyclic device, net Q = net W):

$$q_{\text{cond}} = RE + w_c$$

Verification:

$$(h_2 - h_3) = (h_1 - h_4) + (h_2 - h_1)$$

### Step 6: COP Definition
The Coefficient of Performance is the ratio of desired effect (refrigeration) to work input:

$$COP_{VCRS} = \frac{\text{Desired Output}}{\text{Required Input}} = \frac{RE}{w_c}$$

Substituting from Steps 1 and 4:

$$\boxed{COP_{VCRS} = \frac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **single most important equation** in Module 2. Memorize, derive, and understand its physical meaning.

### Step 7: Comparison with Carnot COP
The Carnot (reversible) COP between two reservoirs at $T_L$ and $T_H$ (absolute temperatures):

$$COP_{Carnot} = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$$

**KTU Expectation:** Always state that $COP_{VCRS} < COP_{Carnot}$ for the same temperature limits, because real VCRS has irreversibilities (throttling) and non-isothermal heat transfer in the evaporator/condenser.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Effect of Superheating on COP

Let state 1' be the superheated state (instead of saturated vapour at state 1).

- **New RE:** $RE' = h_{1'} - h_4$  (increases, because $h_{1'} > h_1$)
- **New compressor work:** $w_c' = h_{2'} - h_{1'}$  (also increases, because we compress a higher-temperature gas)

**Decision rule:**

$$\text{If } \frac{h_{1'} - h_4}{h_{2'} - h_{1'}} > \frac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1} \Rightarrow \text{COP increases}$$

**Practical KTU Result:** *Useful superheating* (within the compression range where the work penalty is small) **increases COP**, while *excessive superheating* (entering the high-temperature gas region) **decreases COP**. KTU typically tests the latter with a warning callout.

---

## 3.3 Derivation — Effect of Subcooling on COP

Let state 3' be subcooled below the saturation temperature at condenser pressure.

- **New throttling:** $h_4' = h_3'$  (since throttling is isenthalpic)
- **New RE:** $RE' = h_1 - h_4' = h_1 - h_3'$  (increases because $h_3' < h_3$)
- **Compressor work:** $w_c$ unchanged (state 1 and 2 unaffected)

**Result:**

$$COP_{subcooled} = \frac{h_1 - h_3'}{h_2 - h_1} > COP_{simple} = \frac{h_1 - h_3}{h_2 - h_1}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Subcooling ALWAYS increases COP** (as long as the same refrigerant mass flow is maintained). This is a KTU board-exam "safe answer" topic.

---

## 3.4 Derivation — Liquid-Suction Heat Exchanger (LSHX) Energy Balance

In an LSHX, the warm liquid leaving the condenser (state 3) gives up heat to the cool vapour leaving the evaporator (state 1). After heat exchange:
- Liquid cools further from state 3 → state 3' (additional subcooling)
- Vapour warms from state 1 → state 1' (superheating)
- **No heat loss to surroundings** (assumed ideal): heat lost by vapour = heat gained by liquid

**Energy balance per kg of refrigerant:**

$$h_3 - h_{3'} = h_{1'} - h_1$$

Rearranged:

$$\boxed{h_3 + h_1 = h_{3'} + h_{1'}}$$

**Effect on COP:**

$$COP_{LSHX} = \frac{h_{1'} - h_{3'}}{h_{2} - h_{1'}}$$

- Numerator (RE) **increases** (h₁' up, h₃' down).
- Denominator (work) **increases** (h₁' up means h₂ also rises since compression is from a higher enthalpy point).

**Net effect:** Depends on the refrigerant. For **R-134a** (and most modern refrigerants with positive $dh/ds$ slope in the superheat region), LSHX **increases COP**. KTU loves this caveat.

---

## 3.5 Derivation — Cascade Refrigeration System COP

A cascade system uses **two separate VCRS cycles** coupled via a **heat exchanger** (the cascade condenser/evaporator).

- **Low-Temperature Cycle (LTC):** Cools the cold space; rejects heat to the cascade HX.
- **High-Temperature Cycle (HTC):** Rejects heat from the cascade HX to the environment.

**Energy balance at cascade heat exchanger:**

$$\dot{m}_L (h_2 - h_3) = \dot{m}_H (h_6 - h_5)$$

where subscript $L$ = LTC refrigerant, $H$ = HTC refrigerant.

**Overall COP:**

$$\boxed{COP_{cascade} = \frac{(h_1 - h_4)}{(h_2 - h_1) + \frac{(h_1 - h_4)(h_7 - h_6)}{(h_5 - h_8)}}}$$

**Why cascade?** When the temperature ratio $T_H/T_L$ is too large (e.g., -80 °C to +40 °C), a single VCRS has very low COP. Splitting into two cycles with intermediate temperature $T_{int}$ allows each cycle to operate at a more favorable compression ratio.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Forgetting that cascade COP is **NOT** simply $COP_L \cdot COP_H$. It must be derived from first principles as above.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of COP Improvement Methods

| Method | Effect on RE | Effect on Work | Net COP Effect | Typical Application |
|--------|-------------|---------------|----------------|---------------------|
| Useful Superheating | ↑ Increases | ↑ Slightly increases | ↑ Slight gain (if controlled) | Domestic fridges (R-134a) |
| Subcooling | ↑ Increases | No change | ↑ **Always increases** | Chillers, AC plants |
| LSHX | ↑ Increases | ↑ Slightly increases | ↑ Increases (for R-134a) | Modern ACs, heat pumps |
| Cascade Splitting | N/A (cycle design) | Reduces per-cycle | ↑ **Significant** for very low T | Cryogenics, -40 °C and below |
| Multi-staging + Intercooling | ↑ Increases | ↓ Reduces | ↑ Significant | Large industrial chillers |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Similar]`
**Define the Coefficient of Performance (COP) of a vapour compression refrigeration system. Why is it always greater than 1?**

**Model Answer:**

The COP of a VCRS is defined as the ratio of the desired refrigeration effect to the work input to the compressor:

$$COP = \frac{RE}{w_c} = \frac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}$$

COP is always greater than 1 because the desired output (heat extracted from the cold space per unit time) is always more than the work input, since the refrigerant also rejects heat absorbed from the environment in addition to the work input. The energy balance is:

$$Q_{\text{cond}} = Q_e + W_c$$

Hence $Q_e = Q_{\text{cond}} - W_c$, but the ratio $Q_e/W_c$ can be > 1 if the condenser rejects substantially more heat than the work input.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Module 2]`
**State the function of the expansion valve in a VCRS. Why is the throttling process called isenthalpic?**

**Model Answer:**

The expansion valve (also called throttle valve or capillary tube) reduces the pressure of the refrigerant from condenser pressure to evaporator pressure. This sudden pressure drop also causes a temperature drop, partially flashing the liquid refrigerant into a liquid-vapour mixture, which then enters the evaporator to absorb heat.

The throttling process is called **isenthalpic** because it is a steady-flow process with:
- No work transfer ($w = 0$)
- No heat transfer ($q = 0$)
- Negligible changes in kinetic and potential energy

By the Steady Flow Energy Equation (SFEE):

$$h_3 = h_4$$

The enthalpy remains constant, hence "isenthalpic."

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do NOT forget to convert units** (kJ/kg for enthalpy, °C to K for Carnot COP). Marks deducted for unit errors.
> 2. **Label ALL four state points (1, 2, 3, 4)** clearly on every P-h or T-s diagram. Missing labels = -2 marks.
> 3. **Show the throttling line as vertical on P-h diagram** (not slanted). This is a top marking pet peeve.
> 4. **Do not confuse useful vs. excessive superheating** in COP comparison questions.
> 5. **For cascade systems**, write both cycles' state points separately (1-2-3-4 for LTC, 5-6-7-8 for HTC). Mixing them loses 3-4 marks.
> 6. **Always state assumptions** (isentropic compression, negligible KE/PE, no heat loss in LSHX).

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2, Q-choice A]`

**a)** With the help of a neat P-h and T-s diagram, explain the working of a simple vapour compression refrigeration system. State the assumptions made. (7 marks)

**b)** A refrigerant R-134a enters the compressor of a VCRS as saturated vapour at -10 °C and leaves at 0.8 MPa with an enthalpy of 273.66 kJ/kg. The enthalpy at the condenser exit is 65.50 kJ/kg. The refrigerant leaves the expansion valve with an enthalpy of 65.50 kJ/kg. Calculate:
   (i) Refrigeration Effect (RE)
   (ii) Compressor work
   (iii) COP
   (iv) Mass flow rate of refrigerant for 1 TR cooling load
   (7 marks)

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Assumptions of Simple VCRS:**
1. Compression is **isentropic** ($s_1 = s_2$).
2. Throttling is **isenthalpic** ($h_3 = h_4$).
3. Condenser and evaporator operate at **constant pressure**.
4. Refrigerant leaves the evaporator as **saturated vapour** and condenser as **saturated liquid** (baseline case).
5. No heat loss in connecting pipes.
6. Negligible kinetic and potential energy changes.

**Working (refer to P-h and T-s diagrams in Section 4):**
- **Process 1→2 (Compressor):** Low-pressure saturated vapour compressed isentropically to high-pressure superheated vapour.
- **Process 2→3 (Condenser):** High-pressure superheated vapour is condensed to saturated liquid, rejecting heat $q_{\text{cond}}$ to the surroundings.
- **Process 3→4 (Expansion Valve):** High-pressure liquid throttled to low-pressure two-phase mixture, $h_3 = h_4$.
- **Process 4→1 (Evaporator):** Liquid-vapour mixture absorbs heat $RE = h_1 - h_4$ and fully evaporates back to saturated vapour.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

Given:
- $T_1 = -10°C$, saturated vapour → $h_1 = h_g$ at -10°C = **241.30 kJ/kg** (from R-134a tables)
- $h_2 = 273.66$ kJ/kg
- $h_3 = 65.50$ kJ/kg
- $h_4 = h_3 = 65.50$ kJ/kg (throttling)
- Cooling load = 1 TR = **3.517 kW**

**Step-by-step:**

**(i) Refrigeration Effect:**

$$RE = h_1 - h_4 = 241.30 - 65.50 = 175.80 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**(ii) Compressor Work:**

$$w_c = h_2 - h_1 = 273.66 - 241.30 = 32.36 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**(iii) COP:**

$$COP = \frac{RE}{w_c} = \frac{175.80}{32.36} = 5.43$$

**(iv) Mass Flow Rate:**

$$\dot{m} = \frac{Q_e}{RE} = \frac{3.517 \text{ kW}}{175.80 \text{ kJ/kg}} = 0.0200 \text{ kg/s} = 72.0 \text{ kg/hr}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2, Q-choice B]`

**a)** Explain the effect of (i) subcooling and (ii) superheating on the COP of a VCRS. Use P-h diagrams in your explanation. (7 marks)

**b)** Describe the working of a cascade refrigeration system. Why is it preferred over a single VCRS for very low temperature applications? (7 marks)

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**(i) Effect of Subcooling:**

When the liquid refrigerant is cooled below the saturation temperature at the condenser pressure (state 3 → state 3'), the throttling process now occurs from a lower enthalpy point:

$$h_4' = h_3' < h_4 = h_3$$

The new refrigeration effect is:

$$RE' = h_1 - h_4' > RE = h_1 - h_4$$

The compressor work ($h_2 - h_1$) remains **unchanged** because states 1 and 2 are unaffected.

$$\boxed{COP_{\text{subcooled}} = \frac{h_1 - h_3'}{h_2 - h_1} > COP_{\text{simple}} = \frac{h_1 - h_3}{h_2 - h_1}}$$

**Subcooling always increases COP.** [2 marks for this conclusion]

**(ii) Effect of Superheating:**

When the suction vapour is superheated (state 1 → state 1'), the refrigeration effect and compressor work **both increase**:

$$RE' = h_{1'} - h_4 > RE = h_1 - h_4$$

$$w_c' = h_{2'} - h_{1'} > w_c = h_2 - h_1$$

**Useful superheating** (within moderate range) increases COP because the gain in RE outweighs the work penalty. **Excessive superheating** (high temperature gas) reduces COP. The result depends on the refrigerant and the amount of superheat. [3 marks for the nuanced analysis + P-h diagram]

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

A **cascade refrigeration system** uses two separate VCRS coupled by a heat exchanger (cascade condenser-evaporator):

- **Low-Temperature Cycle (LTC):** Uses a low-boiling refrigerant to achieve the desired very low temperature. Rejects heat to the cascade HX.
- **High-Temperature Cycle (HTC):** Uses a moderate-pressure refrigerant to reject the heat from the cascade HX to the atmosphere.

**Why preferred for very low temperatures?**
1. **Compression ratio limit:** Single VCRS at -80 °C would need impractically high compression ratio, leading to low volumetric efficiency and very low COP.
2. **Better refrigerant selection:** Each cycle can use a refrigerant suited to its temperature range.
3. **Improved COP:** Splitting the temperature lift into two stages reduces the overall irreversibility.
4. **Safety and maintainability:** Two smaller systems are easier to maintain than one oversized single-stage system.

[3 marks for diagram and 2 marks each for the four points above]

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Pin this to your wall!**

- ✅ **Simple VCRS** has 4 processes: isentropic compression, constant-pressure condensation, isenthalpic throttling, constant-pressure evaporation.
- ✅ **State points:** 1 = compressor inlet (sat. vap), 2 = compressor outlet (sup. vap), 3 = condenser outlet (sat. liq), 4 = evaporator inlet (two-phase).
- ✅ **Core COP formula:** $COP = \dfrac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}$.
- ✅ **Energy balance:** $q_{\text{cond}} = RE + w_c$ (verify in every numerical).
- ✅ **Throttling:** Always $h_3 = h_4$, vertical line on P-h chart.
- ✅ **Subcooling** → **always increases COP** (safe answer in KTU).
- ✅ **Useful superheating** → increases COP; **excessive superheating** → decreases COP.
- ✅ **LSHX energy balance:** $h_3 + h_1 = h_{3'} + h_{1'}$ (no heat loss assumption).
- ✅ **LSHX effect on COP:** For R-134a, **COP increases**; for some older refrigerants, COP may decrease.
- ✅ **Cascade system:** Two coupled VCRS, with **8 state points** total (1-2-3-4 LTC + 5-6-7-8 HTC).
- ✅ **Cascade preferred** when compression ratio is too high for a single cycle (very low T applications).
- ✅ **1 TR** = **3.517 kW** (must-know conversion for numericals).
- ✅ **Carnot COP** = $T_L/(T_H - T_L)$ — always use **absolute temperatures (K)**.
- ✅ **Assumptions list** (isentropic compression, isenthalpic throttling, no KE/PE, no pipe heat loss) — must be written in every descriptive answer.
- ✅ **Refrigerant notation:** Use consistent state numbering in P-h and T-s diagrams; do not mix cycles in cascade.

> **Final KTU Tip:** When asked "improve COP," always discuss **both** the methodology and the trade-off. A one-sided answer is incomplete.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vapour Absorption Systems: Ammonia-Water System, Lithium Bromide-Water Systems, Electrolux Systems, Comparison with Vapour Compression Systems and Drawbacks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPET631 - REFRIGERATION AND AIR CONDITIONING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Vapour Absorption Systems: Ammonia-Water System, Lithium Bromide-Water Systems, Electrolux Systems, Comparison with Vapour Compression Systems and Drawbacks # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition — Vapour Absorption Refrigeration System (VARS) A **Vapour Absorption Refrigeration System (VARS)** is a heat-operated refrigeration cycle in which the mechanical work of compression in a conventional Vapour Compression Refrigeration System (VCRS) is replaced by a **thermal compression process** consisting of an *absorber* and a *generator* (also called the "reactor" or "still"). The refrigerant vapour leaving the evaporator is absorbed by a suitable liquid called the **absorbent (or solvent)** to form a *rich solution*; this solution is then pumped to a higher pressure, and the refrigerant is boiled off from it in the generator using an external heat source (such as steam, flue gas, solar energy, or waste heat). The high-pressure refrigerant vapour is condensed in the condenser, expanded through the expansion valve, and re-evaporated in the evaporator to produce the cooling effect. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The two working pairs most frequently examined for the KTU Board are **(i) Ammonia–Water (NH₃–H₂O)**, where ammonia is the refrigerant and water is the absorbent, and **(ii) Lithium-Bromide–Water (LiBr–H₂O)**, where water is the refrigerant and LiBr is the absorbent. > [!NOTE] > **Standard Reference Temperatures (KTU Convention):** > - $T_E$ — Evaporator temperature (K) > - $T_C$ — Condenser temperature (K) > - $T_G$ — Generator temperature (K) > - $T_A$ — Absorber temperature (K) > - Standard atmospheric values: $T_C = 308\,\text{K}\,(35^\circ\text{C})$, $T_E = 263\,\text{K}\,(-10^\circ\text{C})$, $T_G = 388\,\text{K}\,(115^\circ\text{C})$, $T_A = 298\,\text{K}\,(25^\circ\text{C})$. ## 1.2 Working-Fluid Pairs — The Concept of "Refrigerant + Absorbent" | Pair | Refrigerant | Absorbent | Typical Use | |------|-------------|-----------|-------------| | **NH₃ – H₂O** | Ammonia (NH₃) | Water (H₂O) | Industrial refrigeration (below 0 °C) | | **LiBr – H₂O** | Water (H₂O) | Lithium Bromide (LiBr) | A/C and heat pumps (above 0 °C) | | **Electrolux (3-fluid)** | Ammonia (NH₃) | Water (H₂O) | Domestic refrigerators (historical) | ## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Sponge-and-Bucket Model" Imagine you have a wet sponge (the **absorbent + refrigerant** mixture) dripping into a bucket. The sponge soaks up the spilled water — this is the **absorber**. Now imagine placing that wet sponge over a fire. Heat drives the water out of the sponge as steam — this is the **generator**. The steam is collected, cooled, and reused. In a refrigerator, the "sponge" is the absorbent (water or LiBr) and the "water" is the refrigerant (NH₃ or H₂O). The fire is the **external heat input** ($Q_G$). > [!TIP] > **Key insight:** The whole system runs on **heat** — a tiny pump handles the liquid. That is why absorption systems are popular where waste heat or solar energy is available. ## 1.4 Why "Absorption" instead of "Compression"? - A mechanical compressor handles **vapour** and consumes **shaft work** ($W_{in}$). - An absorber + pump + generator handle **liquid** and consume **heat** ($Q_G$). - Since pumping a liquid requires $\approx 1/100^{\text{th}}$ of the work of compressing a vapour, the input energy is mostly **thermal**. --- # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Six Essential Components of a VARS 1. **Evaporator** — refrigerant evaporates at low pressure $P_E$, producing refrigeration effect $Q_E$. 2. **Absorber** — refrigerant vapour mixes with the weak solution to form a strong solution; rejects heat $Q_A$. 3. **Pump** — raises pressure of strong solution from $P_E$ to $P_G$ (work $W_p$). 4. **Generator (Analyzer)** — heat $Q_G$ drives refrigerant out of the strong solution. 5. **Condenser** — high-pressure refrigerant condenses at $P_C$, rejecting heat $Q_C$. 6. **Expansion Valve** — throttles liquid refrigerant from $P_C$ to $P_E$ (no work, no heat). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rectifier & Analyzer (NH₃–H₂O only):** Because water also vaporises in the generator, an **analyzer** (partial separation) and a **rectifier** (final purification) are needed to deliver nearly pure NH₃ to the condenser. In **LiBr–H₂O**, water is the refrigerant and LiBr is non-volatile — so **no rectifier is required**. ## 2.2 Step-by-Step Working Logic - **Step 1 — Evaporation:** Liquid NH₃ at low $P_E$ absorbs heat $Q_E$ from the cold space and evaporates. - **Step 2 — Absorption:** NH₃ vapour is absorbed by weak NH₃–H₂O solution in the absorber, releasing heat $Q_A$. The mixture becomes the **strong (rich) solution**. - **Step 3 — Pumping:** A small liquid pump raises the strong solution from $P_E$ to $P_G$. Pump work $W_p = \dot{m}_s v_s (P_G - P_E)$. - **Step 4 — Generation:** External heat $Q_G$ (steam / waste heat) at the generator vaporises NH₃ from the strong solution. NH₃ vapour (with some water) rises; weak solution returns to the absorber via the expansion valve. - **Step 5 — Rectification:** Water vapour is condensed in the rectifier/separator and returned as reflux. Nearly pure NH₃ passes to the condenser. - **Step 6 — Condensation:** NH₃ condenses at $P_C$ by rejecting $Q_C$. - **Step 7 — Expansion:** Liquid NH₃ throttles to $P_E$ and returns to the evaporator. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Quantity | Formula | Conditions / Notes | |---|----------|---------|-------------------| | 1 | **Refrigeration Effect** | $Q_E = \dot{m}_r (h_1 - h_4)$ | per kg of refrigerant circulated | | 2 | **Heat Input at Generator** | $Q_G = \dot{m}_r h_2 + \dot{m}_s h_7 - \dot{m}_s h_6$ | energy balance at generator | | 3 | **Heat Rejected at Absorber** | $Q_A = \dot{m}_s h_6 - \dot{m}_r h_1 - \dot{m}_s h_5$ | energy balance at absorber | | 4 | **Heat Rejected at Condenser** | $Q_C = \dot{m}_r (h_2 - h_3)$ | per kg of refrigerant | | 5 | **Pump Work** | $W_p = \dot{m}_s v_s (P_G - P_E)$ | usually negligible ($\approx$ 1 % of $Q_E$) | | 6 | **Actual COP of VARS** | $\text{COP} = \dfrac{Q_E}{Q_G + W_p}$ | ≈ $Q_E/Q_G$ since $W_p \ll Q_G$ | | 7 | **Maximum (Reversible) COP** | $\text{COP}_{\max} = \dfrac{T_E}{T_G - T_E}\cdot\dfrac{T_G - T_C}{T_G}$ | combined Carnot cycles | | 8 | **Practical COP** | $\text{COP}_{\text{actual}} = 0.4$ to $0.6 \times \text{COP}_{\max}$ | KTU empirical factor | | 9 | **Heat rejected overall** | $Q_C + Q_A = Q_E + Q_G + W_p$ | First-law balance | | 10 | **Solution-circulation ratio** | $f = \dfrac{\dot{m}_s}{\dot{m}_r} = \dfrac{x_r - x_w}{x_s - x_w}$ | by mass-balance of NH₃ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Trap:** In NH₃–H₂O the **absorbent is water (H₂O)**, but in LiBr–H₂O the **absorbent is LiBr** (a *salt*) and the **refrigerant is water**. Confusing the two roles is the single most common KTU Board mistake. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Cogeneration plants** use the VARS to recover waste-heat from the steam turbine exhaust. - **Solar refrigeration** for off-grid cold storage in Kerala's rural regions is increasingly using NH₃–H₂O VARS. - **Hospital and laboratory chillers** favour LiBr–H₂O because it uses water as the refrigerant — non-toxic, non-flammable, and **Ozone Depletion Potential (ODP) = 0**. - **Marine and food-processing industries** prefer NH₃–H₂O because it can reach temperatures below 0 °C easily. --- # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Maximum (Reversible) COP of an Ideal VARS The ideal VARS is modelled as a **combination of two reversed Carnot cycles** working between the same three temperature reservoirs ($T_G$, $T_C$, $T_E$): - **Cycle A** — Refrigeration loop: $T_G$ (heat source) → $T_C$ (condenser) → $T_E$ (evaporator) - **Cycle B** — Heat-pump loop: $T_G$ (generator) → $T_A$ (absorber, equal to $T_E$ in reversible case) → $T_C$ (condenser) ### Derivation $$\text{COP}_{\text{overall}} \;=\; \frac{Q_E}{Q_G}$$ **Step 1 — Energy balance of the heat-engine part (Cycle B, generator ↔ condenser):** $$Q_G = W_{\text{thermal}} + Q_{C,\text{part}}$$ By the Carnot relation for the heat-engine between $T_G$ and $T_C$: $$\frac{W_{\text{thermal}}}{Q_G} \;=\; 1 - \frac{T_C}{T_G}$$ **Step 2 — COP of the refrigeration part (Cycle A, between $T_E$ and $T_G$):** $$\text{COP}_{\text{ref}} \;=\; \frac{Q_E}{W_{\text{thermal}}} \;=\; \frac{T_E}{T_G - T_E}$$ **Step 3 — Multiply the two results:** $$\text{COP}_{\max} \;=\; \frac{Q_E}{Q_G} \;=\; \frac{Q_E}{W_{\text{thermal}}}\cdot\frac{W_{\text{thermal}}}{Q_G}$$ $$\text{COP}_{\max} \;=\; \frac{T_E}{T_G - T_E} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{T_C}{T_G}\right)$$ $$\text{COP}_{\max} \;=\; \frac{T_E}{T_G - T_E} \cdot \frac{T_G - T_C}{T_G}$$ This is the **fundamental maximum COP equation** for an ideal VARS and is a *Killer Question* in KTU Board exams. ## 3.2 Derivation of the Actual COP of a Practical VARS (NH₃–H₂O) Let the **mass flow rate of pure NH₃** circulating through the condenser–evaporator loop be $\dot{m}_r$ (kg/s). Let the **mass flow rate of the strong solution** leaving the absorber be $\dot{m}_s$ (kg/s). Let the **concentrations** (mass fraction of NH₃) be: - $x_s$ — strong solution leaving the absorber - $x_w$ — weak solution leaving the generator - $x_r$ — refrigerant (≈ 1.0 for NH₃ at condenser inlet) **Step 1 — Mass balance on NH₃ in the absorber:** $$\dot{m}_r \;=\; \dot{m}_s\,(x_s - x_w)$$ **Step 2 — Mass flow of weak solution returning to absorber:** $$\dot{m}_w \;=\; \dot{m}_s - \dot{m}_r$$ **Step 3 — Solution-circulation ratio:** $$f \;=\; \frac{\dot{m}_s}{\dot{m}_r} \;=\; \frac{1}{x_s - x_w}$$ **Step 4 — Energy balance at the generator (steady state):** $$Q_G \;=\; \dot{m}_r h_{2} + \dot{m}_s h_{7} - \dot{m}_s h_{6}$$ where $h_2$ = enthalpy of NH₃ vapour leaving the generator, $h_6$ = enthalpy of strong solution entering the generator, $h_7$ = enthalpy of weak solution leaving the generator. **Step 5 — Energy balance at the absorber:** $$Q_A \;=\; \dot{m}_s h_6 - \dot{m}_r h_1 - \dot{m}_s h_5$$ where $h_1$ = enthalpy of refrigerant vapour leaving the evaporator, $h_5$ = enthalpy of weak solution entering the absorber. **Step 6 — Refrigeration effect:** $$Q_E \;=\; \dot{m}_r (h_1 - h_4)$$ **Step 7 — Actual COP:** $$\text{COP} \;=\; \frac{Q_E}{Q_G + W_p} \;\approx\; \frac{\dot{m}_r (h_1 - h_4)}{Q_G}$$ (neglecting $W_p$ since $W_p \approx 0.005\,Q_E$ for typical pressures). ## 3.3 Sample KTU Numerical **Problem:** A NH₃–H₂O VARS operates with $T_E = 263\,\text{K}$, $T_C = 308\,\text{K}$, $T_G = 388\,\text{K}$. Find the maximum COP and compare it with a VCRS operating between $T_E$ and $T_C$. **Step 1 — Maximum COP of VARS:** $$\text{COP}_{\max} \;=\; \frac{T_E}{T_G - T_E}\cdot\frac{T_G - T_C}{T_G}$$ $$\text{COP}_{\max} \;=\; \frac{263}{388 - 263}\cdot\frac{388 - 308}{388}$$ $$\text{COP}_{\max} \;=\; \frac{263}{125}\cdot\frac{80}{388}$$ $$\text{COP}_{\max} \;=\; 2.104 \times 0.2062 \;=\; 0.4339$$ **Step 2 — COP of VCRS (Carnot):** $$\text{COP}_{\text{VCRS}} \;=\; \frac{T_E}{T_C - T_E} \;=\; \frac{263}{308 - 263} \;=\; \frac{263}{45} \;=\; 5.844$$ **Step 3 — Actual COP of VARS (assuming 50 % of max):** $$\text{COP}_{\text{actual}} \;=\; 0.5 \times 0.4339 \;=\; 0.2170$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key inference:** A VARS has a **theoretical COP far lower** than a VCRS. Its advantage lies **NOT in efficiency** but in the use of **cheap / waste heat** instead of **costly electrical work**. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — At a Glance | Subsystem | Energy In | Energy Out | Working Fluid | |-----------|-----------|------------|---------------| | Evaporator | $Q_E$ from cold space | Refrigerant vapour | Pure NH₃ / H₂O | | Absorber | Refrigerant vapour + weak solution | Strong solution + $Q_A$ | NH₃ + H₂O mixture | | Pump | $W_p$ (shaft) | High-pressure liquid | Strong solution | | Generator | $Q_G$ (heat) | NH₃ vapour + weak solution | NH₃ + H₂O mixture | | Condenser | Refrigerant vapour | Liquid + $Q_C$ | Pure NH₃ / H₂O | | Expansion valve | High-pressure liquid | Two-phase mixture | Pure NH₃ / H₂O | --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Functional Flow of a NH₃–H₂O VARS ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[EVAPORATOR
Low Pressure PE
Refrigeration Effect QE] -->|NH3 Vapour h1| B1[ABSORBER
Low Pressure PE
Heat Rejected QA] B1 -->|Strong Solution hs at PE| C1[PUMP
Work Input Wp] C1 -->|Strong Solution hs at PG| D1[HEAT EXCHANGER
HX1 Weak Solution Heats Up] D1 -->|Strong Solution Heated| E1[GENERATOR
High Pressure PG
Heat Input QG] E1 -->|NH3 Vapour With Water h2| F1[RECTIFIER AND ANALYSER
Purification of NH3] E1 -->|Weak Solution hw at PG| G1[EXPANSION VALVE 1
Throttling PG to PE] G1 -->|Weak Solution at PE| B1 F1 -->|Pure NH3 Vapour h2| H1[CONDENSER
High Pressure PC

Throttling PC to PE] I1 -->|Two Phase NH3 h4| A1 D1 -.->|Preheats Strong Solution| G1 ``` **Reading guide for students:** - Follow the **refrigerant loop** (A1 → I1 → back to A1) — that is the same as in VCRS. - Follow the **solution loop** (B1 → C1 → E1 → G1 → B1) — this is the *thermal compressor*. - The **rectifier** (F1) is what makes NH₃ pure; without it water would clog the condenser. ## 4.2 Mermaid Block — LiBr–H₂O VARS (Single-Effect Chiller) ```mermaid flowchart LR A2[EVAPORATOR
Low Pressure PE
Water Evaporates Absorbs Heat QE] -->|Water Vapour h1| B2[ABSORBER
Low Pressure PE
Heat Rejected QA] B2 -->|Strong LiBr Solution at PE| C2[PUMP
Work Input Wp] C2 -->|Strong LiBr Solution at PG| D2[HEAT EXCHANGER
Weak Solution Heats Strong Solution] D2 -->|Strong LiBr Heated| E2[GENERATOR
High Pressure PG

High Pressure PC
Heat Rejected QC] E2 -->|Weak LiBr Solution at PG| G2[EXPANSION VALVE 1
