Throttling PC to PE] I2 -->|Two Phase Water h4| A2 D2 -.->|Weak LiBr Preheats| G2 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Notice the difference:** In LiBr–H₂O there is **no rectifier** because LiBr is *non-volatile* — only water vaporises. Hence the system is simpler. However, water being the refrigerant means it **cannot produce temperatures below 0 °C** — LiBr–H₂O is used **only for A/C (T_E ≈ 5–10 °C)**. ## 4.3 Mermaid Block — Electrolux Three-Fluid System ```mermaid flowchart LR A3[EVAPORATOR
NH3 Evaporates QE] -->|NH3 Vapour| B3[ABSORBER
NH3 Absorbed in Water] B3 -->|NH3 Water Solution| C3[PUMP
Wp to PG] C3 -->|High Pressure Solution| D3[GENERATOR
Hydrogen Gas Inert Pad] D3 -->|NH3 Liberated With H2| E3[SEPARATOR AND RECTIFIER] E3 -->|Pure NH3 Vapour| F3[CONDENSER
QC Rejected] D3 -->|H2 Recirculation| A3 E3 -->|H2 With NH3| A3 F3 -->|Liquid NH3| G3[EXPANSION VALVE] G3 -->|Two Phase NH3| A3 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Electrolux three-fluid principle:** A third inert gas (**hydrogen, H₂**) circulates in the evaporator side. Partial pressures allow NH₃ to evaporate even at uniform total pressure. The advantage: **no moving pump** is theoretically needed — gravity / thermosiphon drives the flow. Historically used in **domestic refrigerators** (e.g., 1925 Servel units). ## 4.4 Comparison Block Diagram — VARS vs VCRS ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph VCRS_BLOCK[Vapour Compression Refrigeration System] V1[Evaporator] -->|Vapour| V2[Compressor
Work Input Wc] V2 -->|Hot Vapour| V3[Condenser] V3 -->|Liquid| V4[Expansion Valve] V4 -->|Two Phase| V1 end subgraph VARS_BLOCK[Vapour Absorption Refrigeration System] W1[Evaporator] -->|Vapour| W2[Absorber
Heat Rejected QA] W2 -->|Strong Solution| W3[Pump
Wp Tiny] W3 -->|High Pressure Solution| W4[Generator
Heat Input QG] W4 -->|Vapour| W5[Condenser] W5 -->|Liquid| W6[Expansion Valve] W6 -->|Two Phase| W1 end ``` --- # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Comparison: Vapour Compression vs Vapour Absorption | Feature | Vapour Compression (VCRS) | Vapour Absorption (VARS) | |---------|---------------------------|---------------------------| | Input energy | **Mechanical work** (shaft) | **Heat** (steam, waste, solar) | | Compressor | Mechanical — handles vapour | **Absent** — replaced by Absorber + Pump + Generator | | Moving parts | Many (compressor, motor) | Very few (only a small liquid pump) | | Noise / vibration | High | Almost silent | | COP | **High** (3–5 for practical systems) | **Low** (0.3–0.7) | | Capacity | Wide range (W to MW) | Mainly **large capacity** (> 100 kW) | | Refrigerant | Any (CFC, HFC, NH₃, etc.) | NH₃ / H₂O or LiBr / H₂O | | Use of waste heat | **No** | **Yes** — major advantage | | Initial cost | Lower | Higher (extra absorber + generator) | | Maintenance | Frequent | Low (no wear parts) | | Ability to use solar / geothermal | Not possible | **Possible** | | Temperature range | Very low ($< -40\,^\circ$C easily) | NH₃ system: < 0 °C; LiBr system: > 0 °C only | | Start-up under load | Possible immediately | Difficult — must preheat generator | ## 5.2 Drawbacks of Vapour Absorption Systems 1. **Low COP** compared to VCRS — typically 30–50 % of Carnot. 2. **Bulky equipment** — absorber and generator are large heat exchangers. 3. **High initial cost** — heat-exchanger area is significant. 4. **Slow response to load changes** — generator thermal inertia is high. 5. **Crystallisation of LiBr** — at high concentration LiBr may crystallise, blocking flow. 6. **Corrosion** by NH₃ on copper parts (so copper tubing is avoided). 7. **Need for rectification** in NH₃ systems adds complexity. 8. **Limited temperature range** — LiBr cannot reach below 0 °C. 9. **Pumping penalty** in tall installations (head of strong-solution column). 10. **Difficult capacity modulation** — part-load efficiency drops sharply. ## 5.3 KTU Model Questions --- ### Part A — 3-Mark Questions (Answer in ~50 words) **A1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *Differentiate between Vapour Compression and Vapour Absorption refrigeration systems on the basis of input energy and COP.* **Model Answer:** A Vapour Compression Refrigeration System (VCRS) uses **mechanical work** from an electric-motor-driven compressor to circulate the refrigerant and typically has a COP of **3–5**. A Vapour Absorption Refrigeration System (VARS) uses **heat energy** at the generator (steam, waste heat, solar) and replaces the mechanical compressor with an *absorber + pump + generator* combination. The COP of a VARS is much lower (0.3–0.7), but it can use low-grade or waste heat that would otherwise be discarded. **A2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]** *Why is a rectifier used in the NH₃–H₂O absorption system but not in the LiBr–H₂O system?* **Model Answer:** In the **NH₃–H₂O system**, water is the absorbent but is also *volatile*; it vaporises along with NH₃ in the generator. A **rectifier** (and analyzer) is therefore needed to remove water vapour so that *nearly pure NH₃* enters the condenser. In the **LiBr–H₂O system**, water is the refrigerant and LiBr is a **non-volatile salt** — it does not vaporise. Hence no rectification is required and the system is mechanically simpler. --- ### Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Choice-based, with sub-parts) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]** **(a)** With a neat schematic, explain the working of a **simple vapour absorption refrigeration system** using NH₃–H₂O as the working pair. Clearly mark all state points, pressures, and the direction of flow of refrigerant and strong solution. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Derive an expression for the **maximum COP of an ideal vapour absorption refrigeration system** in terms of evaporator, condenser, and generator temperatures. Compare it with the Carnot COP of a VCRS operating between the same $T_E$ and $T_C$. **(7 Marks)** --- **Model Solution for Q-A(a):** 1. **Components and state points (NH₃–H₂O VARS):** - **State 1** — Saturated NH₃ vapour leaving the **evaporator** at $(P_E, T_E)$. - **State 2** — NH₃ vapour leaving the **generator/rectifier** at $(P_G, T_G)$, ≈ pure. - **State 3** — Saturated liquid NH₃ leaving the **condenser** at $(P_C, T_C)$. - **State 4** — Two-phase mixture after **expansion valve 2** at $(P_E, T_E)$. - **State 5** — Weak (lean) NH₃–H₂O solution entering the **absorber** at $P_E$. - **State 6** — Strong (rich) NH₃–H₂O solution leaving the **absorber** at $P_E$. - **State 7** — High-pressure strong solution leaving the **pump** at $P_G$. - **State 8** — Weak solution leaving the **generator** at $P_G$. 2. **Working cycle:** - **Evaporator:** Liquid NH₃ at state 4 evaporates at low $P_E$, absorbing refrigeration effect $Q_E = \dot{m}_r (h_1 - h_4)$ from the cold space. - **Absorber:** NH₃ vapour at state 1 is absorbed by weak solution (state 5) to form strong solution (state 6), releasing $Q_A$. - **Pump:** Strong solution (state 6 → state 7) is pressurised from $P_E$ to $P_G$ using pump work $W_p$. - **Generator + Rectifier:** Heat $Q_G$ is supplied (typically from steam or waste heat) to vaporise NH₃ from the strong solution. The vapour passes through the analyzer + rectifier where water vapour is condensed and returned as reflux; pure NH₃ vapour (state 2) goes to the condenser. - **Condenser:** NH₃ condenses at $P_C$ and $T_C$, releasing $Q_C = \dot{m}_r (h_2 - h_3)$. - **Expansion valve:** Liquid NH₃ throttles from $P_C$ to $P_E$ (state 3 → state 4) and re-enters the evaporator. > [!NOTE] > The solution loop (5 → 6 → 7 → 8 → 5) is the "thermal compressor" — it does the job of a mechanical compressor in VCRS but uses heat instead of work. --- **Model Solution for Q-A(b):** **Step 1 — Identify the heat-engine and the refrigerator portions:** - The *generator–condenser* pair forms a **heat engine** that operates between $T_G$ (hot source) and $T_C$ (cold sink). - The *evaporator–absorber* pair forms a **refrigerator** that operates between $T_E$ (cold space) and $T_G$ (hot source, i.e. the temperature at which heat is rejected from the working fluid of the heat engine). **Step 2 — Carnot efficiency of the heat engine:** $$\eta_{\text{Carnot}} \;=\; 1 - \frac{T_C}{T_G}$$ The work produced by the heat engine per unit heat input $Q_G$: $$\frac{W}{Q_G} \;=\; 1 - \frac{T_C}{T_G} \;=\; \frac{T_G - T_C}{T_G}$$ **Step 3 — COP of the refrigerator (using $W$ as input):** $$\text{COP}_{\text{ref}} \;=\; \frac{Q_E}{W} \;=\; \frac{T_E}{T_G - T_E}$$ **Step 4 — Multiply (Step 2 × Step 3):** $$\text{COP}_{\max} \;=\; \frac{Q_E}{Q_G} \;=\; \frac{T_E}{T_G - T_E} \cdot \frac{T_G - T_C}{T_G}$$ **Step 5 — Compare with VCRS COP:** $$\text{COP}_{\text{VCRS,Carnot}} \;=\; \frac{T_E}{T_C - T_E}$$ The ratio: $$\frac{\text{COP}_{\text{VARS,max}}}{\text{COP}_{\text{VCRS,Carnot}}} \;=\; \frac{(T_G - T_C)(T_C - T_E)}{(T_G - T_E)T_G}$$ For typical values $T_E = 263\,\text{K}$, $T_C = 308\,\text{K}$, $T_G = 388\,\text{K}$: $$\frac{\text{COP}_{\text{VARS}}}{\text{COP}_{\text{VCRS}}} \;=\; \frac{(388-308)(308-263)}{(388-263)\cdot 388} \;=\; \frac{80 \times 45}{125 \times 388} \;=\; 0.0742$$ So the ideal VARS COP is **only 7.4 %** of the VCRS COP — confirming the inherent thermodynamic inferiority of the absorption cycle, offset only by the use of cheap waste heat. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2023]** **(a)** Explain the construction and working of a **Lithium Bromide–Water (LiBr–H₂O) vapour absorption system**. State clearly why this system is preferred for **air-conditioning applications** but **not for sub-zero refrigeration**. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the working of the **Electrolux three-fluid absorption system**. What is the role of hydrogen gas and why is it called a "no-moving-part" refrigerator? **(7 Marks)** --- **Model Solution for Q-B(a):** 1. **Construction of LiBr–H₂O VARS:** - The system consists of an **evaporator**, **absorber**, **pump**, **heat exchanger**, **generator**, **condenser**, and **two expansion valves**. - **Water** is the **refrigerant**; **LiBr (a hygroscopic salt)** is the **absorbent**. - Both absorber and generator operate at *very low pressures* (≈ 6–10 mm Hg in the evaporator and ≈ 70–100 mm Hg in the generator/condenser) to allow water to boil at low temperatures. 2. **Working:** - **Evaporator:** Water refrigerant evaporates at ≈ 5 °C by absorbing heat from the chilled water circulating through the cooling coil (refrigeration effect $Q_E$). - **Absorber:** Water vapour is absorbed by the weak LiBr solution (≈ 60 % concentration), forming strong LiBr solution (≈ 65 %). Heat of absorption $Q_A$ is rejected to the cooling-water circuit. - **Pump:** Strong solution is pumped to the high-pressure generator. - **Generator:** Low-pressure steam (≈ 0.1–0.2 MPa) supplies heat $Q_G$ to boil water out of the strong solution, leaving behind weak LiBr solution. - **Condenser:** Pure water vapour condenses on the cooling tubes, rejecting $Q_C$ to the cooling water. - **Expansion valve:** Water throttles to the evaporator pressure; weak LiBr solution throttles back to the absorber. - **Solution heat exchanger:** Recovers heat from the hot weak solution leaving the generator to pre-heat the strong solution going to the generator — improves COP by 40–50 %. 3. **Why preferred for A/C:** - **Water as refrigerant** is non-toxic, non-flammable, and has **ODP = 0**, **GWP = 0**. - The system runs at **very low pressures** (vacuum) — safe, no leak hazard. - Quiet operation, few moving parts (just one pump). - Can use **low-grade steam, hot water, or solar thermal** as the heat source. - The COP is reasonable for A/C temperatures (typically 0.6–0.8). 4. **Why not for sub-zero refrigeration:** - Water **freezes at 0 °C (273.15 K)** — it cannot remain a refrigerant below 0 °C. - At low evaporator pressures, **water + LiBr solution can form ice crystals** and cause **crystallisation of LiBr** at high concentration, blocking tubes. - The maximum achievable $T_E$ is limited to ≈ 0–5 °C; therefore LiBr–H₂O is **exclusively for A/C and heat-pump applications above 0 °C**. --- **Model Solution for Q-B(b):** 1. **Electrolux Three-Fluid System — Construction:** - The system uses **three fluids**: - **Refrigerant:** Ammonia (NH₃) - **Absorbent:** Water (H₂O) - **Inert gas:** Hydrogen (H₂) - The main components are: **evaporator, absorber, generator, condenser, separator/rectifier** — but **no liquid pump and no expansion valve**. 2. **Working principle:** - The whole system is filled with **hydrogen gas** at a slight over-pressure to lower the partial pressure of NH₃ in the evaporator. - In the **generator**, heat $Q_G$ vaporises NH₃ from the NH₃–H₂O solution. NH₃ vapour (mixed with H₂) rises and is separated in the rectifier — the **NH₃ passes upward to the condenser**, while the H₂ returns downward. - In the **condenser**, NH₃ condenses by rejecting $Q_C$ to the surroundings. - The liquid NH₃ then enters the **evaporator** where it meets the circulating H₂ stream. The partial pressure of NH₃ drops, allowing NH₃ to **evaporate at the desired low temperature** while absorbing refrigeration effect $Q_E$ from the cold space. - The NH₃ + H₂ mixture then passes to the **absorber**, where NH₃ is absorbed by the water (the H₂ circulates back to the evaporator). - The strong NH₃–H₂O solution is returned to the generator by **gravity** (or thermosiphon), not by a pump. 3. **Role of hydrogen:** - Hydrogen acts as an **inert buffer gas** that lowers the **partial pressure** of NH₃ in the evaporator so that NH₃ can evaporate at the required temperature **without lowering the total system pressure** below atmospheric. - The total pressure remains approximately atmospheric — this eliminates the need for a **vacuum-tight, leak-proof construction**. 4. **"No-moving-part" refrigerator:** - The system has **no compressor, no liquid pump, and no expansion valve**. - The flow of liquid NH₃ between the condenser and evaporator is driven by **gravity** (in vertical configurations) or by the density difference of H₂-rich and H₂-poor gas mixtures. - Therefore, it operates **silently and with no mechanical wear**. - Historically used in **domestic refrigerators** by the Servel and Electrolux companies in the 1920s–1940s. Modern variants include the **diffusion-absorption refrigerator** and the **Platinum-Refrigerator** used in hotel mini-bars. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Confusing the working pair:** NH₃–H₂O — *NH₃ is the refrigerant*; LiBr–H₂O — *H₂O is the refrigerant*. Marks deducted for the wrong role. > 2. **Forgetting the rectifier in NH₃–H₂O:** Many students omit the analyzer/rectifier from the schematic — this is a 1-mark loss. > 3. **Wrong sign in COP formula:** Writing $\text{COP} = Q_G / Q_E$ instead of $Q_E / Q_G$ — full 7-mark sub-part may be invalidated. > 4. **Forgetting the temperature limits of LiBr–H₂O:** Stating it is used for "all refrigeration" — it is restricted to *above 0 °C*. > 5. **Not stating that the pump work in VARS is negligible:** This is a key KTU 2024 insight; missing it costs 1 mark. > 6. **In numerical problems, not computing both the *maximum* and the *practical* COP** — KTU asks for both. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember - **VARS = Heat-driven refrigeration**; **VCRS = Work-driven refrigeration**. - The **absorber + pump + generator** combination is the *thermal compressor* of a VARS. - **NH₃–H₂O** uses NH₃ as refrigerant — for industrial and sub-zero applications; needs a **rectifier**. - **LiBr–H₂O** uses H₂O as refrigerant — for A/C only (above 0 °C); **no rectifier** because LiBr is non-volatile. - **Electrolux system** uses NH₃ + H₂O + H₂; **no moving parts**; suitable for silent domestic refrigeration. - **Maximum COP of an ideal VARS:** $$\text{COP}_{\max} = \frac{T_E}{T_G - T_E} \cdot \frac{T_G - T_C}{T_G}$$ - **Actual COP** is only **40–60 %** of $\text{COP}_{\max}$. - **VARS has lower COP than VCRS** but uses **cheap / waste heat** — its merit is economic, not thermodynamic. - **Pump work in VARS** ≈ 0.5–1 % of $Q_E$ — usually neglected. - **Drawbacks:** low COP, bulky equipment, slow response, risk of LiBr crystallisation, NH₃ corrosion, high initial cost. - **Heat balance:** $Q_G + Q_E + W_p = Q_C + Q_A$ (overall). - **Solution-circulation ratio:** $f = 1 / (x_s - x_w)$ — must be computed for NH₃ systems. - **KTU Killer numbers to memorise:** Standard $T_E = 263\,\text{K}$, $T_C = 308\,\text{K}$, $T_G = 388\,\text{K}$, $T_A = 298\,\text{K}$. - **Preferred exam points:** Schematic + state-point labelling (NH₃); COP derivation; comparison table with VCRS; drawbacks of VARS.

topics.title: Refrigerants: Properties, Eco-Friendly Refrigerants, Mixed Refrigerants, Selection Criteria for Different Applications, Refrigerant Leakage Detection, Refrigerant Charging
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET631 - REFRIGERATION AND AIR CONDITIONING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Refrigerants: Properties, Eco-Friendly Refrigerants, Mixed Refrigerants, Selection Criteria for Different Applications, Refrigerant Leakage Detection, Refrigerant Charging

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Refrigerant:** A refrigerant is a working fluid used in a refrigeration cycle that undergoes a phase change (typically vaporization and condensation) to absorb heat from a low-temperature reservoir (evaporator) and reject it to a high-temperature reservoir (condenser). It is the "blood" of any refrigeration system, circulating continuously through the vapor compression cycle.

Mathematically, the performance of a refrigerant is evaluated through the **Coefficient of Performance (COP)**:

$$COP_{ref} = \frac{Q_L}{W_{net}} = \frac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}$$

where $h_1, h_2, h_3, h_4$ are the specific enthalpies (kJ/kg) at the compressor inlet, compressor exit, condenser exit, and evaporator inlet respectively.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Think of a Refrigerant as a "Heat Taxi":**
> Imagine a taxi (refrigerant) that picks up passengers (heat) in a cold neighborhood (evaporator), drives them across town, and drops them off in a hot downtown area (condenser). The taxi is empty (low-pressure vapor) when it returns to pick up more passengers. Just like we want a taxi that is fuel-efficient, safe, cheap, and doesn't pollute the air, an ideal refrigerant must have excellent thermodynamic, physical, chemical, and environmental properties.

### 1.3 Classification of Refrigerants

> [!NOTE]
> **Primary Classification Categories:**
> 1. **Primary Refrigerants** – Fluids that directly produce cooling by absorbing heat (e.g., R-12, R-22, R-134a, NH₃, CO₂).
> 2. **Secondary Refrigerants** – Fluids that transport heat from the space to be cooled to the primary refrigerant (e.g., brine, glycol, water).

| Generation | Era | Example Refrigerants | Key Characteristic |
|------------|-----|----------------------|--------------------|
| 1st Gen | 1800s–1930s | Natural refrigerants (NH₃, CO₂, SO₂, H₂O, R-11, R-12) | CFCs introduced |
| 2nd Gen | 1930s–1980s | R-12, R-22, R-502 (CFC/HCFC family) | High stability, but ozone-depleting |
| 3rd Gen | 1980s–1990s | R-134a, R-123 (HFC/HCFC alternatives) | Zero ODP replacements |
| 4th Gen (Eco) | 1990s–Present | HFCs, HFOs, Natural (R-290, R-744, R-717) | Low GWP, zero ODP |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Desired Properties of an Ideal Refrigerant

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **An ideal refrigerant should possess four categories of properties:**

### 2.1.1 Thermodynamic Properties

- **Low boiling point** at atmospheric pressure (lower than the desired evaporator temperature).
- **High latent heat of vaporization** → higher refrigeration effect per kg.
- **Low specific volume of vapor** → smaller compressor displacement.
- **High critical temperature** and pressure (but not too high critical pressure).
- **Low condenser pressure** → to use air-cooled condensers easily.
- **High COP** for the given temperature range.
- Low **specific heat of liquid** (so that throttling loss is small).

### 2.1.2 Physical Properties

- **Low freezing point** (so it doesn't solidify at evaporator conditions).
- Low viscosity (good flow characteristics, small pressure drop).
- High thermal conductivity (efficient heat transfer).
- Non-corrosive to metals (compatible with copper, steel, aluminum).
- **High dielectric strength** (for hermetic compressors).
- Non-miscible with lubricating oil (so oil returns easily to compressor).

### 2.1.3 Chemical Properties

- **Chemically stable** (does not decompose at high temperatures).
- Non-flammable and non-explosive.
- Non-toxic and non-irritating.
- No chemical reaction with moisture or oil.
- No bad odor.

### 2.1.4 Safety & Environmental Properties

- **Zero Ozone Depletion Potential (ODP = 0)**.
- **Low Global Warming Potential (GWP)**.
- Non-flammable (preferably A1 class: lower toxicity, no flame propagation).
- Long atmospheric life (less leakage impact).
- No harmful breakdown products.

### 2.2 Environmental Indices: ODP, GWP, TEWI & LCCP

> [!NOTE]
> **Ozone Depletion Potential (ODP):** Relative measure of a substance's ability to destroy stratospheric ozone. R-11 (CFC) is the reference with **ODP = 1.0**.

**Global Warming Potential (GWP):** Relative measure of heat trapped in the atmosphere over a time horizon (typically 100 years), with **CO₂ as reference (GWP = 1.0)**.

$$GWP = \frac{\int_0^{TH} a_i \cdot [x_i(t)] dt}{\int_0^{TH} a_{CO_2} \cdot [x_{CO_2}(t)] dt}$$

where $a_i$ is the radiative efficiency, $x_i(t)$ is the time-decaying atmospheric concentration, and $TH$ is the time horizon (usually **100 years**).

**TEWI (Total Equivalent Warming Impact):**

$$TEWI = GWP_{direct} \times m_{leakage} + GWP_{indirect} \times E_{annual} \times n$$

where $m_{leakage}$ = total refrigerant leakage (kg), $E_{annual}$ = annual energy consumption (kWh), $n$ = system lifetime in years.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Property | Expression | Units | Purpose |
|--------------------|------------|-------|---------|
| Refrigeration Effect | $q_L = h_1 - h_4$ | kJ/kg | Heat absorbed per kg refrigerant |
| Compressor Work | $W_c = h_2 - h_1$ | kJ/kg | Work input to compressor |
| Heat Rejected | $q_H = h_2 - h_3$ | kJ/kg | Heat rejected at condenser |
| COP (Actual) | $COP = \dfrac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}$ | – | Real system efficiency |
| Theoretical COP (Carnot) | $COP_{Carnot} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$ | – | Upper limit benchmark |
| Volumetric Capacity | $q_v = \rho_1 \cdot (h_1 - h_4)$ | kJ/m³ | Cooling per unit volume |
| Pressure Ratio | $r_p = \dfrac{P_{cond}}{P_{evap}}$ | – | Compressor discharge ratio |
| ODP (Relative) | $ODP_x = \dfrac{\text{Destruction by } x}{\text{Destruction by R-11}}$ | – | Ozone impact |
| GWP (Relative, 100-yr) | $GWP_x = \dfrac{\text{Heat trapped by } x}{\text{Heat trapped by CO}_2}$ | – | Climate impact |
| TEWI | $GWP \cdot m_{leak} + n \cdot E_{annual} \cdot GWP_{CO_2}$ | kg CO₂ eq. | Life-cycle climate load |
| Atmospheric Life | $\tau = \dfrac{1}{k_{sink}}$ | years | Persistence in air |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The properties of refrigerants directly impact:
> - **System COP** (electricity bills).
> - **Compressor size and cost** (specific volume of vapor).
> - **Material selection** (corrosion, lubrication compatibility).
> - **Regulatory compliance** (Montreal & Kigali Protocols, F-Gas Regulation in EU).
> - **Safety hazards** (toxicity, flammability ratings by ASHRAE Standard 34).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Theoretical COP of an Ideal Vapor Compression Cycle

Consider a **Carnot refrigeration cycle** operating between a high-temperature reservoir $T_H$ and a low-temperature reservoir $T_L$.

**Step 1:** Net heat rejected to high-temperature reservoir:

$$q_H = T_H (S_2 - S_1) = T_H \cdot \Delta S$$

**Step 2:** Net heat absorbed from low-temperature reservoir:

$$q_L = T_L (S_1 - S_4) = T_L \cdot \Delta S$$

**Step 3:** Net work input (by First Law, energy conservation):

$$W_{net} = q_H - q_L = (T_H - T_L) \cdot \Delta S$$

**Step 4:** COP definition (ratio of desired effect to work input):

$$\begin{aligned}
COP_{ref} &= \frac{q_L}{W_{net}} \\
&= \frac{T_L \cdot \Delta S}{(T_H - T_L) \cdot \Delta S} \\
COP_{ref} &= \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}
\end{aligned}$$

This is the **theoretical maximum COP**. Real refrigerants operate well below this due to irreversibilities.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Major Refrigerants

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is **mandatory for KTU exams** and provides side-by-side engineering comparison.

| Property | R-12 (CFC) | R-22 (HCFC) | R-134a (HFC) | R-717 (NH₃) | R-744 (CO₂) | R-290 (Propane) |
|----------|------------|-------------|--------------|-------------|-------------|------------------|
| Chemical Formula | CCl₂F₂ | CHClF₂ | CH₂FCF₃ | NH₃ | CO₂ | C₃H₈ |
| Molecular Weight (kg/kmol) | 120.91 | 86.48 | 102.03 | 17.03 | 44.01 | 44.10 |
| Boiling Point (°C) | –29.79 | –40.81 | –26.07 | –33.34 | –78.4 (sublimes) | –42.07 |
| Critical Temp. (°C) | 112.0 | 96.2 | 101.06 | 132.4 | 31.0 | 96.7 |
| Critical Pressure (MPa) | 4.11 | 4.97 | 4.059 | 11.33 | 7.38 | 4.25 |
| Latent Heat (kJ/kg at 0°C) | 152.0 | 205.2 | 198.4 | 1377 | 232 (at 0°C) | 425 |
| ODP | 1.0 | 0.055 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| GWP (100-yr) | 10,900 | 1,810 | 1,430 | 0 | 1 | 3 |
| Atmospheric Life (years) | 100 | 12 | 14.6 | <0.02 | Variable | 0.04 |
| ASHRAE Safety Class | A1 | A1 | A1 | B2L | A1 | A3 |
| Toxicity | Low | Low | Low | High | Low | Low |
| Flammability | None | None | None | Slight | None | High |
| Oil Miscibility | Miscible | Miscible | Miscible | Immiscible | Immiscible | Miscible |
| Application | Phased out | AC (phased out) | Domestic AC, auto AC | Industrial, cold storage | Cascade, trans-critical | Domestic fridges |

### 3.3 Eco-Friendly Refrigerants (Green Refrigerants)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** Eco-friendly refrigerants are those with **ODP = 0** and **GWP < 150** (per Kigali Amendment to Montreal Protocol).

**Categories:**

1. **Natural Refrigerants** (Ozone-safe, ultra-low GWP):
   - **R-717 (Ammonia, NH₃)** – Industrial cold storage, ice plants. Toxic but excellent thermodynamics.
   - **R-744 (Carbon Dioxide, CO₂)** – Trans-critical cycles, hot climates, automotive AC.
   - **R-290 (Propane, C₃H₈)** – Domestic refrigerators, small chillers. Highly flammable but GWP = 3.
   - **R-600a (Isobutane)** – Domestic refrigerators (replaced R-134a in Europe).
   - **R-1270 (Propylene)** – Industrial chillers.

2. **HFC Refrigerants** (Zero ODP, but high GWP):
   - R-134a, R-32, R-125, R-143a, R-152a.

3. **HFO Refrigerants** (Zero ODP, ultra-low GWP):
   - **R-1234yf** (GWP = 4) – Auto AC replacement for R-134a.
   - **R-1234ze** (GWP < 1) – Commercial chillers.
   - Mildly flammable (A2L class).

4. **HFC/HFO Blends**: R-513A, R-450A, R-407F – drop-in replacements.

### 3.4 Mixed Refrigerants

> [!NOTE]
> **Mixed Refrigerant Definition:** A binary or tertiary blend of two or more pure refrigerants to combine desirable properties and eliminate individual drawbacks.

**Step 1: Why mix refrigerants?**
- To obtain intermediate boiling points.
- To reduce flammability (e.g., adding R-32 to flammable HFO).
- To match glide characteristics with heat exchangers (zeotropes).
- To provide non-flammable blends (R-410A, R-407C).

**Step 2: Types of Refrigerant Mixtures**

| Type | Definition | Example | Behavior During Phase Change |
|------|------------|---------|------------------------------|
| **Azeotropic** | Behaves like a pure fluid; constant boiling point | R-502, R-507, R-410A | Single saturation temperature at given pressure |
| **Zeotropic** | Exhibits "glide" – temperature varies during phase change | R-407C, R-404A, R-409A | Different bubble and dew points at same pressure |
| **Near-Azeotropic** | Very small temperature glide (< 1°C) | R-401A, R-401B | Practically constant saturation T |

**Step 3: Glide Analysis for a Zeotrope**

$$\Delta T_{glide} = T_{dew} - T_{bubble}$$

For example, **R-407C** has a glide of about **7°C**, meaning the refrigerant boils across a temperature range rather than at a single temperature. This is advantageous in counterflow heat exchangers (better temperature match, higher effectiveness).

**Step 4: Composition and Leakage Impact**

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Issue:** In zeotropic blends, if the mixture leaks (say, the more volatile component leaks preferentially), the **composition shifts**, and the system must be recharged with **liquid** (not vapor) to restore original composition. This is called **fractionation**.

### 3.5 Selection Criteria for Different Applications

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Refrigerant selection is a multi-attribute decision based on:**

| Application | Typical Temperature Range | Preferred Refrigerant | Justification |
|-------------|--------------------------|----------------------|---------------|
| Domestic Refrigerator | –10°C to 0°C evaporator | R-600a, R-134a | Low charge, low toxicity, energy efficient |
| Window/Split AC | 5°C to 10°C evaporator | R-32, R-410A | High latent heat, non-flammable, low-mid GWP |
| Centrifugal Chiller (Large) | 0°C to 5°C | R-123, R-134a, R-1234ze | High volumetric capacity, low pressure ratio |
| Industrial Cold Storage (–40°C) | –35°C evaporator | R-717 (NH₃), R-744, R-404A | High latent heat, low temp operation |
| Frozen Food (–30°C to –50°C) | –40°C evaporator | R-502, R-507, R-404A | Low boiling point, good low-temp performance |
| Auto AC (Hot Climates) | 0°C to 5°C | R-1234yf, R-134a | Trans-critical CO₂ also used |
| Cryogenics (< –80°C) | Cascade systems | R-13, R-23, R-744 | Very low boiling points |
| Heat Pump (Heating) | –15°C to 0°C | R-410A, R-32, R-290 | High COP at low ambient, high discharge T |

**Selection Matrix (Weighted Scoring):**

$$\text{Score}_i = w_1 \cdot T_i + w_2 \cdot S_i + w_3 \cdot E_i + w_4 \cdot C_i + w_5 \cdot A_i$$

where weights $w_1, ..., w_5$ correspond to Thermodynamics, Safety, Environment, Cost, and Availability respectively.

### 3.6 Refrigerant Leakage Detection

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Detect Leaks?** Loss of refrigerant → reduced cooling capacity, increased compressor work, environmental damage, safety hazard (toxic/flammable).

**Methods of Leak Detection:**

| Method | Working Principle | Sensitivity | Best Application |
|--------|-------------------|-------------|------------------|
| **Soap Bubble Test** | Apply soapy water; bubbles form at leak | Moderate (visible leaks) | Field quick check |
| **Electronic Sniffer (Heated Diode/Sensor)** | Detects refrigerant in air via heated sensor | High (3 g/yr for HFCs) | Routine maintenance |
| **Halide Torch** | Copper ring turns green with halogens (CFC/HCFC) | Moderate | Old CFCs only |
| **Ultrasonic Detector** | Picks up high-frequency hiss of escaping gas | High | Pressurized systems |
| **Fluorescent Dye (UV)** | Dye added to system, leaks glow under UV light | Moderate | Oil systems, large chillers |
| **Pressure Decay Test** | Isolate section, monitor pressure drop over time | Very high | New installations |
| **Mass Spectrometer (Helium Tracer)** | He injected, leaks detected with mass spec | Ultra-high | R&D, factory testing |
| **IR Spectroscopy** | Detects C-F bond absorption of HFCs/HFOs | High | Modern systems |
| **Capacitance Diaphragm Gauge** | Measures minute pressure changes | Ultra-high | Laboratory |

**ASHRAE Standard 147 Procedure (Standardized Leak Test):**
1. Pressurize the system with dry nitrogen to design pressure.
2. Isolate the system and wait for temperature stabilization.
3. Record pressure vs. time for 24 hours.
4. Calculate leak rate: $\dot{m} = \dfrac{V \cdot \Delta P}{R_{spec} \cdot T \cdot \Delta t}$

### 3.7 Refrigerant Charging

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** Refrigerant charging is the controlled addition of refrigerant into a refrigeration system to achieve design operating conditions (proper suction superheat, subcooling, and capacity).

**Step-by-Step Charging Procedure (Vapor Compression System):**

**Step 1:** Ensure system is leak-tested, evacuated (≤ 500 microns Hg), and dry.

**Step 2:** Verify liquid/vapor charging line; for blends, charge from **liquid cylinder** (upright position) to prevent fractionation.

**Step 3:** Add refrigerant gradually through the service valve on the low-pressure side (for vapor) or liquid line (for bulk charge).

**Step 4:** **For subcritical systems** with **TXV (Thermostatic Expansion Valve):** Charge to manufacturer's recommended **subcooling** (typically 8–12°F / 4–7°C).

**Step 5:** **For systems with fixed orifice (capillary tube):** Charge to recommended **superheat** (typically 6–10°C).

**Step 6:** Verify operating parameters:
- Suction pressure/temperature
- Discharge pressure/temperature
- Superheat at evaporator outlet
- Subcooling at condenser outlet
- Compressor amp draw

**Step 7:** Use **charging chart** or **digital scale** to add correct mass.

**Step 8:** Document refrigerant type, mass, date, and technician in service log.

**Critical Superheat Formula:**

$$SH = T_{suction,actual} - T_{saturation}\,(P_{evap})$$

**Critical Subcooling Formula:**

$$SC = T_{saturation}\,(P_{cond}) - T_{liquid\,outlet,actual}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Refrigerant Classification Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Refrigerants]
    A --> B[By Function]
    A --> C[By Chemistry]
    A --> D[By Environmental Impact]

    B --> B1[Primary]
    B --> B2[Secondary Brine, Glycol]

    C --> C1[CFC Chlorofluorocarbon]
    C --> C2[HCFC Hydrochlorofluorocarbon]
    C --> C3[HFC Hydrofluorocarbon]
    C --> C4[HFO Hydrofluoroolefin]
    C --> C5[Natural Refrigerants]

    C1 --> C1a[R11, R12, R502]
    C2 --> C2a[R22]
    C3 --> C3a[R134a, R32, R125, R143a]
    C4 --> C4a[R1234yf, R1234ze]
    C5 --> C5a[R717 Ammonia]
    C5 --> C5b[R744 Carbon Dioxide]
    C5 --> C5c[R290 Propane]
    C5 --> C5d[R600a Isobutane]

    D --> D1[Eco-Friendly ODP 0, GWP less than 150]
    D --> D2[Phasing Out High ODP or GWP]
    D --> D3[Banned Substances]

    D1 --> D2a
    D2 --> C2a
    D3 --> C1a
```

### 4.2 Desired Properties of an Ideal Refrigerant (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Ideal Refrigerant]
    A --> T[Thermodynamic Properties]
    A --> P[Physical Properties]
    A --> C[Chemical Properties]
    A --> E[Environmental Properties]
    A --> S[Safety Properties]

    T --> T1[Low Boiling Point]
    T --> T2[High Latent Heat]
    T --> T3[High Critical Temp]
    T --> T4[Low Specific Volume]
    T --> T5[High COP]

    P --> P1[Low Freezing Point]
    P --> P2[Low Viscosity]
    P --> P3[High Thermal Conductivity]
    P --> P4[Oil Miscibility Control]
    P --> P5[Non Corrosive]

    C --> C1[Chemical Stability]
    C --> C2[No Reaction with Oil and Moisture]

    E --> E1[Zero ODP]
    E --> E2[Low GWP less than 150]
    E --> E3[Short Atmospheric Life]
    E --> E4[Low TEWI]

    S --> S1[Non Toxic]
    S --> S2[Non Flammable ASHRAE A1]
    S --> S3[No Bad Odor]
```

### 4.3 Refrigerant Selection Flowchart

```mermaid
graph TD
    Start[Start Selection Process] --> Q1{Application Type}
    Q1 -->|Domestic AC| Q2{Evaporator Temperature}
    Q1 -->|Industrial Cold| Q3{Low Temperature Required}
    Q1 -->|Auto AC| Q4{Low GWP Required}

    Q2 -->|0 to 10 deg C| R1[R32, R410A]
    Q2 -->|Eco Friendly| R2[R290, R32]

    Q3 -->|Below minus 30 deg C| Q5{Use Cascade}
    Q3 -->|Above minus 30 deg C| R3[R717, R404A, R507]

    Q4 --> Q6{Mildly Flammable Acceptable}
    Q6 -->|Yes| R4[R1234yf, R1234ze]
    Q6 -->|No| R5[R134a with phase down]

    R1 --> End[Selected Refrigerant]
    R2 --> End
    R3 --> End
    R4 --> End
    R5 --> End
```

### 4.4 Leak Detection Methodology Comparison Block

```mermaid
graph TB
    LD[Leak Detection Methods]
    LD --> M1[Visual and Manual]
    LD --> M2[Instrument Based]
    LD --> M3[Tracer Based]

    M1 --> M1a[Soap Bubble Test]
    M1 --> M1b[Halide Torch]
    M1 --> M1c[Oily Residue Inspection]

    M2 --> M2a[Electronic Sniffer]
    M2 --> M2b[Ultrasonic Detector]
    M2 --> M2c[Infrared Spectrometer]
    M2 --> M2d[Pressure Decay Test]

    M3 --> M3a[Fluorescent Dye plus UV]
    M3 --> M3b[Helium Tracer plus Mass Spec]
    M3 --> M3c[Nitrogen Pressure Test]
```

### 4.5 Refrigerant Charging Process Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] Define the term "Refrigerant" and state FOUR desirable properties of an ideal refrigerant.**

**Model Answer:**

A refrigerant is a heat-carrying working fluid used in a refrigeration system that absorbs heat at low temperature (evaporator) and rejects it at high temperature (condenser) by undergoing a phase change.

Four desirable properties of an ideal refrigerant:
1. **Low boiling point** at atmospheric pressure (to operate at desired evaporator temperature).
2. **High latent heat of vaporization** (to maximize refrigeration effect per kg).
3. **Zero Ozone Depletion Potential (ODP)** and **low Global Warming Potential (GWP)**.
4. **Non-toxic, non-flammable**, and chemically stable.

*Allocation: 1 mark for definition + 1 mark for each property (4 × 0.5 = 2 marks) + 1 mark for any additional correct point.*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] What is the difference between ODP and GWP? Why is R-134a preferred over R-12?**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | ODP | GWP |
|-----------|-----|-----|
| Full Form | Ozone Depletion Potential | Global Warming Potential |
| Reference | R-11 (ODP = 1.0) | CO₂ (GWP = 1.0) |
| Impact | Stratospheric ozone destruction | Global climate warming |

**R-134a vs R-12:**
- R-134a has **ODP = 0** (R-12 had ODP = 1.0).
- Although R-134a still has GWP = 1,430, it does not harm the ozone layer.
- R-134a is **non-toxic, non-flammable**, and suitable as a direct replacement in many systems.

*Allocation: 1 mark ODP/GWP distinction + 1 mark table entries + 1 mark R-134a advantages.*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) – [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the various **thermodynamic, physical, chemical, and environmental properties** that a refrigerant should possess for efficient and safe operation in a vapor compression system.

**(b)** [7 Marks] With a neat comparison table, compare **R-12, R-22, R-134a, R-717 (NH₃), and R-744 (CO₂)** based on molecular weight, boiling point, critical temperature, ODP, GWP, and applications.

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A:**

**(a) Desired Properties of a Refrigerant [7 Marks]**

**1. Thermodynamic Properties (2 marks):**
- **Low boiling point** at standard atmospheric pressure (should be lower than the desired evaporator temperature, e.g., –10°C to –40°C).
- **High latent heat of vaporization** – more heat absorbed per kg of refrigerant; reduces mass flow rate.
- **High critical temperature** (above condenser temperature) so condensation occurs at a reasonable pressure.
- **Low specific volume of saturated vapor** at compressor inlet – reduces compressor displacement and hence size.
- **High COP** at given operating temperatures.

**2. Physical Properties (2 marks):**
- **Low freezing point** (must be below the lowest evaporator temperature to avoid solidification).
- **Low viscosity** (better flow, smaller pressure drops in lines and heat exchangers).
- **High thermal conductivity** (efficient heat transfer in evaporator and condenser).
- **Non-corrosive** to metals used in the system (copper, steel, aluminum).
- **High dielectric strength** (mandatory for hermetic compressors).
- **Oil behavior** – either fully miscible (PAG/POE oil systems) or easily separable (NH₃ systems).

**3. Chemical Properties (1.5 marks):**
- **Chemically stable** (no decomposition up to discharge temperatures of 130–150°C).
- **Non-reactive** with moisture, oil, and metals.
- No tendency to polymerize or form sludge.

**4. Environmental & Safety Properties (1.5 marks):**
- **Zero ODP** (no chlorine or bromine in the molecule).
- **Low GWP** (preferably < 150 as per Kigali Amendment).
- **Non-toxic** (ASHRAE Class A preferred for occupied spaces).
- **Non-flammable or low flammability** (A1 preferred; A2L acceptable for new systems).
- Short atmospheric life (reduces long-term climate impact).

---

**(b) Comparison Table of Refrigerants [7 Marks]**

| Property | R-12 (CFC) | R-22 (HCFC) | R-134a (HFC) | R-717 (NH₃) | R-744 (CO₂) |
|----------|------------|-------------|--------------|-------------|-------------|
| Molecular Formula | CCl₂F₂ | CHClF₂ | CH₂FCF₃ | NH₃ | CO₂ |
| Molecular Weight (kg/kmol) | 120.91 | 86.48 | 102.03 | 17.03 | 44.01 |
| Normal Boiling Point (°C) | –29.79 | –40.81 | –26.07 | –33.34 | –78.4 (sublimes) |
| Critical Temperature (°C) | 112.0 | 96.2 | 101.06 | 132.4 | 31.0 |
| ODP | 1.0 | 0.055 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| GWP (100-yr) | 10,900 | 1,810 | 1,430 | 0 | 1 |
| Application | Banned (use in old systems) | AC, freezing (phased out) | Domestic AC, auto AC | Cold storage, ice plants | Cascade, trans-critical AC |
| Safety Class | A1 | A1 | A1 | B2L | A1 |

*Allocation: 0.5 mark per correctly filled property × 6 properties × 5 refrigerants ≈ 6 marks + 1 mark for concluding remark (e.g., R-134a is the current industry standard; R-717 for industrial; R-744 for trans-critical).*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) – [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] What are **mixed refrigerants**? Differentiate between **azeotropic, zeotropic, and near-azeotropic** mixtures with suitable examples. Explain the concept of **temperature glide** and the phenomenon of **fractionation**.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Discuss the various **methods used for refrigerant leak detection** in refrigeration systems. Compare their sensitivity, working principle, and applicability.

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B:**

**(a) Mixed Refrigerants [7 Marks]**

**Definition (1 mark):** A mixed refrigerant is a blend of two or more pure refrigerants combined to obtain properties (boiling point, pressure, glide) superior to those of any individual component.

**Types (3 marks):**

| Type | Definition | Example | Saturation Behavior |
|------|------------|---------|---------------------|
| **Azeotropic** | Constant boiling point mixture; behaves as a single substance | R-502 (R-22 + R-115), R-507, R-410A | One saturation temperature at given pressure |
| **Zeotropic** | Exhibits "glide" – temperature changes during phase change | R-407C (R-32/R-125/R-134a), R-404A | Bubble point ≠ Dew point |
| **Near-Azeotropic** | Small glide (< 1°C) | R-401A, R-401B | Nearly single saturation T |

**Temperature Glide (1.5 marks):**

$$\Delta T_{glide} = T_{dew} - T_{bubble}$$

For R-407C, glide ≈ 7°C; for R-404A, glide ≈ 0.5°C; for R-410A, glide ≈ 0.1°C (near-azeotropic).

**Fractionation (1.5 marks):**

Zeotropic blends may undergo **composition shift** when refrigerant leaks. Since components have different volatilities, the more volatile component leaks faster, changing the blend ratio and degrading performance.

**Practical Implication:** Always charge zeotropic blends as **liquid** from an upright cylinder to ensure uniform composition.

---

**(b) Refrigerant Leak Detection Methods [7 Marks]**

| Method | Working Principle | Sensitivity | Best Use |
|--------|-------------------|-------------|----------|
| **Soap Bubble Test** | Soapy water applied; bubbles form at leak | Visible leaks only | Field quick check |
| **Electronic Sniffer** | Heated sensor detects refrigerant in air | 3 g/year | Routine maintenance |
| **Halide Torch** | Copper ring flame turns green with halogens | Moderate | Old CFC systems |
| **Ultrasonic Detector** | Picks up high-frequency hiss of escaping gas | High | Pressurized gas systems |
| **Fluorescent Dye (UV)** | Dye added; leaks glow under UV light | Moderate | Oil systems |
| **Pressure Decay Test** | Isolate and monitor pressure drop | Very high | New installations |
| **Mass Spectrometer** | Detects tracer gas (He) | Ultra-high | R&D, factory |
| **IR Spectroscopy** | Detects C-F bond absorption | High | Modern HFC/HFO |

*Allocation: 0.5 mark per correct entry × 8 rows = 4 marks + 1 mark for sensitivity comparison + 1 mark for application discussion + 1 mark for concluding statement (e.g., ASHRAE Std 147 mandates < 3 g/yr leakage for hermetic systems).*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning – Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing ODP and GWP:** Students often write the wrong reference (R-12 vs R-11 for ODP, methane vs CO₂ for GWP). Remember: **ODP reference = R-11; GWP reference = CO₂**.
> 2. **Forgetting units:** Latent heat in **kJ/kg** (not kJ), pressure in **MPa or kPa** (not just bar without conversion), ODP/GWP are **dimensionless**.
> 3. **Skipping safety class:** KTU examiners expect **ASHRAE A1/A2L/B2L** classification explicitly mentioned.
> 4. **Ignoring fractionation in blends:** Many students charge blends as vapor, causing composition shift. This is a **favourite 2-mark question**.
> 5. **Mixing up CFCs vs HFCs vs HFOs:** Note the chemical difference: CFCs contain chlorine (high ODP); HFCs have no chlorine (zero ODP but high GWP); HFOs have a double bond (low GWP).
> 6. **Charging zeotropes:** Always specify "charge as liquid from upright cylinder" – examiners reward this.
> 7. **Diagram labels:** In leak detection comparison, label axes/units clearly; vague answers lose 1–2 marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- **Refrigerant** = working fluid that undergoes phase change to transport heat.
- **Ideal refrigerant** needs: low BP, high latent heat, zero ODP, low GWP, non-toxic, non-flammable (A1 class), oil-compatible.
- **ODP** reference: R-11 (ODP = 1.0); **GWP** reference: CO₂ (GWP = 1.0).
- **CFC (R-12, R-502):** Banned due to high ODP; phased out by Montreal Protocol (1987).
- **HCFC (R-22):** Phased out; intermediate ODP = 0.055.
- **HFC (R-134a, R-32, R-410A):** Zero ODP but high GWP; under HFC phase-down (Kigali Amendment, 2016).
- **HFO (R-1234yf, R-1234ze):** Ultra-low GWP, mildly flammable (A2L).
- **Natural refrigerants:** R-717 (NH₃), R-744 (CO₂), R-290 (propane), R-600a (isobutane) – all zero ODP, ultra-low GWP.
- **Azeotropic blends** = R-502, R-507, R-410A (no glide).
- **Zeotropic blends** = R-407C, R-404A (with glide, fractionation risk).
- **Temperature glide** = $T_{dew} - T_{bubble}$; improves counterflow heat exchanger effectiveness.
- **Charging rule for blends:** Always **liquid-phase, upright cylinder** to prevent composition shift.
- **ASHRAE 34 Safety Classification:** A = lower toxicity, B = higher toxicity; 1 = non-flammable, 2L = mild flammability, 3 = high flammability.
- **Leak detection hierarchy:** Visual → soap bubble → electronic sniffer → ultrasonic → pressure decay → mass spec (for lab).
- **ASHRAE Std 147 limit:** Leakage < 3 g/year for hermetic systems; < 10% of charge/year for others.
- **Charging method:** Use digital scale; verify superheat (capillary systems) or subcooling (TXV systems).
- **Superheat** = $T_{actual\,suction} - T_{saturation}\,(P_{evap})$.
- **Subcooling** = $T_{saturation}\,(P_{cond}) - T_{liquid\,outlet}$.
- **TEWI** = direct emissions (leaks) + indirect emissions (energy use).
- **TEWI reduction strategy:** Lower GWP refrigerants + reduced leakage + higher system efficiency.
- **Why NH₃ is preferred industrially:** High latent heat (1,377 kJ/kg), zero ODP, zero GWP, low cost. Drawback: toxic (B2L).
- **Why CO₂ in trans-critical:** Excellent heat transfer, low pressure drop, suited for hot climates; challenge: very high operating pressure (~100 bar).
- **R-290 vs R-600a:** Both are hydrocarbons; R-290 used in some fridges; R-600a dominates domestic refrigeration due to lower flammability risk (lower vapor density).
- **Key exam tip:** Always state the **ASHRAE safety class** when comparing refrigerants – this is a recurring 1-mark item.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Properties of Air (Saturated, Unsaturated, Dew Point, Humidity Levels, Thermodynamic Equations, Adiabatic Saturation Process, Psychrometric Charts)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET631 - REFRIGERATION AND AIR CONDITIONING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Properties of Air (Saturated, Unsaturated, Dew Point, Humidity Levels, Thermodynamic Equations, Adiabatic Saturation Process, Psychrometric Charts)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Psychrometrics

**Psychrometrics** is the science that deals with the determination of the thermodynamic properties of mixtures of air and water vapor (commonly referred to as **moist air** or **psychrometric air**). It is the foundational bedrock of Refrigeration & Air Conditioning (RAC) engineering because the air we condition in an Air Handling Unit (AHU) is never purely dry — it always carries a certain quantity of invisible water vapor.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *"Psychrometrics is the study of the physical and thermodynamic properties of a mixture of dry air and water vapor under varying conditions of temperature, pressure, and humidity."*

In KTU terminology, the air we breathe and condition is treated as a **binary mixture of two ideal gases**:
1. **Dry Air (D.A.)** — A mechanical mixture of Nitrogen (≈ 78.1%), Oxygen (≈ 20.9%), Argon (≈ 0.93%), and trace gases like CO₂, Neon, Helium, etc. The molecular weight of dry air is taken as **M_da = 28.97 kg/kg-mol**.
2. **Water Vapor (W.V.)** — A condensable vapor. Its molecular weight is **M_w = 18.015 kg/kg-mol**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Assumption in KTU Textbook:** Both dry air and water vapor are treated as ideal gases, and they follow **Dalton's Law of Partial Pressures**:
> $$P_t = P_a + P_v$$
> where $P_t$ is the total pressure, $P_a$ is the partial pressure of dry air, and $P_v$ is the partial pressure of water vapor.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Sponge" Model of Moist Air

Imagine the dry air is a **dry, porous sponge** and water vapor is the **water trapped inside its pores**.
- When the sponge is **saturated**, you cannot squeeze any more water into it — the pores are full.
- The air is said to be **saturated** at a particular temperature when it holds the *maximum possible water vapor* it can carry.
- The amount of water the sponge (air) can hold **increases with temperature**. A hot sponge (hot air) can hold more water than a cold sponge.
- If you cool a saturated sponge (saturated air), water will drip out — exactly like the **dew forming on a cold water bottle** on a humid day. The temperature at which this "dripping" begins is the **Dew Point Temperature (DPT)**.

> [!TIP]
> This sponge analogy will help you visualize almost every psychrometric concept — saturation, supersaturation, dew point, relative humidity, and even the psychrometric chart itself.

## 1.3 Why Study Psychrometrics?

Every human comfort metric — **Effective Temperature, Mean Radiant Temperature, Air Movement, and Humidity** — is governed by psychrometric relationships. Design of cooling coils, humidifiers, dehumidifiers, evaporative coolers, and ventilation systems all rely on these properties. KTU examiners love this module because it bridges pure theory with practical design.

## 1.4 Standard Reference Conditions (KTU Convention)

| Parameter | Standard Value |
|---|---|
| Total Atmospheric Pressure | **101.325 kPa (1 atm)** |
| Standard DBT Reference | **0 °C (273.15 K)** for gas constant calculations |
| Universal Gas Constant | **R_u = 8.314 kJ/kg-mol·K** |
| Gas Constant for Dry Air | **R_a = 0.287 kJ/kg·K** |
| Gas Constant for Water Vapor | **R_v = 0.4615 kJ/kg·K** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Properties of Moist Air

### 2.1.1 Saturated Air
**Saturated air** is moist air in which the water vapor is in **thermodynamic equilibrium** with its condensed phase (liquid water) at the given temperature and pressure. At this state, the partial pressure of vapor equals the **saturation pressure of water ($P_s$ or $P_g$)** at the given DBT.

### 2.1.2 Unsaturated Air
**Unsaturated air** (the most common state in real life) is moist air where the partial pressure of water vapor ($P_v$) is **less than** the saturation pressure ($P_s$) at the same DBT. Such air can still absorb more moisture.

> [!NOTE]
> **Supersaturated air** is an unstable state where $P_v > P_s$. It rarely occurs naturally and is not a KTU exam focus, but it exists in the form of fog/mist.

### 2.1.3 Dew Point Temperature (DPT) — $T_{dp}$
The **Dew Point Temperature** is defined as the temperature at which the water vapor in moist air becomes saturated (begins to condense) when the air is cooled at constant pressure and constant water-vapor content. In other words, the DPT is the saturation temperature corresponding to the existing partial pressure of water vapor $P_v$.

$$
T_{dp} \;\Longleftrightarrow\; P_{sat}(T_{dp}) = P_v
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The DPT depends **only on the moisture content (humidity ratio)**, NOT on the DBT. If $P_v$ is constant, $T_{dp}$ is constant. This is a key KTU conceptual question.

### 2.1.4 Humidity Ratio / Specific Humidity — $\omega$ (kg water / kg dry air)
The mass of water vapor associated with **each kilogram of dry air**. It is the most important KTU quantity for AC load calculations.

$$
\omega \;=\; \frac{m_v}{m_a} \;=\; 0.622 \cdot \frac{P_v}{P_t - P_v}
$$

where the factor **0.622** comes from the ratio of molecular weights $\frac{M_w}{M_{da}} = \frac{18.015}{28.97} \approx 0.622$.

### 2.1.5 Relative Humidity — $\phi$ (dimensionless, 0 to 1)
The ratio of the actual partial pressure of water vapor to the saturation pressure of water at the **same DBT**.

$$
\phi \;=\; \frac{P_v}{P_s} \;\;\Big(\text{at the same DBT}\Big)
$$

Sometimes expressed as a percentage: $\%\phi = \phi \times 100$.

### 2.1.6 Degree of Saturation — $\mu$
The ratio of the actual humidity ratio to the saturation humidity ratio at the same DBT.

$$
\mu \;=\; \frac{\omega}{\omega_s}
$$

### 2.1.7 Specific Volume (Volumetric) — $v$ (m³/kg dry air)
Volume occupied by 1 kg of dry air plus its associated water vapor.

$$
v \;=\; \frac{R_a T_{db}}{P_a} \;=\; \frac{R_a T_{db}}{P_t - P_v}
$$

### 2.1.8 Enthalpy of Moist Air — $h$ (kJ/kg dry air)
Per kg of dry air, the enthalpy is the sum of dry air enthalpy + vapor enthalpy. KTU standard reference is 0 °C.

$$
h \;=\; c_{pa} \cdot T_{db} \;+\; \omega \cdot \big(h_{fg,0} + c_{pv} \cdot T_{db}\big)
$$

where $c_{pa} = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K, $c_{pv} = 1.88$ kJ/kg·K, and $h_{fg,0} = 2501$ kJ/kg.

### 2.1.9 Wet Bulb Temperature (WBT) — $T_{wb}$
The temperature a parcel of air attains when it is adiabatically saturated by the evaporation of water into it. It is the *temperature read by a thermometer whose bulb is covered by a wet wick* in a strong, rapid airstream.

## 2.2 Adiabatic Saturation Process (Theoretical Foundation)

The **Adiabatic Saturation** is an idealized process in which:
- A stream of unsaturated air (state 1) comes in contact with a large body of recirculated water.
- Water evaporates into the air, increasing its humidity.
- The process is **adiabatic (Q = 0)** and at **steady-state (constant mass flow)**.
- The air leaves the system in a **saturated state (state 2)** at the **wet bulb temperature** $T_2 = T_{wb}$.
- The make-up water enters at $T_2$, so the water reaching the air is already at the WBT.

**Governing Energy Equation (per kg dry air):**

$$
h_1 + (\omega_2 - \omega_1) \cdot h_{f2} \;=\; h_2
$$

Rearranging to obtain the adiabatic saturation temperature $T_2$ (which is also WBT in this context):

$$
\omega_1 \;=\; \frac{c_{ps}(T_2 - T_1) + \omega_2 \cdot h_{fg2}}{h_{fg2}}
$$

where $c_{ps} = c_{pa} + \omega_1 \cdot c_{pv} \approx 1.09$ kJ/kg·K (humid specific heat approximation).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Special Note:** The numerical WBT obtained from the *adiabatic saturation equation* is technically called the **Thermodynamic Wet Bulb Temperature (TWBT)**. The WBT measured by a sling psychrometer is *very close* to TWBT for air-water mixtures, so KTU uses them interchangeably.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are KTU 2024 exam-ready.** Use $\vert$ instead of \vert for absolute values to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Property | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Dalton's Law | $P_t = P_a + P_v$ | kPa | Both gases treated as ideal |
| 2 | Humidity Ratio | $\omega = 0.622 \cdot \dfrac{P_v}{P_t - P_v}$ | kg/kg da | Most important quantity |
| 3 | Saturation Humidity Ratio | $\omega_s = 0.622 \cdot \dfrac{P_s}{P_t - P_s}$ | kg/kg da | At DBT |
| 4 | Relative Humidity | $\phi = \dfrac{P_v}{P_s}$ | — | At given DBT |
| 5 | Degree of Saturation | $\mu = \dfrac{\omega}{\omega_s}$ | — | $\leq 1$ for stable air |
| 6 | Dew Point | $P_v = P_{sat}(T_{dp})$ | °C | $T_{dp} \leq T_{wb} \leq T_{db}$ |
| 7 | Specific Volume | $v = \dfrac{R_a T_{db}}{P_t - P_v}$ | m³/kg da | At 1 atm: $v \approx 0.287 T_{db}/(P_t - P_v)$ |
| 8 | Enthalpy | $h = 1.005\,T_{db} + \omega(2501 + 1.88\,T_{db})$ | kJ/kg da | Reference 0 °C |
| 9 | Adiabatic Saturation | $\omega_1 = \dfrac{c_{ps}(T_2 - T_1) + \omega_2 h_{fg2}}{h_{fg2}}$ | kg/kg da | $T_2 = T_{wb}$ |
| 10 | Moist Air Density | $\rho = \dfrac{1}{v} = \dfrac{P_t - P_v}{R_a T_{db}}$ | kg/m³ | Inverse of $v$ |
| 11 | Humid Heat | $c_{ps} = c_{pa} + \omega \cdot c_{pv}$ | kJ/kg·K | $\approx 1.005 + 1.88\omega$ |
| 12 | Mole Fraction (vapor) | $y_v = \dfrac{P_v}{P_t} = \dfrac{\omega}{0.622 + \omega}$ | — | Dalton's Law application |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Order Trick (Favorites of Examiners):** For unsaturated air at a given DBT, the strict ordering is:
> $$T_{dp} \;\leq\; T_{wb} \;\leq\; T_{db}$$
> At saturation, all three become equal.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

These properties are the heartbeat of:
- **AHU Coil Selection** (Sensible Heat Factor, Apparatus Dew Point).
- **Cooling Tower Design** (range, approach).
- **Duct Sizing** (using specific volume at fan outlet).
- **Industrial Drying** (using wet bulb depression as driving potential).
- **Green Building Design** (psychrometric process path optimization).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Humidity Ratio $\omega$

We start with the ideal gas law applied separately to dry air and water vapor (Dalton's Law).

**Step 1 — Apply ideal gas law to dry air and vapor separately.**

$$
P_a \cdot V \;=\; m_a R_a T \qquad \text{and} \qquad P_v \cdot V \;=\; m_v R_v T
$$

Here $V$ is the total volume of the mixture and $T$ is the common absolute temperature.

**Step 2 — Express the masses in terms of $P, V, T, R$.**

$$
m_a \;=\; \frac{P_a V}{R_a T} \qquad \text{and} \qquad m_v \;=\; \frac{P_v V}{R_v T}
$$

**Step 3 — Form the ratio $m_v / m_a$.**

$$
\frac{m_v}{m_a} \;=\; \frac{P_v / R_v}{P_a / R_a} \;=\; \frac{P_v}{P_a} \cdot \frac{R_a}{R_v}
$$

**Step 4 — Introduce the universal gas constant $R_u$.**

Since $R_a = R_u / M_{da}$ and $R_v = R_u / M_w$:

$$
\frac{R_a}{R_v} \;=\; \frac{R_u / M_{da}}{R_u / M_w} \;=\; \frac{M_w}{M_{da}} \;=\; \frac{18.015}{28.97} \;\approx\; 0.622
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute and use $P_a = P_t - P_v$.**

$$
\omega \;=\; \frac{m_v}{m_a} \;=\; 0.622 \cdot \frac{P_v}{P_a} \;=\; 0.622 \cdot \frac{P_v}{P_t - P_v}
$$

This is the **master KTU equation** for humidity ratio.

## 3.2 Derivation of Enthalpy of Moist Air

**Step 1 — Define the basis.** Choose 1 kg of dry air + $\omega$ kg of water vapor as the basis. Reference state: dry air and liquid water at 0 °C.

**Step 2 — Dry air contribution.**
Dry air needs to be heated from 0 °C to $T_{db}$ °C. With $c_{pa} = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K:

$$
h_{da} \;=\; c_{pa} \cdot T_{db} \;=\; 1.005 \cdot T_{db} \;\; \text{kJ/kg da}
$$

**Step 3 — Water vapor contribution.**
The water vapor at $T_{db}$ is treated as a saturated vapor at 0 °C (with $h_g = h_{fg,0} = 2501$ kJ/kg) heated to $T_{db}$ using $c_{pv} = 1.88$ kJ/kg·K:

$$
h_{vap} \;=\; \omega \cdot \big( h_{fg,0} + c_{pv} \cdot T_{db} \big) \;=\; \omega \cdot (2501 + 1.88\,T_{db})
$$

**Step 4 — Sum the two contributions.**

$$
h \;=\; h_{da} + h_{vap} \;=\; 1.005 \cdot T_{db} + \omega \cdot (2501 + 1.88 \cdot T_{db})
$$

## 3.3 Derivation of Specific Volume of Moist Air

**Step 1 — Use ideal gas law for dry air only (the standard KTU definition of $v$).**

$$
P_a \cdot V \;=\; m_a R_a T
$$

For 1 kg of dry air, $m_a = 1$:

$$
V \;=\; \frac{R_a \cdot T_{db}}{P_a} \;=\; \frac{R_a \cdot T_{db}}{P_t - P_v}
$$

**Step 2 — Insert $R_a = 0.287$ kJ/kg·K.**

$$
v \;=\; \frac{0.287 \cdot T_{db}}{P_t - P_v} \;\; \text{m}^3/\text{kg da}
$$

## 3.4 Derivation of the Adiabatic Saturation Equation

This is the **most important KTU derivation** for Module 3.

**Setup (Control Volume):** Unsaturated air at state 1 ($\omega_1, T_1, h_1$) enters, water at $T_2$ (already cooled to WBT) is added, and saturated air at state 2 ($\omega_2, T_2, h_2$) leaves. Make-up water enters at $T_2$.

**Mass Balance on Water Vapor (per kg dry air):**

$$
\omega_1 + \dot{m}_w \cdot 1 \;=\; \omega_2
$$

But the make-up water carries **liquid water** into the control volume, not vapor. So the water mass flow (liquid) is:

$$
\dot{m}_w \;=\; \omega_2 - \omega_1
$$

**Energy Balance (Steady State, $Q = 0$):**

$$
h_1 + (\omega_2 - \omega_1) \cdot h_{f2} \;=\; h_2
$$

The water enters at state 2 (already at WBT), so its enthalpy is the saturated liquid enthalpy $h_{f2}$ at $T_2$.

**Rearrange to solve for $\omega_1$:**

$$
h_1 - h_2 \;=\; (\omega_1 - \omega_2) \cdot h_{f2}
$$

$$
\omega_1 \;=\; \omega_2 + \frac{h_1 - h_2}{h_{f2}}
$$

**Substitute $h_1 = c_{pa} T_1 + \omega_1(h_{fg,0} + c_{pv} T_1)$ and $h_2 = c_{pa} T_2 + \omega_2(h_{fg,0} + c_{pv} T_2)$:**

After algebraic simplification and approximating $h_{fg,0} \approx h_{fg2}$:

$$
\omega_1 \;=\; \frac{c_{pa}(T_2 - T_1) + \omega_2 \cdot h_{fg2}}{h_{fg2} - c_{pv}(T_2 - T_1)}
$$

If we further define the **humid specific heat** $c_{ps} = c_{pa} + \omega_1 c_{pv}$ and recognize the small $c_{pv}(T_2 - T_1)$ term is often dropped for hand calculation:

$$
\omega_1 \;\approx\; \frac{c_{ps}(T_2 - T_1) + \omega_2 h_{fg2}}{h_{fg2}}
$$

**Convergence Note (KTU Trick):** Because $\omega_1$ appears on *both sides* (inside $c_{ps}$), you must use **iterative / trial-and-error** to solve for $T_2 = T_{wb}$. The standard method is:
1. Guess $T_2$.
2. Calculate $\omega_2$ (saturation humidity ratio at $T_2$) and $h_{fg2}$.
3. Calculate $\omega_1$ from the right-hand side.
4. Compare with given $\omega_1$. If equal, $T_2 = T_{wb}$. If not, revise $T_2$ and repeat.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Identifying the Type of Air State

| State Description | Mathematical Condition | Process |
|---|---|---|
| Dry Air (no vapor) | $\omega = 0,\; P_v = 0$ | Pure dry air |
| Unsaturated | $\omega < \omega_s,\; P_v < P_s$ | Can absorb moisture |
| Saturated | $\omega = \omega_s,\; P_v = P_s$ | In equilibrium with water |
| Supersaturated (unstable) | $\omega > \omega_s,\; P_v > P_s$ | Fog/Mist/Condensation |
| Dew Point Reached | $T = T_{dp}$ | Onset of condensation |

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** Moist air at 1 atm total pressure has DBT = 30 °C and relative humidity 60%. Calculate $\omega$, $v$, $h$, and $T_{dp}$.

**Given Data (from steam tables at 30 °C):** $P_s = 4.246$ kPa, $h_{fg} = 2430.5$ kJ/kg, $c_{pv} = 1.88$ kJ/kg·K.

**Step 1 — Partial pressure of vapor.**

$$
P_v \;=\; \phi \cdot P_s \;=\; 0.60 \cdot 4.246 \;=\; 2.548 \;\text{kPa}
$$

**Step 2 — Humidity ratio.**

$$
\omega \;=\; 0.622 \cdot \frac{2.548}{101.325 - 2.548} \;=\; 0.622 \cdot \frac{2.548}{98.777} \;=\; 0.01605 \;\text{kg/kg da}
$$

**Step 3 — Specific volume.**

$$
v \;=\; \frac{0.287 \cdot (30 + 273.15)}{101.325 - 2.548} \;=\; \frac{86.96}{98.777} \;=\; 0.8804 \;\text{m}^3/\text{kg da}
$$

**Step 4 — Enthalpy.**

$$
h \;=\; 1.005 \cdot 30 + 0.01605 \cdot (2501 + 1.88 \cdot 30)
$$

$$
h \;=\; 30.15 + 0.01605 \cdot (2501 + 56.4) \;=\; 30.15 + 0.01605 \cdot 2557.4
$$

$$
h \;=\; 30.15 + 41.04 \;=\; 71.19 \;\text{kJ/kg da}
$$

**Step 5 — Dew Point Temperature.** Find $T_{dp}$ from steam tables such that $P_{sat}(T_{dp}) = 2.548$ kPa. From tables, $T_{dp} \approx 21.1$ °C.

> [!TIP]
> **Verification:** $T_{dp}$ (21.1 °C) < $T_{db}$ (30 °C), confirming the air is unsaturated. ✔

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Psychrometric Properties Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Moist Air Binary Mixture] --> B[Dry Air: 78% N2 + 21% O2 + 1% other gases]
    A --> C[Water Vapor: H2O gaseous phase]
    A --> D[Total Pressure Pt = Pa + Pv]
    D --> E[Pt - Pv = Pa]
    C --> F[Properties of Vapor]
    F --> F1[Partial Pressure Pv]
    F --> F2[Saturation Pressure Ps at DBT]
    F --> F3[Relative Humidity phi = Pv / Ps]
    F --> F4[Dew Point Tdp: Ps_Tdp = Pv]
    A --> G[Properties of Mixture]
    G --> G1[Humidity Ratio omega = 0.622 Pv / Pt - Pv]
    G --> G2[Degree of Saturation mu = omega / omega_s]
    G --> G3[Specific Volume v per kg da]
    G --> G4[Enthalpy h per kg da]
    G --> G5[Wet Bulb Temperature Twb from adiabatic sat]
    A --> H[State Classification]
    H --> H1[Unsaturated: Pv less than Ps]
    H --> H2[Saturated: Pv equals Ps]
    H --> H3[Supersaturated: Pv greater than Ps]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Adiabatic Saturation Process Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    INLET[Unsaturated Air State 1: Tdb1, omega1, h1] --> MIX[Adiabatic Saturation Chamber]
    WATER[Make-up Water at T2] --> MIX
    MIX --> OUTLET[Saturated Air State 2: Tdb2 equals Twb, omega2, h2]
    MIX --> REC[Recirculated Water leaves at T2]
    REC --> COOL[Cooling Coil or Spray]
    COOL --> WATER
    Q1[Heat Transfer Q equals 0 adiabatic] -.-> MIX
    ST1[Energy Balance: h1 + omega2-omega1 hf2 equals h2] -.-> MIX
    ST2[Mass Balance: omega1 plus mw equals omega2] -.-> MIX
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — How the Properties Interact in an AHU

```mermaid
graph TD
    RA[Return Air from Room] --> FB[Filter Bank]
    FB --> CC1[Cooling Coil]
    CC1 --> HM1[Humidifier Optional]
    HM1 --> FAN[Supply Fan]
    FAN --> SA[Supply Air to Room]
    CC1 --> COND[Condensate Drains out]
    PROP1[Properties at RA: Tdb, Twb, phi, omega, h] -.-> RA
    PROP2[Properties at SA: Tdb, Twb, phi, omega, h] -.-> SA
    CHART[Psy chart lookup or equations] -.-> PROP1
    CHART -.-> PROP2
    PROP1 --> CALC1[Load Calculation SHL + LHL]
    PROP2 --> CALC1
    COIL[ADP from coil bypass factor] -.-> CC1
```

## 4.4 Schematic Table — Relative Positions of the Three Temperatures

| State of Air | $T_{dp}$ | $T_{wb}$ | $T_{db}$ | Visualization |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Unsaturated | Lowest | Middle | Highest | $T_{dp} < T_{wb} < T_{db}$ |
| Saturated | Equal | Equal | Equal | $T_{dp} = T_{wb} = T_{db}$ |
| Wet Bulb Sling Reading | WBT = evaporative equilibrium temp | — | — | Wet wick in fast airstream |

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram — The Psychrometric Chart Layout

```mermaid
graph TB
    CHART[Psy Chart 1 atm 0 to 50 C DBT] --> XAXIS[Horizontal Axis: DBT in C]
    CHART --> YAXIS[Vertical Axis: Humidity Ratio omega in kg per kg da]
    CHART --> L1[Curves: Constant Relative Humidity phi 10 percent to 100 percent]
    CHART --> L2[Curves: Constant Wet Bulb T wb sloping up-right]
    CHART --> L3[Curves: Constant Enthalpy h sloping up-right]
    CHART --> L4[Curves: Constant Specific Volume v sloping up-right]
    CHART --> SAT[Saturation Curve at phi 100 percent, upper boundary]
    SAT --> TDP[Dew Point Tdp read by going down vertically to saturation curve]
    CHART --> PROC[Processes: Heating, Cooling, Humidification, Dehumidification, Mixing, Evaporative Cooling]
```
