# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question Bank]**

**Define ventilation. List any four objectives of ventilation in a conditioned space.**

**Model Answer:**

**Ventilation** is the process of supplying outdoor air to and removing indoor air from a space to control air contaminants, humidity, and temperature, as defined by **ASHRAE Standard 62.1**.

**Four Objectives:**
1. **Oxygen replenishment** — maintaining O₂ above 19.5 % for occupant health.
2. **Dilution of bio-effluents** — keeping CO₂ below **1000 ppm**.
3. **Removal of indoor pollutants** — VOCs, odors, particulates.
4. **Control of humidity** — preventing microbial growth and condensation.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Question Bank]**

**Explain the role of relative humidity in controlling microbial growth inside HVAC systems.**

**Model Answer:**

Relative Humidity (RH) directly governs **water activity ($a_w$)**, the primary driver of microbial metabolism. Most bacteria require $a_w > 0.91$, equivalent to RH > **91 %** at room temperature. Mold species (*Aspergillus*, *Penicillium*) can germinate at RH > **75 %**, especially on cooling coils and in drain pans.

Conversely, **viral survivability** is enhanced at RH < **40 %** because the hygroscopic aerosol particles remain dry and intact.

Therefore, maintaining RH in the **40–60 %** band — the ASHRAE comfort zone — simultaneously suppresses bacterial growth (by keeping $a_w$ low) and viral transmission (by ensuring aerosols absorb moisture and settle faster).

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper Pattern]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

A conference hall of floor area **400 m²** and ceiling height **3.5 m** has a maximum occupancy of **150 people**. Using ASHRAE 62.1 Ventilation Rate Procedure:

- **People-related outdoor air rate** = 8.0 L/s·person
- **Area-related outdoor air rate** = 1.2 L/s·m²
- **Zone distribution effectiveness** $E_z = 1.0$
- **System ventilation efficiency** $E_v = 0.9$

**Calculate:**
1. The breathing-zone outdoor airflow $V_{bz}$.
2. The system-level outdoor airflow $V_{ot}$.
3. The required **ACH** if the system supplies only outdoor air (no recirculation).

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — People-related outdoor air rate:**

$$R_p = P \times R_{per\,person} = 150 \times 8.0 = 1200 \text{ L/s}$$

**Step 2 — Area-related outdoor air rate:**

$$R_{bz} = A_z \times R_{area} = 400 \times 1.2 = 480 \text{ L/s}$$

**Step 3 — Breathing-zone outdoor air rate:**

$$V_{bz} = R_p + R_{bz} = 1200 + 480 = 1680 \text{ L/s}$$

**Step 4 — System outdoor air rate:**

$$V_{ot} = \frac{V_{bz}}{E_v} = \frac{1680}{0.9} = 1866.67 \text{ L/s}$$

Converting to m³/s: $V_{ot} = 1.867$ m³/s.

**Step 5 — Room volume:**

$$V_{room} = 400 \times 3.5 = 1400 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 6 — ACH (no recirculation, $Q_v = V_{ot}$):**

$$ACH = \frac{V_{ot} \times 3600 / 1000}{V_{room}} = \frac{1.867 \times 3600}{1400}$$

$$ACH = \frac{6721.2}{1400} = 4.80 \text{ hr}^{-1}$$

**Final Answers:**
- $V_{bz} = 1680$ L/s
- $V_{ot} = 1866.7$ L/s = **1.867 m³/s**
- ACH ≈ **4.8 hr⁻¹**

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Discuss with a neat diagram how relative humidity affects (i) microbial growth, (ii) human comfort, and (iii) building structure in a ventilation system. State the ASHRAE-recommended RH range and justify.**

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction:**
Relative Humidity (RH) is the ratio of actual water vapor partial pressure to saturation pressure at the same dry-bulb temperature. RH critically influences IAQ.

**(i) Effect on Microbial Growth:**

- Bacteria: optimal growth at **RH > 70 %** (high water activity).
- Mold (*Aspergillus*, *Cladosporium*): germinate at **RH > 75 %** on coil surfaces.
- Viruses (e.g., influenza): survive longer in **RH < 40 %** (dry aerosols).
- **Sweet spot: RH 40–60 %** suppresses both bacteria and viruses.

**(ii) Effect on Human Comfort:**

- **Low RH (< 30 %):** dry skin, eye irritation, electrostatic shocks.
- **High RH (> 70 %):** perceived air becomes "sticky," sweat evaporation is impaired, leading to thermal discomfort.
- **Optimal:** ASHRAE 55 recommends RH between **30–60 %** at 25 °C.

**(iii) Effect on Building Structure:**

- **High RH** → condensation on cool surfaces → paint peeling, wood rot, corrosion of ducts.
- **Condensation cycles** → mold on walls, ceiling tiles, carpets.
- **Low RH** → wood shrinkage, cracking of timber furniture.

**ASHRAE Recommendation:**

$$RH_{recommended} = 40\% - 60\%$$

**Justification:** This range minimizes microbial proliferation, ensures thermal comfort, prevents condensation at typical indoor surface temperatures, and reduces viral aerosol persistence.

**Diagram:** Refer to the **RH Effect Spectrum** diagram in SECTION 4.4.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Alternative Choice Pattern]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

A classroom of volume **1200 m³** has 60 occupants. The indoor CO₂ setpoint is **900 ppm** and outdoor CO₂ is **420 ppm**. The CO₂ generation rate per person is **0.31 L/min**.

**Calculate:**
1. The ventilation rate $Q_v$ required to maintain 900 ppm using the CO₂ balance method.
2. The ACH achieved.
3. The time needed to reduce CO₂ from an initial 1500 ppm to 900 ppm (assuming no further generation, outdoor air remains at 420 ppm).

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Total CO₂ generation rate:**

$$G = 60 \times 0.31 = 18.6 \text{ L/min}$$

Convert to m³/s:

$$G = \frac{18.6}{60000} = 3.10 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 2 — Required ventilation rate:**

$$Q_v = \frac{G}{C_{set} - C_{out}} = \frac{3.10 \times 10^{-4}}{(900 - 420) \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$Q_v = \frac{3.10 \times 10^{-4}}{4.80 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.6458 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

In L/s: $Q_v = 645.8$ L/s.

**Step 3 — ACH:**

$$ACH = \frac{Q_v \times 3600}{V_{room}} = \frac{0.6458 \times 3600}{1200} = 1.94 \text{ hr}^{-1}$$

> Note: This is low ACH because the room has only 60 occupants in a large volume. A typical classroom with $ACH = 6$ would supply ~2000 L/s, well above the CO₂ demand — illustrating that **CO₂ demand may not govern** in low-density spaces.

**Step 4 — Purge time:**

Using $k_{decay} = Q_v / V = 0.6458 / 1200 = 5.38 \times 10^{-4}$ s⁻¹.

Using the transient equation:

$$C(t) = C_{out} + (C_{ini} - C_{out}) \cdot e^{-k \cdot t}$$

Setting $C(t) = 900$ ppm, $C_{ini} = 1500$ ppm:

$$900 = 420 + (1500 - 420) \cdot e^{-5.38 \times 10^{-4} \cdot t}$$

$$\frac{900 - 420}{1500 - 420} = e^{-5.38 \times 10^{-4} \cdot t}$$

$$\frac{480}{1080} = e^{-5.38 \times 10^{-4} \cdot t}$$

$$0.4444 = e^{-5.38 \times 10^{-4} \cdot t}$$

Take natural log:

$$\ln(0.4444) = -5.38 \times 10^{-4} \cdot t$$

$$-0.8109 = -5.38 \times 10^{-4} \cdot t$$

$$t = \frac{0.8109}{5.38 \times 10^{-4}} = 1507 \text{ seconds} \approx 25.1 \text{ minutes}$$

**Final Answers:**
- $Q_v = 0.646$ m³/s = **646 L/s**
- ACH = **1.94 hr⁻¹**
- Purge time ≈ **25 minutes**

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Explain the strategies for controlling microbial growth in HVAC systems. List the four zones in an HVAC system that are most prone to microbial contamination and the methods to mitigate each.**

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction:**
Microbial contamination (bacteria, mold, viruses) in HVAC systems poses **Legionnaires' disease**, **hypersensitivity pneumonitis**, and **sick-building syndrome** risks. ASHRAE 188 mandates a structured **Water Management Program**.

**Four Critical Microbial Zones:**

| Zone | Microbial Risk | Mitigation Strategy |
|------|----------------|---------------------|
| **1. Cooling Coils & Drain Pans** | *Legionella*, *Pseudomonas* in condensate | Slope drain pans ≥ 1 %; biocide treatment; UV-C lamps in plenum |
| **2. Humidifiers (Adiabatic)** | Aerosolized bacteria & algae | Reverse-osmosis water; UV sterilization; periodic descaling |
| **3. Ductwork & Insulation** | Mold (*Aspergillus*) on internal lining | Seamless GI ducts; proper insulation vapor barrier; NADCA cleaning every 3–5 years |
| **4. Filters** | Loaded filters harbor pathogens | Replace at Δp limit; use MERV 13+ where IAQ critical |

**Three-Stage Control Approach:**

1. **Source Control:** Eliminate nutrients (dust, organic films) through regular cleaning.
2. **Environmental Control:** Maintain RH **40–60 %** and surface temperatures above dew point.
3. **Disinfection:** UV-C germicidal lamps (**254 nm**, dose 30–50 mJ/cm²); **TiO₂ photocatalytic oxidation**; chlorine/bromine biocides in cooling towers.

**ASHRAE 188 Compliance:** Implement a **Water Management Program (WMP)** with:
- Risk assessment
- Control limits (temperature 25–45 °C = "danger zone")
- Monitoring protocols
- Verification & documentation

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** Many students mix L/s, m³/s, CFM without conversion. Always state units explicitly. ASHRAE formulas expect L/s for $R_p$, $R_a$.
> 2. **Confusing $R_a$ vs $R_p$:** ASHRAE 62.1 uses $R_p$ for *per-person* and $R_{p,z}$ for *per-area* — **do not swap**.
> 3. **Forgetting $E_v$ and $E_z$:** Omitting the ventilation effectiveness factors leads to **under-sized systems** by 10–20 %, costing marks.
> 4. **RH-IAQ Misconception:** Writing *"high RH kills microbes"* — **incorrect**. High RH **promotes** bacterial/mold growth. Low RH **prolongs virus survival** but **reduces bacteria**.
> 5. **Cooling Coil Temperature:** Not stating that **coil surface < 12 °C** causes continuous condensation → biofilm risk. This specific number scores bonus marks.
> 6. **Neglecting ASHRAE 188:** Many answers miss that **Legionella control is legally mandated**, not optional.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Exam**

### Core Concepts
- ✅ Ventilation = introduction of **outdoor air** + removal of **indoor air** to control IAQ.
- ✅ IAQ pillars: Thermal comfort, CO₂, RH, contaminants.
- ✅ ASHRAE 62.1 specifies **ventilation rate procedure (VRP)** using people and area components.
- ✅ Final design ventilation rate = **MAX** of (per-person, per-area, ACH-based).

### Key Formulas (must memorize)
- ✅ $V_{bz} = P \cdot R_p + A_z \cdot R_{p,z}$
- ✅ $V_{ot} = \sum V_{oz} / E_v$
- ✅ $C_{ss} = C_{out} + G / Q_v$ (steady-state)
- ✅ $C(t) = C_{out} + (C_{ini} - C_{out}) e^{-k t}$ (transient)
- ✅ $k_{decay} = Q_v / V$
- ✅ $Q_{sens,vent} = 1.206 \cdot Q_v \cdot \Delta T$
- ✅ $Q_{lat,vent} = 3001 \cdot Q_v \cdot \Delta W$

### Relative Humidity
- ✅ RH range **40–60 %** = optimal IAQ band (ASHRAE).
- ✅ RH > **70 %** → bacterial/mold proliferation.
- ✅ RH < **40 %** → viral survivability increases.
- ✅ Ventilation moisture balance: $W_{sup} = (Q_{ot} W_{out} + Q_{rec} W_{rec}) / Q_{sup}$.

### Microbial Control
- ✅ Four critical zones: **coils, drain pans, humidifiers, ducts**.
- ✅ UV-C at **254 nm** for germicidal action.
- ✅ ASHRAE 188 → Water Management Program mandatory.
- ✅ Coil surface temperature **< 12 °C** = continuous condensation → **Legionella risk**.

### Constants to Remember
- ✅ $\rho_{air} = 1.20$ kg/m³
- ✅ $c_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K
- ✅ $h_{fg} = 2501$ kJ/kg
- ✅ CO₂ outdoor = **420 ppm**; setpoint = **1000 ppm** (ASHRAE)
- ✅ CO₂ generation (sedentary person) = **0.31 L/min**

### Standards Referenced
- ✅ **ASHRAE 62.1** — Commercial ventilation.
- ✅ **ASHRAE 62.2** — Residential ventilation.
- ✅ **ASHRAE 188** — Legionellosis risk management.
- ✅ **ASHRAE 55** — Thermal comfort (links to RH).

### KTU 2024 Frequently Asked Topics
- ✅ ASHRAE VRP calculations (always tested in Part B).
- ✅ CO₂-based DCV numerical.
- ✅ Transient purge time / decay constant.
- ✅ RH vs microbial growth curve explanation.
- ✅ Microbial control zones + UV-C.
- ✅ Energy Recovery Ventilator (ERV) justification for hot-humid climates (e.g., Kerala).

> **Final Tip:** Always pair every ventilation calculation with **ASHRAE 62.1 references** and end your answers with an **energy-efficiency note** (HRV/ERV). This signals board-exam-level depth and fetches full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Ventilation Systems: Supply System - Devices Used in Supply Systems, Air Inlet System, Filters, Heating & Cooling Equipment, Fans, Duct, Grills, Diffusers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET631 - REFRIGERATION AND AIR CONDITIONING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Types of Ventilation Systems: Supply System - Devices Used in Supply Systems, Air Inlet System, Filters, Heating & Cooling Equipment, Fans, Duct, Grills, Diffusers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Module 3: Ventilation Systems – Supply System & Its Devices

## 1.1 Ventilation Systems: A Formal Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> *Ventilation* is the intentional introduction of **outdoor air** into a space or the movement of air between spaces to control temperature, humidity, odors, contaminants, smoke, or dust. A *Ventilation System* is the engineered network of devices that conditions, transports, and distributes this air to maintain Indoor Environmental Quality (IEQ) within prescribed limits of **ASHRAE Standard 62.1** and **ISHRAE** guidelines.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Performance Metrics:**
> - **Air Changes per Hour (ACH):** The number of times the total volume of air in a room is replaced in one hour. For general offices **4–6 ACH**, for hospitals **12–15 ACH**, for clean rooms **≥ 25 ACH**.
> - **Fresh Air (Outdoor Air):** Minimum **8.5 m³/h per person** (ISHRAE) for non-smoking areas.
> - **Static Pressure:** Typically **25–50 mm of water column (mm WC)** for low-pressure HVAC duct systems.

### 1.2 Three Primary Ventilation System Architectures

| Architecture | Driving Force | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Natural Ventilation** | Wind pressure + Stack effect | Warehouses, sheds, passive buildings |
| **Mechanical (Forced) Ventilation** | Fan-driven positive pressure | Clean rooms, hospitals, commercial AC |
| **Mixed-Mode (Hybrid) Ventilation** | Both natural & mechanical | Energy-efficient green buildings |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy: "The Human Respiratory System"

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Intuition:**
> Think of a building as a *living organism*. The **supply system** is its **mouth and lungs**—it inhales fresh outdoor air (intake), cleans it (filter), warms or cools it (heat exchanger), pushes it through arteries (ducts), and finally exhales it gently into rooms via nozzles (diffusers). Just as our body filters dust via nasal hair and warms air to body temperature before it reaches the lungs, an HVAC supply unit conditions the air to **22 °C ± 1 °C and 50% RH ± 5%** before delivery.

---

## 1.4 Classification of Mechanical Ventilation Systems

A **Supply System** delivers conditioned outdoor + recirculated air *into* the conditioned space. It is one of three fundamental arrangements:

1. **Supply System (Positive Pressure System)** — *Air pushed in.*
2. **Extract System (Negative Pressure System)** — *Air pulled out.*
3. **Supply–Extract (Balanced) System** — *Both push in and pull out.*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The *Supply System* (also called the **Positive-Pressure System**) maintains the room at a pressure slightly **higher (typically +12.5 Pa to +25 Pa)** than surrounding areas. This prevents infiltration of unconditioned or contaminated air, making it the **preferred choice for clean rooms, operation theatres, and pharmaceutical suites**.

---

## 1.5 Mainline Schematic of a Supply System

A complete supply air handling chain consists of the following sequential components:

$$
\boxed{\text{Air Intake} \rightarrow \text{Filter} \rightarrow \text{Heating/Cooling Coil} \rightarrow \text{Fan} \rightarrow \text{Duct} \rightarrow \text{Grill/Diffuser}}
$$

Each device in this chain performs a specific thermodynamic or aerodynamic function and is studied in detail in the following sections.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis of Supply-System Devices

## 2.1 Air Inlet System

> [!NOTE]
> The **Air Inlet** (also called *outdoor air intake*) is the entry point where untreated atmospheric air enters the AHU (Air Handling Unit). It must be located to avoid contamination from exhaust plumes, cooling towers, or vehicular emissions.

### Components of the Inlet System:

| Component | Function | Material |
|---|---|---|
| **Louver** | Permits air entry, blocks rain, allows drainage | Extruded aluminium |
| **Bird Screen / Mesh** | Prevents entry of birds, leaves, debris | GI wire, SS-304 |
| **Sand Trap / Louvred Panel** | Removes coarse dust by inertial separation | GI sheet |
| **Damper (Inlet)** | Controls the volume of outdoor air admitted | Galvanized steel |
| **Plenum Chamber** | Equalises velocity and directs air into filter bank | GI / MS sheet |

### Design Rule of Thumb

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Face Velocity at Intake Louvers:** Should be limited to **≤ 2.5 m/s** to prevent water carry-over during rain and to minimise pressure drop (typically **≤ 12 mm WC**).

---

## 2.2 Filters

Filters remove particulate and microbial contaminants. They are classified by their **arresting mechanism** and **efficiency**.

### 2.2.1 Types of Air Filters

| Filter Class | Arrestance Mechanism | Efficiency | Typical ΔP (mm WC) | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Pre-Filter (G3 / G4)** | Inertial + Sieve | 60–90% by weight | 5–10 | First stage in AHU |
| **Fine Filter (F5–F9)** | Diffusion + Interception | 40–95% (atmospheric dust) | 10–25 | Secondary stage |
| **HEPA Filter (H10–H14)** | Diffusion + Electrostatic | **≥ 99.97%** at 0.3 μm | 25–50 | OT, pharma cleanrooms |
| **ULPA Filter (U15–U17)** | Diffusion dominant | **≥ 99.9995%** at 0.1–0.2 μm | 50–75 | Semi-conductor fabs |
| **Electrostatic Filter** | Electrostatic attraction | 80–95% | 5–10 | Commercial kitchens |
| **Activated Carbon Filter** | Adsorption (gases, odours) | Molecular | 10–25 | Museums, hospitals |
| **Washable / viscous filter** | Coarse impingement | 30–60% | 5–10 | Industrial exhaust |

### 2.2.2 Filter Efficiency Formulas

For weight-arrestance efficiency:

$$\eta_{w} = \frac{W_{in} - W_{out}}{W_{in}} \times 100 \quad [\%]$$

For atmospheric dust-spot efficiency (used for HEPA rating):

$$\eta_{D} = 1 - \frac{D_{out}}{D_{in}}$$

where:
- $W$ = weight of dust upstream (in) and downstream (out)
- $D$ = dust-spot measurement

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **MPPS (Most Penetrating Particle Size)** for HEPA filters is **0.3 μm**, which is why HEPA ratings are quoted at this exact particle diameter.

---

## 2.3 Heating & Cooling Equipment

### 2.3.1 Heating Coils

| Type | Heat Transfer Fluid | Typical Capacity |
|---|---|---|
| **Hot Water Coil (HWC)** | Hot water at 80/60 °C | 5–100 kW |
| **Steam Coil** | Low-pressure saturated steam | 50–500 kW |
| **Electric Heater** | Resistance (NiCr) | 0.5–30 kW |
| **DX Heating (Heat Pump)** | Refrigerant in heating mode | 3–50 kW |

The **heat transfer rate** for any coil:

$$
Q_{h} = \dot{m}_{a} \, c_{p,a} \, \Delta T = \dot{m}_{w} \, c_{p,w} \, \Delta T_{w}
$$

Where:
- $\dot{m}_a$ = mass flow rate of air (kg/s)
- $c_{p,a}$ = **1.005 kJ/kg·K** (specific heat of air)
- $\Delta T$ = temperature rise across the coil (K)

### 2.3.2 Cooling Coils

Cooling coils are typically **direct expansion (DX)** or **chilled water (CHW)** types operating between **6 °C / 12 °C** (CHW) supply/return temperatures.

The **SHR (Sensible Heat Ratio)** determines whether dehumidification occurs:

$$
SHR = \frac{Q_{sensible}}{Q_{sensible} + Q_{latent}} = \frac{Q_s}{Q_{total}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **cooler coil is a dehumidifier** whenever the coil surface temperature falls *below* the dew point of the entering air. If coil surface $T_{coil} < T_{dp,air}$, water condenses and latent heat is removed.

### 2.3.3 The Bypass Factor (BPF) – A KTU Favourite

The **Bypass Factor** quantifies how much entering air *misses* the cooling coil:

$$
BPF = \frac{T_2 - T_1}{T_3 - T_1}
$$

Where:
- $T_1$ = entering air DBT
- $T_2$ = leaving air DBT
- $T_3$ = coil surface temperature (ADP)

> A *low* BPF (0.05–0.15) indicates an efficient coil; BPF rises as coil rows decrease or face velocity rises.

---

## 2.4 Fans

### 2.4.1 Types of Fans Used in HVAC

| Fan Type | Pressure Rise (mm WC) | Flow Range (m³/s) | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Propeller Fan (Axial)** | 5–25 | 0.5–50 | Exhaust, wall-mounted |
| **Tube-Axial Fan** | 25–75 | 1–30 | Industrial ventilation |
| **Vane-Axial Fan** | 50–150 | 5–50 | Large AHU supply |
| **Centrifugal – SWSI Forward Curved** | 25–75 | 0.5–25 | Small AHUs (clean air) |
| **Centrifugal – Backward Inclined** | 50–200 | 5–100 | Large AHUs, clean air |
| **Centrifugal – Radial Blade (Lime)** | 100–400 | 5–50 | Dust-laden airstreams |
| **Plug / Multiblade Fan** | 50–150 | 5–100 | Compact AHU |

### 2.4.2 Fan Laws (Critical for KTU)

The three classic fan laws, used to scale fan performance, are:

$$
\frac{Q_1}{Q_2} = \frac{D_1^3 N_1}{D_2^3 N_2}
$$

$$
\frac{P_1}{P_2} = \frac{\rho_1}{\rho_2} \left(\frac{D_1 N_1}{D_2 N_2}\right)^2
$$

$$
\frac{P_{w1}}{P_{w2}} = \frac{\rho_1}{\rho_2} \left(\frac{D_1 N_1}{D_2 N_2}\right)^3
$$

Where:
- $Q$ = volume flow rate
- $P$ = pressure rise
- $P_w$ = power absorbed
- $D$ = impeller diameter
- $N$ = rotational speed (RPM)

---

## 2.5 Ducts

### 2.5.1 Duct Material & Classification

| Material | Max Velocity (m/s) | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **GI Sheet** (G90 galvanized) | 5–10 (low-pressure) | Standard HVAC |
| **Aluminium Sheet** | 8–12 | Clean environments |
| **MS Sheet (welded)** | 10–15 | Industrial exhaust |
| **FRP / PVC** | 4–7 | Corrosive fumes |
| **Flexible (PVC + steel helix)** | 4–6 | Final connection to diffusers |

### 2.5.2 Duct Sizing – Equal Friction Method

The duct diameter (or equivalent) is selected such that the **frictional loss per unit length is constant** (typically **0.08–0.1 mm WC per metre** for low-pressure systems):

$$
\frac{\Delta P_f}{L} = \text{constant} \approx 0.08 \text{ to } 0.1 \text{ mm WC/m}
$$

The **Hagen–Poiseuille-type** duct friction formula used in practice:

$$
\Delta P_f = \frac{f \cdot L}{D_h} \cdot \frac{\rho v^2}{2}
$$

Where $f$ = Darcy friction factor (function of Reynolds number and roughness), $D_h$ = hydraulic diameter.

For a rectangular duct of dimensions $a \times b$:

$$
D_h = \frac{2ab}{a + b}
$$

---

## 2.6 Grills and Diffusers

> [!NOTE]
> **Grills (Grilles)** have fixed or adjustable *vanes* with single deflection (horizontal or vertical).
> **Diffusers** are *terminal devices* that mix primary (supply) air with secondary (room) air to maintain uniform throw, drop, and diffusion.

### Types of Diffusers

| Diffuser Type | Throw Pattern | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Ceiling Square Cone Diffuser** | Radial, 360° | Offices, lobbies |
| **Linear Slot Diffuser** | Plane, long-throw | Theatres, corridors |
| **Perforated Face Diffuser** | Low-induction, radial | Hospitals, libraries |
| **Jet Nozzle Diffuser** | Long-throw (≥ 15 m) | High-bay industrial halls |
| **Swirl Diffuser** | Rotational, induced | Variable air volume (VAV) |
| **Egg Crate / Return Grille** | Return air | False-ceiling plenums |

### Important Diffuser Terminology

- **Throw ($T$)** – Horizontal distance from diffuser to where air velocity drops to **0.25 m/s** (or 50 fpm).
- **Drop** – Vertical distance between supply air and occupied zone.
- **Spread** – Lateral spread of the air stream at the throw distance.
- **Noise Criterion (NC)** – Acoustic rating; for offices **NC ≤ 35**, hospitals **NC ≤ 30**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For supply diffusers, the *induction ratio* is the volumetric ratio of **secondary (room) air** to **primary (supply) air**. A good ceiling diffuser achieves an induction ratio between **3:1 and 5:1** at the throw distance.

---

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\eta_w = \frac{W_{in}-W_{out}}{W_{in}} \times 100$ | Filter weight efficiency (%) |
| 2 | $Q = \dot{m}_a \, c_p \, \Delta T$ | Heating/cooling load (kW) |
| 3 | $SHR = \frac{Q_s}{Q_s + Q_l}$ | Sensible Heat Ratio |
| 4 | $BPF = \frac{T_2 - T_1}{T_3 - T_1}$ | Coil Bypass Factor |
| 5 | $ADP = T_1 - BPF \cdot (T_1 - T_3)$ | Apparatus Dew Point |
| 6 | $D_h = \frac{2ab}{a+b}$ | Hydraulic diameter of rectangular duct |
| 7 | $\Delta P_f = \frac{fL}{D_h} \cdot \frac{\rho v^2}{2}$ | Duct friction loss |
| 8 | $\frac{Q_1}{Q_2} = \frac{D_1^3 N_1}{D_2^3 N_2}$ | Fan Law #1 (Flow) |
| 9 | $\frac{P_1}{P_2} = \frac{\rho_1}{\rho_2}\left(\frac{D_1 N_1}{D_2 N_2}\right)^2$ | Fan Law #2 (Pressure) |
| 10 | $\frac{P_{w1}}{P_{w2}} = \frac{\rho_1}{\rho_2}\left(\frac{D_1 N_1}{D_2 N_2}\right)^3$ | Fan Law #3 (Power) |
| 11 | $ACH = \frac{Q \cdot 3600}{V_{room}}$ | Air changes per hour |
| 12 | $\eta_s = 1 - \frac{D_{out}}{D_{in}}$ | Dust-spot efficiency |

> [!NOTE]
> All temperatures in °C, mass flow in kg/s, lengths in metres, pressures in mm WC (multiply by **9.81** to convert to Pa).

### Real-World Engineering Utility

These equations and devices underpin every **Green Building certification (LEED, IGBC, GRIHA)**, every **pharma cleanroom** (ISO Class 5/7/8), and every **operation theatre** (ISO 14644). Mastery of the supply system design is therefore a *non-negotiable* skill for MEP consultants, HVAC design engineers, and energy auditors.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Worked Examples

## 3.1 Derivation: Relation between Air Quantity and Duct Size (Equal Friction Method)

### Given:
- Total air quantity $Q$ (m³/s) to be carried
- Length of duct $L$ (m)
- Allowable pressure drop per unit length $(ΔP/L)_{allow}$

### Derivation:

The Darcy–Weisbach friction equation for airflow in a duct is:

$$\Delta P_f = \frac{f \cdot L \cdot \rho \cdot v^2}{2 D_h}$$

Velocity in terms of volumetric flow:

$$v = \frac{Q}{A}$$

For a round duct $A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$, so:

$$v = \frac{4Q}{\pi D^2}$$

Substituting:

$$\Delta P_f = \frac{f L \rho}{2D} \cdot \left(\frac{4Q}{\pi D^2}\right)^2$$

$$\Delta P_f = \frac{8 f L \rho Q^2}{\pi^2 D^5}$$

Therefore the **diameter** for a given pressure drop per metre:

$$D = \left(\frac{8 f \rho Q^2}{\pi^2 \, (\Delta P_f/L)}\right)^{1/5}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This **fifth-root dependence** is why a small increase in diameter causes a major drop in pressure loss — a *core insight* in duct design economics.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example #1 — Cooling Coil Bypass Factor (14-Mark Standard)

**Problem:** An AHU cooling coil has an entering air DBT of **30 °C**, WBT of **22 °C**. The coil ADP (apparatus dew point) is **8 °C** and the leaving air DBT is **14 °C**. Determine:
1. The **Bypass Factor (BPF)**.
2. The leaving air **WBT**.
3. The **SHR** if 2 g/kg of moisture is removed.

### Solution:

#### Step 1 — Bypass Factor

$$
BPF = \frac{T_2 - T_1}{T_3 - T_1} = \frac{14 - 30}{8 - 30} = \frac{-16}{-22}
$$

$$
\boxed{BPF = 0.727}
$$

**Remark:** BPF > 0.5 indicates a *short* coil (typically 4 rows). To bring BPF ≤ 0.15, increase coil rows or reduce face velocity.

#### Step 2 — Leaving Air WBT (Psychrometric Construction)

For a coil BPF plot on the psychrometric chart:
- Plot points at $(T_1 = 30 \text{ °C}, W_1 \text{ from WBT}=22\text{ °C})$ → entering state.
- Plot $(T_3 = 8 \text{ °C}, W_3 \text{ at saturation})$ → coil surface ADP.
- Mark point at BPF = 0.727 on the line from $T_3$ to $T_1$.

Reading from chart (or using the energy balance for a linear process):

$$
W_2 = W_3 + BPF \cdot (W_1 - W_3)
$$

For $W_1$ at 30 °C DBT / 22 °C WBT ≈ **0.0136 kg/kg**, and $W_3$ at 8 °C saturation ≈ **0.0073 kg/kg**:

$$
W_2 = 0.0073 + 0.727 \times (0.0136 - 0.0073) = 0.0073 + 0.727 \times 0.0063
$$

$$
W_2 = 0.0073 + 0.00458 = \boxed{0.01188 \text{ kg/kg}}
$$

#### Step 3 — Sensible Heat Ratio

Sensible heat:

$$
Q_s = \dot{m}_a \, c_p \, (T_1 - T_2) = 1.005 \times (30 - 14) = 16.08 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

Latent heat (for ΔW = 0.0017 kg/kg):

$$
Q_l = \dot{m}_a \cdot h_{fg} \cdot \Delta W = 2501 \times 0.0017 = 4.252 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

Total:

$$
Q_{total} = 16.08 + 4.252 = 20.33 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

$$
SHR = \frac{16.08}{20.33} = \boxed{0.791}
$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Example #2 — Fan Laws (14-Mark Standard)

**Problem:** A centrifugal fan delivers **2.5 m³/s** at a static pressure of **30 mm WC** while running at **960 rpm** and absorbing **3.0 kW**. The impeller diameter is **600 mm**. If the speed is increased to **1200 rpm** with the same impeller diameter, determine the new flow, pressure, and power. Assume air density is unchanged.

### Solution:

Using Fan Laws with $D_1 = D_2$ (same impeller) and $\rho_1 = \rho_2$:

#### Step 1 — New Volume Flow

$$
\frac{Q_2}{Q_1} = \frac{N_2}{N_1}
$$

$$
Q_2 = Q_1 \cdot \frac{N_2}{N_1} = 2.5 \times \frac{1200}{960}
$$

$$
\boxed{Q_2 = 3.125 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}}
$$

#### Step 2 — New Static Pressure

$$
\frac{P_2}{P_1} = \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^2
$$

$$
P_2 = 30 \times \left(\frac{1200}{960}\right)^2 = 30 \times 1.5625
$$

$$
\boxed{P_2 = 46.875 \text{ mm WC}}
$$

#### Step 3 — New Power

$$
\frac{P_{w2}}{P_{w1}} = \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^3
$$

$$
P_{w2} = 3.0 \times \left(\frac{1200}{960}\right)^3 = 3.0 \times 1.953125
$$

$$
\boxed{P_{w2} = 5.859 \text{ kW}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Insight:** Doubling fan speed **doubles flow** but **triples power** and **quadruples pressure** — this is why VFD (Variable Frequency Drive) speed reduction is the *most energy-efficient* flow-control method.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example #3 — Filter Efficiency

**Problem:** A pre-filter upstream of an AHU collects **12 g/h** of dust from an airstream that carries **50 g/h**. The downstream HEPA filter collects **0.04 g/h** out of **5 g/h** arriving. Determine the efficiency of each filter and the overall efficiency.

### Solution:

#### Pre-filter efficiency:

$$
\eta_{w1} = \frac{50 - 12}{50} \times 100 = \boxed{76\%}
$$

#### HEPA efficiency:

$$
\eta_{w2} = \frac{5 - 0.04}{5} \times 100 = \boxed{99.2\%}
$$

#### Overall efficiency:

Dust entering = 50 g/h, dust leaving = 0.04 g/h:

$$
\eta_{overall} = \frac{50 - 0.04}{50} \times 100 = \boxed{99.92\%}
$$

#### Alternative formula verification:

$$
\eta_{overall} = 1 - (1-\eta_1)(1-\eta_2) = 1 - (0.24)(0.008) = 1 - 0.00192 = 0.99808 \approx 99.92\%
$$

Matches perfectly. ✓

---

## 3.5 Worked Example #4 — Duct Diameter using Equal Friction

**Problem:** A low-pressure duct must carry **1.2 m³/s** of air with a maximum allowable friction loss of **0.10 mm WC per metre**. Take air density **1.2 kg/m³** and Darcy friction factor **f = 0.02**. Find the diameter.

### Solution:

Using:

$$
D = \left(\frac{8 f \rho Q^2}{\pi^2 \, (\Delta P_f/L)}\right)^{1/5}
$$

#### Substituting:

- $f = 0.02$
- $\rho = 1.2 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $Q = 1.2 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$
- $\Delta P_f/L = 0.10 \text{ mm WC/m} = 0.981 \text{ Pa/m}$

Numerator:

$$
8 \times 0.02 \times 1.2 \times (1.2)^2 = 0.192 \times 1.44 = 0.27648
$$

Denominator:

$$
\pi^2 \times 0.981 = 9.8696 \times 0.981 = 9.6821
$$

Ratio:

$$
\frac{0.27648}{9.6821} = 0.02856
$$

Fifth root:

$$
D = (0.02856)^{0.2} = \boxed{0.494 \text{ m} \approx 500 \text{ mm}}
$$

#### Convert to rectangular duct equivalent:

For $D = 500$ mm, equal aspect ratio $a = b$:

$$
a = b = \frac{D}{1.30} \approx 385 \text{ mm}
$$

(Ratio of diameter to side of equivalent square uses $D = 1.30 \sqrt{ab}$.)

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 System-Level Flow Diagram of a Supply Ventilation System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[OUTDOOR AIR ATMOSPHERE] --> A2[LOUVER + BIRD SCREEN]
    A2 --> A3[INLET DAMPER]
    A3 --> A4[PRE FILTER G3 G4]
    A4 --> A5[FINE FILTER F7]
    A5 --> A6[COOLING COIL CHW]
    A6 --> A7[HEATING COIL HOT WATER]
    A7 --> A8[ELIMINATOR / MOISTURE SEPARATOR]
    A8 --> A9[SAFETY GRID FOR FAN]
    A9 --> A10[SUPPLY FAN CENTRIFUGAL BI]
    A10 --> A11[MOTOR BELT DRIVE 1450 RPM]
    A11 --> A12[DISCHARGE PLENUM]
    A12 --> A13[MAIN TRUNK DUCT]
    A13 --> A14[BRANCH DUCT]
    A14 --> A15[VCD VOLUME CONTROL DAMPER]
    A15 --> A16[DIFFUSER CEILING TYPE]
    A16 --> A17[CONDITIONED SPACE OFFICE ROOM]
    A17 --> A18[RETURN AIR GRILLE]
    A18 --> A19[RETURN DUCT]
    A19 --> A20[RETURN FAN]
    A20 --> A21[MIXING BOX WITH OUTDOOR AIR]
    A21 --> A4

    classDef def fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd,color:#000
    classDef filter fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#ef6c00,color:#000
    classDef coil fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#6a1b9a,color:#000
    classDef fan fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#000
    classDef deliver fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#ad1457,color:#000
    class A1,A2,A3,A12,A13,A14,A15,A17,A18,A19 def
    class A4,A5,A8,A21 filter
    class A6,A7 coil
    class A9,A10,A11,A20 fan
    class A16 deliver
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture – Filter Train (Pre + Fine + HEPA)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[CONTAMINATED OUTDOOR AIR] --> P1[PRE FILTER G4]
    P1 --> P1D[(Dust Accumulation 90 percent coarse)]
    P1D --> P2[FINE FILTER F7]
    P2 --> P2D[(Remaining 30 percent fine particulate)]
    P2D --> P3[HEPA H13]
    P3 --> P3D[(MPPS 0.3 micron 99.97 percent removal)]
    P3D --> O1[CLEAN AIR TO COIL]

    S1[RETURN AIR FROM ROOM] --> MP[MIXING PLENUM]
    MP --> P1
    EXH[EXHAUST PORTION 10 percent] -->|WASTE| OUT1[RELIEF TO ATMOSPHERE]
    MP --> EXH
    O1 --> COIL[COOLING COIL]
    COIL --> HEAT[HEATING COIL]
    HEAT --> FAN[SUPPLY FAN]
    FAN --> DUCT[DISTRIBUTION DUCT]
    DUCT --> ROOM[CONDITIONED ROOM]

    classDef stage fill:#fffde7,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000
    classDef unit fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1565c0,color:#000
    classDef reject fill:#ffebee,stroke:#c62828,color:#000
    class S,O1,ROOM unit
    class P1,P2,P3,COIL,HEAT,FAN,DUCT,MP stage
    class P1D,P2D,P3D,EXH,OUT1 reject
```

## 4.3 Fan Curve vs System Curve – Operating Point

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F1[FAN CURVE PRESSURE RISES WITH FLOW DECREASES] --> OP[OPERATING POINT INTERSECTION]
    S1[SYSTEM CURVE PRESSURE INCREASES AS SQUARE OF FLOW] --> OP
    OP --> QDES[DESIGN FLOW Q AT NOMINAL PRESSURE]
    OP1[NEW FAN AT HIGHER SPEED] --> OP2[NEW OPERATING POINT HIGHER Q HIGHER P]
    OP --> EFFICIENCY[FAN EFFICIENCY PEAK REGION 60 to 80 percent OF WIDE OPEN FLOW]

    classDef pt fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000
    classDef warn fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c,color:#000
    class OP,QDES,EFFICIENCY,OP2 pt
    class F1,S1 warn
```

## 4.4 Diffuser Throw Pattern Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[CEILING DIFFUSER] --> B[PRIMARY JET 22 DEG C]
    B --> C[INDUCTION OF ROOM AIR 26 DEG C]
    C --> D[MIXED AIR STREAM]
    D --> E[OCCUPIED ZONE 1.5 M ABOVE FLOOR]
    E --> F[VELOCITY 0.25 M/S AT THROW]

    classDef pri fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#000
    classDef mix fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000
    classDef occ fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000
    class A,B pri
    class C,D mix
    class E,F occ
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Aid:** In the supply chain diagram above, each coloured node is a **physical component** in the AHU. The arrows represent the *direction of airflow*. The "filter train" architecture illustrates how multiple filter stages in series achieve cumulative efficiency approaching 99.99% — the foundation of every ISO Class 5–8 cleanroom.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Differentiate between a HEPA filter and an ULPA filter in terms of efficiency and particle size.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | HEPA Filter | ULPA Filter |
|---|---|---|
| Full form | High-Efficiency Particulate Air | Ultra-Low Penetration Air |
| Test particle size | 0.3 μm (MPPS) | 0.1–0.2 μm (MPPS) |
| Minimum efficiency | 99.97% | 99.9995% |
| Typical ΔP | 25–50 mm WC | 50–75 mm WC |
| Application | Hospitals, OTs, pharma | Semi-conductor fabs, nuclear |

*(3 marks: 1 each for efficiency, particle size, application)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**Define Bypass Factor of a cooling coil. What is its significance?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Bypass Factor (BPF)** of a cooling coil is defined as the ratio of the temperature difference between leaving air and entering air to the temperature difference between entering air and the Apparatus Dew Point (ADP) of the coil surface.

$$
BPF = \frac{T_2 - T_1}{T_{ADP} - T_1}
$$

**Significance:**
1. Lower BPF → more effective coil → better dehumidification.
2. BPF depends on coil rows, face velocity, and fin spacing.
3. An ideal coil has BPF = 0; a *very short* coil has BPF → 1.

*(3 marks: 1 for formula, 2 for significance)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU Internal Choice Format

---

### QUESTION A — (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**(a) Describe with neat sketch the various components of a supply ventilation system. List the function of each component.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The supply ventilation system consists of the following sequential devices:

1. **Air Intake (Louver + Screen):** Allows entry of outdoor air while preventing ingress of rain, leaves, and birds. Face velocity limited to ≤ 2.5 m/s.

2. **Damper:** A louvered control device that regulates the volume of fresh/recirculated air entering the AHU.

3. **Filters (Pre + Fine + HEPA):** Remove airborne particulates in stages. Pre-filter (G4) for coarse dust, fine filter (F7) for fine dust, HEPA (H13) for sub-micron particles at ≥ 99.97% efficiency at 0.3 μm.

4. **Cooling Coil:** Either direct expansion (DX) or chilled water (CHW at 6–12 °C). Removes both sensible and latent heat.

5. **Heating Coil:** Hot water (80/60 °C) or steam. Used for reheating to control dew point.

6. **Eliminator / Moisture Separator:** Removes entrained moisture droplets from the airstream after the cooling coil.

7. **Supply Fan:** Centrifugal backward-inclined (or forward-curved for clean air) fan, sized for system resistance.

8. **Duct Network:** GI sheet metal ducts sized by equal-friction method; carries air from AHU to conditioned spaces.

9. **Volume Control Damper (VCD):** Balances airflow to each terminal.

10. **Diffusers & Grilles:** Terminal devices distributing air into the room with proper throw, drop, and noise criterion.

**Sketch** (description): Block diagram from *Atmosphere → Louver → Damper → Filter → Coil → Fan → Duct → VCD → Diffuser → Room*.

*(7 marks: 4 for list with functions, 2 for sketch, 1 for working principle)*

---

**(b) A centrifugal fan delivers 5 m³/s of air at 40 mm WC static pressure when running at 1000 rpm and absorbing 7.5 kW. The impeller diameter is 750 mm. Calculate the flow, pressure, and power if the speed is changed to 1500 rpm with the same diameter.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Given:
- $Q_1 = 5 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$
- $P_1 = 40 \text{ mm WC}$
- $N_1 = 1000 \text{ rpm}$
- $P_{w1} = 7.5 \text{ kW}$
- $D_1 = D_2 = 750 \text{ mm}$
- $N_2 = 1500 \text{ rpm}$

#### Step 1 — New Volume Flow (Fan Law #1)

$$
\frac{Q_2}{Q_1} = \frac{N_2}{N_1} \Rightarrow Q_2 = 5 \times \frac{1500}{1000}
$$

$$
\boxed{Q_2 = 7.5 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}}
$$

#### Step 2 — New Static Pressure (Fan Law #2)

$$
\frac{P_2}{P_1} = \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^2 \Rightarrow P_2 = 40 \times \left(\frac{1500}{1000}\right)^2 = 40 \times 2.25
$$

$$
\boxed{P_2 = 90 \text{ mm WC}}
$$

#### Step 3 — New Power (Fan Law #3)

$$
\frac{P_{w2}}{P_{w1}} = \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^3 \Rightarrow P_{w2} = 7.5 \times (1.5)^3 = 7.5 \times 3.375
$$

$$
\boxed{P_{w2} = 25.31 \text{ kW}}
$$

#### Result Table:

| Parameter | Original | New |
|---|---|---|
| Flow (m³/s) | 5.0 | 7.5 |
| Pressure (mm WC) | 40 | 90 |
| Power (kW) | 7.5 | 25.31 |

*(7 marks: 2 for fan law identification, 4 for three numerical results, 1 for physical insight on cubic power relation)*

---

### QUESTION B — (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a) Explain the working principle of a HEPA filter with a labelled sketch. What is MPPS and why is it chosen as the standard for rating HEPA filters?** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

#### Working Principle:

A **HEPA (High-Efficiency Particulate Air) filter** is a *pleated* mechanical air filter made of randomly arranged **borosilicate microfibers** (diameter 0.5–2 μm) bonded into a sheet, folded into a zig-zag mat with corrugated aluminium separators, and sealed in a rigid frame (wood, GI, or aluminium).

Air is forced through the filter mat. Particles are trapped by **four simultaneous mechanisms**:

1. **Inertial Impaction** – Large (> 1 μm) particles cannot follow the air streamlines around the fibres and impact them.
2. **Interception** – Medium-sized (0.5–1 μm) particles follow streamlines but touch a fibre due to their finite radius.
3. **Diffusion (Brownian)** – Sub-micron (< 0.5 μm) particles undergo random motion and collide with fibres.
4. **Electrostatic Attraction** – Some filters carry static charge that attracts oppositely charged particles.

#### MPPS (Most Penetrating Particle Size):

The filter efficiency is **lowest** at a particle size of approximately **0.3 μm**, known as **MPPS**.

**Why MPPS is the Standard:**
- Particles **larger than 0.3 μm** are caught efficiently by impaction + interception.
- Particles **smaller than 0.3 μm** are caught by diffusion (Brownian motion).
- At **0.3 μm** neither mechanism is dominant — hence the filter is "worst-case" tested at this size.
- By quoting efficiency at MPPS, manufacturers provide a *conservative* guarantee.

#### Labelled Sketch (description):
Frame → Pleated Filter Media → Corrugated Separator → Gasket → Air Inlet (Dirty) → Outlet (Clean) → ΔP gauge.

*(7 marks: 3 for working principle, 2 for mechanisms, 2 for MPPS explanation)*

---

**(b) An AHU cooling coil has entering air at 32 °C DBT and 24 °C WBT. The coil ADP is 10 °C and the leaving air DBT is 17 °C. Calculate (i) Bypass Factor (ii) leaving air humidity ratio (iii) SHR if latent heat corresponds to 0.0018 kg/kg moisture removal. Take h_fg = 2501 kJ/kg.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

#### (i) Bypass Factor

$$
BPF = \frac{T_2 - T_1}{T_{ADP} - T_1} = \frac{17 - 32}{10 - 32} = \frac{-15}{-22}
$$

$$
\boxed{BPF = 0.682}
$$

#### (ii) Leaving Air Humidity Ratio

From psychrometric chart: at 32 °C DBT / 24 °C WBT → $W_1 \approx 0.0160$ kg/kg.
At 10 °C saturation → $W_3 \approx 0.0076$ kg/kg.

$$
W_2 = W_3 + BPF \cdot (W_1 - W_3) = 0.0076 + 0.682 \times (0.0160 - 0.0076)
$$

$$
W_2 = 0.0076 + 0.682 \times 0.0084 = 0.0076 + 0.00573
$$

$$
\boxed{W_2 = 0.01333 \text{ kg/kg}}
$$

#### (iii) Sensible Heat Ratio

Sensible heat per kg:

$$
q_s = c_p \cdot (T_1 - T_2) = 1.005 \times (32 - 17) = 1.005 \times 15 = 15.075 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

Latent heat per kg:

$$
q_l = h_{fg} \cdot \Delta W = 2501 \times 0.0018 = 4.502 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

Total heat:

$$
q_{total} = 15.075 + 4.502 = 19.577 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

$$
SHR = \frac{q_s}{q_{total}} = \frac{15.075}{19.577}
$$

$$
\boxed{SHR = 0.770}
$$

*(7 marks: 2 for BPF, 2 for W₂, 2 for SHR, 1 for final interpretation)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning – Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign error in BPF:** Always remember $(T_2 - T_1)$ and $(T_{ADP} - T_1)$ must carry the *same sign* — both are negative in cooling, so BPF is positive. Students often forget the negative signs and get a *negative* BPF.
> 2. **Fan Law wrong variable:** Mixing up $Q$ (∝ N), $P$ (∝ N²), $P_w$ (∝ N³). A wrong exponent loses 2 marks.
> 3. **Duct diameter units:** Pressure must be in **Pa** (or mm WC converted via $\times 9.81$) in the friction formula; mixing units yields a wrong diameter by an order of magnitude.
> 4. **Confusing HEPA with ULPA:** HEPA = 99.97% at 0.3 μm; ULPA = 99.9995% at 0.1–0.2 μm. A 1-mark loss per mismatch.
> 5. **Forgetting the elbow equivalent length:** In duct design, never forget to add the dynamic-loss coefficient of fittings ($K \cdot v^2/2$) to the straight-run friction loss.
> 6. **Grille vs Diffuser confusion:** Grilles are typically *return* devices (louvered face, no induction); diffusers are *supply* devices designed for induction and throw. A wrong pairing costs 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 3 – Supply System: One-Page Revision**

- [ ] A **Supply System** maintains a *positive* room pressure of **+12.5 to +25 Pa** above ambient.
- [ ] The supply chain order: **Intake → Filter → Coil → Fan → Duct → VCD → Diffuser → Room**.
- [ ] **HEPA efficiency** = **99.97%** at MPPS **0.3 μm**; ULPA = **99.9995%** at **0.1–0.2 μm**.
- [ ] **Filter weight efficiency** $\eta_w = (W_{in} - W_{out}) / W_{in} \times 100$.
- [ ] **Cooling coil BPF** formula: $BPF = (T_2 - T_1)/(T_{ADP} - T_1)$. Lower BPF ⇒ better coil.
- [ ] **SHR** = $Q_s / (Q_s + Q_l)$; if coil surface $T < T_{dp,air}$, dehumidification occurs.
- [ ] **Fan Laws** when $D$ is unchanged: $Q \propto N$, $P \propto N^2$, $P_w \propto N^3$.
- [ ] **Centrifugal BI fans** are preferred for clean-air AHU; **radial-blade fans** for dust-laden air.
- [ ] **Equal-friction method** keeps $\Delta P_f/L = 0.08$–$0.10$ mm WC/m.
- [ ] **Duct hydraulic diameter** $D_h = 2ab/(a+b)$ for a rectangular duct of dimensions $a \times b$.
- [ ] **Diffuser throw** is the distance to where velocity drops to **0.25 m/s** (50 fpm).
- [ ] **NC levels:** offices ≤ 35; hospitals ≤ 30; libraries ≤ 30; auditoriums ≤ 25.
- [ ] Standard **CHW temperatures**: **6 °C supply / 12 °C return**; **HW**: **80 °C supply / 60 °C return**.
- [ ] **Standard air density** at STP: **1.2 kg/m³** (use 1.293 kg/m³ for exact NTP).
- [ ] **Velocity at louvers** ≤ **2.5 m/s** to avoid rain entrainment.
- [ ] Always add the **dynamic loss of fittings** (K-factors) to the straight-run duct friction loss.
- [ ] **Induction ratio** for ceiling diffusers: typically **3:1 to 5:1** at terminal end.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Exhaust System: General Exhaust Systems, Removal of Pollutants and Contaminated Air, Air Cleaning Devices
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET631 - REFRIGERATION AND AIR CONDITIONING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Exhaust System: General Exhaust Systems, Removal of Pollutants and Contaminated Air, Air Cleaning Devices

# 🏭 Exhaust Systems, Pollutant Removal & Air Cleaning Devices

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

An **Exhaust System** is an engineered network of ducts, fans, hoods, dampers, and air-cleaning devices designed to capture, convey, and discharge contaminated, stale, or hot air from a building, industrial process, or occupied space to the outside atmosphere in a controlled manner. It is the **"out-bound counterpart"** of the supply air distribution system and is essential for maintaining indoor air quality (IAQ), occupational health, fire safety, and energy-efficient HVAC operation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Snapshot:**
> *"An exhaust system is a combination of equipment designed to remove air from a space by suction, filter or clean it as required, and discharge the cleaned or contaminated air to a safe location outside the occupied zone."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Lungs of the Building"

Imagine a building is like a **human body**. Just as the lungs inhale fresh oxygen and exhale carbon dioxide, a building needs to **inhale** conditioned fresh air (Supply System) and **exhale** stale, contaminated air (Exhaust System). If a person only inhales but never exhales, carbon dioxide builds up and suffocates them — similarly, a building without an exhaust system suffers from pollutant buildup, sick-building syndrome (SBS), and occupant discomfort.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:**
> - A **kitchen chimney** = Local Exhaust System (captures smoke at the stove itself)
> - A **bathroom exhaust fan** = General Exhaust System (dilutes moisture and odors from the entire bathroom)
> - A **factory smokestack** with scrubbers = Industrial Exhaust with Air Cleaning Devices

### 1.3 Classification of Exhaust Systems

| Type | Capture Method | Typical Application | Pollutant Removed |
|------|---------------|---------------------|-------------------|
| **Local Exhaust Ventilation (LEV)** | At source via hood | Welding, soldering, lab fume hoods | Smoke, dust, fumes, vapors |
| **General Exhaust Ventilation** | Dilution of room air | Toilets, kitchens, parking garages | Moisture, odors, CO |
| **Process Exhaust** | Direct from process | Boilers, furnaces, reactors | Combustion gases, heat |
| **Spot Exhaust** | Near breathing zone | Cleanrooms, hospital OTs | Bioaerosols, particles |

### 1.4 Need for Exhaust Systems

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **ASHRAE Standard 62.1** mandates a minimum ventilation rate that implicitly requires exhaust.
> - Removal of **heat gains** (kitchens, server rooms).
> - Removal of **moisture** (swimming pools, laundries).
> - Removal of **contaminants** (VOCs, CO, particulates).
> - Maintenance of **negative pressure** (isolation rooms, labs).
> - Compliance with **OSHA / Factory Act / NBC 2016** permissible exposure limits (PELs).

### 1.5 Key Physical Metrics Used in Exhaust Design

- **Volumetric Flow Rate (Q)** — measured in **m³/s** or **CFM** (Cubic Feet per Minute). **1 CFM = 0.0004719 m³/s**.
- **Face Velocity** of hood — typically **0.5 to 1.0 m/s** for general exhaust hoods.
- **Transport Velocity** in duct — **10 to 20 m/s** to prevent settling of dust.
- **Static Pressure (Ps)** — negative pressure in duct, in **Pa** or **mm of water column (mmWC)**. **1 mmWC = 9.806 Pa**.
- **Stack Discharge Velocity** — minimum **5 m/s** to prevent re-entrainment of exhaust.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Functional Components of a General Exhaust System

A complete exhaust system comprises **five** essential functional blocks:

1. **Hood (Entry Point)** — Captures the contaminant at or near its source.
2. **Duct Network (Transport Path)** — Conveys contaminated air with sufficient velocity.
3. **Air Cleaning Device (Filter / Scrubber / ESP)** — Removes pollutants before discharge.
4. **Exhaust Fan (Driving Force)** — Provides negative pressure to overcome duct resistance.
5. **Discharge Stack (Exit)** — Releases cleaned air at a safe height and velocity.

### 2.2 Operating Principle — Why Exhaust Works

The exhaust fan creates a **negative static pressure (suction)** at the hood inlet. Atmospheric pressure pushes contaminated room air into the hood, through the duct, into the cleaning device, and finally up the stack. The fan must overcome:

- **Velocity Pressure** ($P_v = \frac{1}{2}\rho v^2$)
- **Friction Losses** along duct walls (Darcy-Weisbach equation)
- **Dynamic Losses** at fittings (elbows, dampers, hood entry)
- **Air-Cleaning Device Pressure Drop** (critical design constraint)

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" Behind Negative Pressure:**
> The fan does NOT "suck" air — it removes air from the downstream side, lowering pressure. The higher atmospheric pressure upstream then pushes air into the low-pressure region. This is identical to how a straw works when you inhale.

### 2.3 Design Philosophy of Local Exhaust Systems

The **Industrial Ventilation Manual** (ACGIH) prescribes a four-step design approach:

- **Step 1:** Identify contaminant source, generation rate (g/s), and toxicity.
- **Step 2:** Choose appropriate **hood type** — enclosing, exterior, or receiving.
- **Step 3:** Calculate required **capture velocity** ($V_c$) — typically **0.25 to 1.0 m/s** depending on toxicity.
- **Step 4:** Determine exhaust flow $Q = V_c \times A$, where $A$ = cross-sectional area at hood face.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Application |
|---|-----------|---------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | Volumetric Flow Rate | $Q = A \times v$ | m³/s | Hood capture flow |
| 2 | Velocity Pressure | $P_v = \frac{1}{2}\rho v^2$ | Pa | Fan total pressure calc |
| 3 | Duct Friction Loss (Hagen-Poiseuille equivalent) | $\Delta P_f = \frac{f L \rho v^2}{2 D}$ | Pa | Duct sizing |
| 4 | Equivalent Duct Length Method | $\Delta P_{total} = \Delta P_f + \sum \Delta P_{fittings}$ | Pa | Fan static pressure |
| 5 | Hood Entry Loss | $\Delta P_{hood} = \zeta \cdot P_v$ | Pa | Local loss coefficient ζ |
| 6 | Stack Height (re-entrainment check) | $H = 0.5 \sqrt{Q}$ (approx) | m | Dispersion height |
| 7 | Filter Pressure Drop | $\Delta P_f = K \cdot \mu \cdot v$ | Pa | Viscous filter model |
| 8 | Cyclone Collection Efficiency | $\eta = \frac{d_{cut}^{2}}{d_{cut}^{2} + d^2}$ | dimensionless | Particle cut-size |
| 9 | Scrubber Removal Efficiency | $\eta_{overall} = 1 - (1-\eta_g)(1-\eta_p)$ | dimensionless | Combined gas + particle |
| 10 | ESP Deutsch Efficiency | $\eta = 1 - e^{-kA/Q}$ | dimensionless | Migration velocity model |

### 2.5 Removal of Pollutants — The Hierarchy of Control

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hierarchy of Control (per OSHA / Industrial Hygiene):**
> 1. **Elimination** — remove the source entirely
> 2. **Substitution** — use less toxic materials
> 3. **Engineering Controls** — exhaust ventilation, enclosures
> 4. **Administrative Controls** — shift rotation, training
> 5. **PPE** — respirators, masks *(last resort)*

Exhaust ventilation falls under **Engineering Controls** and is the **primary** method of pollutant removal in HVAC.

### 2.6 Classification of Pollutants

| Class | Examples | Health Effect | Air Cleaning Device |
|-------|----------|---------------|---------------------|
| **Particulates (PM10, PM2.5)** | Dust, pollen, smoke | Respiratory, lung cancer | HEPA, ESP, Cyclone |
| **Gaseous** | CO, CO₂, NOₓ, SO₂, VOCs | Asphyxiation, irritation | Scrubber, Carbon adsorber |
| **Biological** | Bacteria, viruses, mold | Infections, allergies | UVGI, HEPA, Bi-polar ionization |
| **Thermal** | Sensible heat, latent heat | Discomfort, equipment damage | Heat recovery wheel |
| **Moisture** | Water vapor, steam | Condensation, corrosion | Dehumidifier, desiccant |

### 2.7 Real-World Utility in Engineering

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Applications:**
> - **Pharmaceutical cleanrooms**: HEPA + laminar flow exhaust.
> - **Semiconductor fabs**: ULPA filters + scrubbers for acid fumes.
> - **Hospital OTs**: 100% exhaust with HEPA + UVGI to prevent infection.
> - **Industrial kitchens**: Grease filters + carbon adsorbers.
> - **Welding shops**: Mobile LEV with spark arrestor + carbon filter.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Required Exhaust Flow Rate from a Hood

**Problem Statement:** A laboratory fume hood has a sash opening of width **W = 1.2 m** and height **H = 0.6 m**. The required capture (face) velocity to contain the contaminant is **V_c = 0.5 m/s**. Determine the volumetric flow rate that the exhaust fan must deliver.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1:** Identify the cross-sectional area of the hood face.

$$A = W \times H$$

$$A = 1.2 \times 0.6 = 0.72 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2:** Apply the continuity equation $Q = A \times V$.

$$Q = 0.72 \text{ m}^2 \times 0.5 \text{ m/s}$$

$$Q = 0.36 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 3:** Convert to standard units (CMH = cubic meters per hour).

$$Q = 0.36 \times 3600 = 1296 \text{ CMH}$$

**Step 4:** Convert to CFM (1 m³/s = 2118.88 CFM).

$$Q = 0.36 \times 2118.88 = 762.8 \text{ CFM}$$

> **Engineering Interpretation:** The exhaust fan and duct must be rated for at least **1300 CMH** with a safety margin (typically ×1.1) → **final design Q ≈ 1430 CMH**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Duct Friction Loss Using Darcy-Weisbach Equation

**Problem Statement:** A circular exhaust duct of diameter **D = 0.3 m** carries air at velocity **v = 12 m/s**. The duct length is **L = 25 m**, and the Darcy friction factor **f = 0.02**. Air density **ρ = 1.2 kg/m³**. Calculate the frictional pressure drop.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1:** Recall the Darcy-Weisbach equation:

$$\Delta P_f = \frac{f \cdot L \cdot \rho \cdot v^2}{2 \cdot D}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the numerical values:

$$\Delta P_f = \frac{0.02 \times 25 \times 1.2 \times (12)^2}{2 \times 0.3}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the numerator:

$$0.02 \times 25 \times 1.2 \times 144 = 86.4$$

**Step 4:** Compute the denominator:

$$2 \times 0.3 = 0.6$$

**Step 5:** Divide to obtain pressure drop:

$$\Delta P_f = \frac{86.4}{0.6} = 144 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 6:** Convert to mmWC (1 mmWC = 9.806 Pa):

$$\Delta P_f = \frac{144}{9.806} = 14.69 \text{ mmWC}$$

> **Engineering Interpretation:** The exhaust fan's static pressure rating must exceed this value **plus** fitting losses (typically 30–50% of friction loss) **plus** filter pressure drop.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Cyclone Separator Cut-Size Diameter

**Problem Statement:** A cyclone separator has a cut-size diameter **d_cut = 8 µm**. Particles of diameter **d = 10 µm** are entering the cyclone. Calculate the fractional collection efficiency.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1:** Use the Lapple cyclone efficiency model:

$$\eta = \frac{d_{cut}^2}{d_{cut}^2 + d^2}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute values:

$$\eta = \frac{(8)^2}{(8)^2 + (10)^2} = \frac{64}{64 + 100}$$

**Step 3:** Simplify:

$$\eta = \frac{64}{164} = 0.3902$$

**Step 4:** Convert to percentage:

$$\eta = 39.02\%$$

> **Engineering Interpretation:** Only **39%** of 10 µm particles are collected. Cyclones are **pre-filters**; finer particles require HEPA or ESP downstream.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — ESP Collection Efficiency (Deutsch Equation)

**Problem Statement:** An electrostatic precipitator has a total plate area **A = 500 m²**, gas flow rate **Q = 5 m³/s**, and migration velocity **w = 0.15 m/s**. Determine the ESP fractional efficiency.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1:** Apply the Deutsch-Anderson equation:

$$\eta = 1 - e^{-\frac{w \cdot A}{Q}}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the exponent:

$$\frac{w \cdot A}{Q} = \frac{0.15 \times 500}{5} = \frac{75}{5} = 15$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate the exponential:

$$e^{-15} = 3.06 \times 10^{-7}$$

**Step 4:** Compute efficiency:

$$\eta = 1 - 3.06 \times 10^{-7} \approx 0.9999997 \approx 99.99997\%$$

> **Engineering Interpretation:** Industrial ESPs routinely achieve **>99.9%** efficiency for fly-ash and particulate matter, which is why they dominate power-plant exhaust systems.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Air Cleaning Device

| Pollutant Type | Best Device | Efficiency Range | Pressure Drop (Pa) | Operating Cost |
|----------------|-------------|------------------|---------------------|----------------|
| **Coarse dust (>10 µm)** | Cyclone separator | 70–95% | 50–150 | Low |
| **Fine particles (1–10 µm)** | Electrostatic precipitator | 95–99.9% | 25–100 | Medium |
| **Submicron particles (<1 µm)** | HEPA filter (H13/H14) | 99.95–99.995% | 100–250 | Medium-High |
| **Ultra-fine (ULPA, <0.1 µm)** | ULPA filter (U15) | 99.9995% | 150–350 | High |
| **Gaseous SO₂, HCl** | Wet scrubber | 90–99% | 250–1500 | High |
| **VOCs, odors** | Activated carbon adsorber | 80–98% | 50–200 | Medium |
| **Bioaerosols** | UVGI + HEPA | 99.9% (combined) | 100–250 | Low |
| **Heat recovery** | Air-to-air heat exchanger | 50–80% | 50–200 | Low (energy saving) |
