# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]
**"What is a Local Exhaust Ventilation system? List any four components with their functions."**

**Model Answer:**

A Local Exhaust Ventilation (LEV) system is a **specific engineering control** that captures airborne contaminants **at or very near the point of generation** before they can disperse into the worker's breathing zone or the room air.

**Four key components and their functions:**

| Component | Function |
|-----------|----------|
| **Hood** | Captures contaminant at source (enclosing, exterior, or receiving type) |
| **Duct** | Transports contaminated air to cleaning/discharge point with adequate velocity |
| **Fan** | Provides suction (negative pressure) to overcome duct + filter resistance |
| **Air Cleaner** | Removes pollutants (filters, scrubbers, ESP) before atmospheric discharge |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-line examiner's tip:** Always mention the **capture velocity** concept in any LEV answer to score full marks.

---

### **Question A2** [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**"Differentiate between Local Exhaust Ventilation (LEV) and General Exhaust Ventilation (GEV)."**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Local Exhaust Ventilation (LEV) | General Exhaust Ventilation (GEV) |
|---------|--------------------------------|----------------------------------|
| **Capture location** | At the source of pollutant | Throughout the room (whole-room dilution) |
| **Capture velocity** | High (0.5–2.5 m/s) | Low (0.1–0.25 m/s) |
| **Energy efficiency** | High (small flow, targeted) | Low (large flow, dilution-based) |
| **Air volume handled** | Small | Large |
| **Cost** | Higher capital (hoods, ductwork) | Lower (simple fans) |
| **Best for** | Toxic/regulated contaminants | Odors, moisture, low-toxicity contaminants |
| **Example** | Welding fume hood, lab fume cupboard | Bathroom exhaust fan, parking garage fan |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (Choice 1)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with neat sketches the different **types of hoods** used in local exhaust systems and state the criteria for selecting hood capture velocity.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A circular exhaust duct of diameter **30 cm** carries contaminated air at a velocity of **12 m/s**. The duct length is **30 m**, and the friction factor is **0.018**. Calculate:
1. The volumetric flow rate in m³/s and CMH.
2. The frictional pressure drop in Pa and mmWC.
Take air density ρ = 1.2 kg/m³.

---

### **Model Solution to Question A(a)**

**Types of Hoods:**

**1. Enclosing Hood (Best Capture Efficiency)**
- Completely surrounds the contaminant source.
- Examples: laboratory fume cupboards, paint spray booths.
- Lowest flow required for given capture.

**2. Exterior Hood (Flange / Lip Type)**
- Placed near the source but does not enclose it.
- Flange improves capture efficiency by 25% compared to unflanged.

**3. Receiving Hood (Canopy Type)**
- Placed above a rising plume (hot gases, steam).
- Captures by virtue of natural updraft plus suction.

**4. Slot Hood (High-velocity Slot)**
- Narrow opening, high velocity, used for narrow processes like grinding.

**Criteria for Capture Velocity Selection:**

| Contaminant Type | Capture Velocity (m/s) |
|------------------|------------------------|
| Released with negligible velocity (cold process) | 0.25–0.5 |
| Released with low velocity (welding, soldering) | 0.5–1.0 |
| Released with high velocity (grinding, spray) | 1.0–2.5 |
| Hot plume (rising) | 0.25 (relying on buoyancy) |

---

### **Model Solution to Question A(b)**

**Step 1: Given Data**

$$D = 0.30 \text{ m}, \quad v = 12 \text{ m/s}, \quad L = 30 \text{ m}, \quad f = 0.018, \quad \rho = 1.2 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

**Step 2: Cross-sectional area of duct**

$$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (0.30)^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 0.09}{4} = 0.0707 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 3: Volumetric flow rate in m³/s**

$$Q = A \times v = 0.0707 \times 12 = 0.848 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 4: Convert to CMH**

$$Q = 0.848 \times 3600 = 3053.4 \text{ CMH}$$

**Step 5: Frictional pressure drop (Darcy-Weisbach)**

$$\Delta P_f = \frac{f \cdot L \cdot \rho \cdot v^2}{2 D}$$

$$\Delta P_f = \frac{0.018 \times 30 \times 1.2 \times 144}{2 \times 0.30}$$

**Step 6: Compute numerator**

$$0.018 \times 30 \times 1.2 \times 144 = 93.31$$

**Step 7: Compute denominator**

$$2 \times 0.30 = 0.60$$

**Step 8: Pressure drop in Pa**

$$\Delta P_f = \frac{93.31}{0.60} = 155.5 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 9: Convert to mmWC**

$$\Delta P_f = \frac{155.5}{9.806} = 15.86 \text{ mmWC}$$

> **Final Answers:** Q = **0.848 m³/s = 3053.4 CMH**, ΔP = **155.5 Pa = 15.86 mmWC**.

---

### **Question B (Choice 2)** [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the working principle and construction of:
1. A **High-Efficiency Particulate Air (HEPA) filter**
2. An **Electrostatic Precipitator (ESP)**

Compare their efficiencies and applications.

**(b) [7 Marks]** An ESP has a collecting plate area **A = 300 m²**, gas flow **Q = 6 m³/s**, and particle migration velocity **w = 0.12 m/s**. Calculate the fractional collection efficiency using the Deutsch-Anderson equation. If the inlet dust concentration is **1500 mg/m³**, what is the outlet dust loading in mg/m³?

---

### **Model Solution to Question B(a)**

**HEPA Filter:**
- Made of randomly arranged **borosilicate microfibers** (0.5–2.0 µm diameter) pleated into a mat.
- Three capture mechanisms: **interception, impaction, diffusion** (Brownian).
- Efficiency: **≥99.97% for 0.3 µm particles** (MPPS — Most Penetrating Particle Size).
- Classes: **H10 (85%), H11 (95%), H12 (99.5%), H13 (99.95%), H14 (99.995%)**.

**Electrostatic Precipitator (ESP):**
- Particles are **charged** by a high-voltage discharge electrode (corona).
- Charged particles migrate to grounded collection plates.
- Plates are **rapped periodically** to dislodge collected dust into hoppers.
- Used in thermal power plants, cement kilns, steel mills.

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | HEPA Filter | ESP |
|---------|-------------|-----|
| Mechanism | Mechanical interception + diffusion | Electrostatic attraction |
| Efficiency | 99.97% @ 0.3 µm | 99.0–99.9% (varies) |
| Pressure drop | High (100–250 Pa) | Low (25–100 Pa) |
| Energy cost | High (fan energy) | Medium (electrical) |
| Particle size | Best for sub-micron | Best for 1–10 µm |
| Maintenance | Periodic replacement | Periodic rapping/wash |

---

### **Model Solution to Question B(b)**

**Step 1: Deutsch-Anderson Equation**

$$\eta = 1 - e^{-\frac{wA}{Q}}$$

**Step 2: Compute the exponent**

$$\frac{wA}{Q} = \frac{0.12 \times 300}{6} = \frac{36}{6} = 6$$

**Step 3: Evaluate the exponential**

$$e^{-6} = 0.002479$$

**Step 4: Compute efficiency**

$$\eta = 1 - 0.002479 = 0.9975 = 99.75\%$$

**Step 5: Outlet concentration calculation**

$$C_{out} = C_{in} \times (1 - \eta) = 1500 \times 0.002479 = 3.72 \text{ mg/m}^3$$

> **Final Answers:** Efficiency = **99.75%**, Outlet dust loading = **3.72 mg/m³** (well within the CPCB limit of 30 mg/m³ for industrial stack emissions).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Unit conversion errors:** Forgetting to convert CFM ↔ m³/s or mmWC ↔ Pa → lose 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Misapplying Deutsch equation:** Students often use w/Q instead of wA/Q.
> 3. **Missing the hood capture velocity selection table** in part (a) answers — examiners specifically check this.
> 4. **Not mentioning negative pressure** when explaining exhaust fan working — vital 1-mark concept.
> 5. **Confusing ESP efficiency (Deutsch)** with cyclone (Lapple) efficiency formula.
> 6. **Forgetting to convert** duct flow rate to standard CMH or CFM units in problem solutions.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📌

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — High-Yield Points for KTU 2024:**

- ✅ An **exhaust system** captures, transports, cleans, and discharges contaminated air via **negative pressure** generated by the fan.

- ✅ **Five components:** Hood → Duct → Cleaning Device → Fan → Stack.

- ✅ **LEV** captures at source (efficient); **GEV** dilutes entire room (less efficient, simpler).

- ✅ **Capture velocity** for hoods ranges from **0.25 m/s** (cold process) to **2.5 m/s** (high-velocity processes).

- ✅ **Transport velocity** in ducts must be **10–20 m/s** to prevent particle settling.

- ✅ **1 mmWC = 9.806 Pa**; **1 m³/s = 2118.88 CFM = 3600 CMH**.

- ✅ **HEPA filter** = **≥99.97%** efficiency at **0.3 µm MPPS**; classes H10–H14.

- ✅ **ESP Deutsch Equation:** $\eta = 1 - e^{-wA/Q}$ — used for **particulate** collection.

- ✅ **Cyclone Lapple Equation:** $\eta = d_{cut}^2 / (d_{cut}^2 + d^2)$ — used for **coarse** particles.

- ✅ **Scrubbers** handle **gaseous** pollutants (SO₂, HCl, NH₃) using liquid absorption.

- ✅ **Activated Carbon** removes **VOCs and odors** via adsorption.

- ✅ **UVGI** is the standard for **bioaerosol** inactivation (254 nm wavelength).

- ✅ **Hierarchy of control:** Elimination → Substitution → Engineering → Administrative → PPE.

- ✅ **Stack discharge velocity** must exceed **5 m/s** to avoid re-entrainment; height ≥ **0.5√Q** m (Q in m³/s).

- ✅ Always provide **make-up air** equal to exhaust volume to avoid building depressurization.

- ✅ **ASME N510** is the standard test for HEPA filter in-place leakage testing.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Comfort and Industrial Air Conditioning: Comfort Air Conditioning Factors, Effective Temperature, Comfort Charts, Cooling Load Estimation, Design Conditions, Supply Conditions, Fresh Air Supply, Thermodynamics of the Human Body
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPET631 - REFRIGERATION AND AIR CONDITIONING > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** Comfort and Industrial Air Conditioning: Comfort Air Conditioning Factors, Effective Temperature, Comfort Charts, Cooling Load Estimation, Design Conditions, Supply Conditions, Fresh Air Supply, Thermodynamics of the Human Body # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Comfort Air Conditioning – Formal Definition **Comfort Air Conditioning (CAC)** is the simultaneous control of **temperature**, **humidity**, **air movement**, **air purity (dust and odour removal)**, and **radiant heat** within an enclosed occupied space such that the human occupants experience a state of *thermal neutrality* — a condition where the body is in thermal equilibrium with its surroundings and no net heat is stored. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** "Comfort air conditioning deals with the conditioning of air for human comfort, requiring the simultaneous control of temperature, humidity, purity, and distribution of air to provide a comfortable and healthy environment." ## 1.2 The Six Fundamental Comfort Factors The human body's perception of thermal comfort depends on **six independent variables** that must be controlled: | # | Factor | Controlling Mechanism | Typical Value | |---|--------|----------------------|---------------| | 1 | **Dry-Bulb Temperature (DBT)** | Cooling coil / heating coil | 22 – 26 °C | | 2 | **Relative Humidity (RH)** | Cooling coil + reheat / dehumidifier | 40 – 60 % | | 3 | **Air Velocity (V)** | Fan / duct design | 0.05 – 0.5 m/s | | 4 | **Air Purity** | Filters (mechanical + activated carbon) | ≤ 0.1 mg/m³ dust | | 5 | **Radiant Heat** | Insulation, surface finishes, false ceiling | MRT ≈ DBT | | 6 | **Activity & Clothing** | Variable (occupant controlled) | 0.8 – 1.2 met | > [!NOTE] > **Comfort vs Industrial AC:** Comfort AC aims at *occupant well-being* (offices, theatres, hospitals). Industrial AC aims at *product/process quality* (pharma, textiles, electronics) where the precise control of temperature and humidity is critical for manufacturing tolerances, not human comfort. ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy – The "Swimming Pool Thermostat" Imagine a swimming pool with a thermostat. The water feels "just right" not because of one number, but because **temperature, water movement (jets), and humidity (steam rising) together** create the sensation. If you crank up the jets (air velocity) but drop the temperature, you can feel the same comfort. This is **exactly how comfort AC works**: we do not control one knob — we orchestrate *all six* factors to land on a single perception of "neither too warm nor too cold." This orchestration is quantified by the **Effective Temperature (ET)**. ## 1.4 Industrial Air Conditioning – Formal Definition **Industrial Air Conditioning** is the precise control of atmospheric conditions (temperature, humidity, and in many cases, particulate count and pressure) within a process enclosure to ensure **product quality, process efficiency, and equipment reliability** — *not* human comfort. The conditions required are often more extreme and more tightly controlled than those for human comfort. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Common Industrial Applications:** > - **Textile industry** – Yarn strength depends on RH (65 – 75 % required). > - **Pharmaceuticals** – Tablet coating, sterile zones (Class 100 / ISO 5). > - **Electronics / Cleanrooms** – 22 °C ± 1 °C, 45 % ± 5 % RH, < 100 particles/ft³. > - **Food storage** – Cold rooms at 0 – 4 °C, 85 – 95 % RH for freshness. > - **Data centres** – 18 – 27 °C, 40 – 55 % RH (ASHRAE TC 9.9). ## 1.5 The Human Body as a Thermal System > [!NOTE] > **Metabolic Heat Generation:** A sedentary adult produces roughly **Q_meta ≈ 100 W** (1 met = 58.2 W/m² of body surface area, with a typical surface area of 1.8 m²). This heat *must* be rejected to the environment; otherwise, body temperature rises by 1 °C per hour, leading to heat stroke. The body balances this metabolic heat by: - **Convection** to surrounding air - **Radiation** to surrounding surfaces - **Evaporation** of sweat (latent loss) - **Respiration** (both sensible and latent) --- # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Six Comfort Factors – Deep Dive ### 2.1.1 Dry-Bulb Temperature (DBT) - The temperature of air measured by a standard thermometer freely exposed to the air but shielded from radiation and moisture. - **Winter design:** 22 °C indoors - **Summer design:** 25 °C indoors (outdoor ~ 35 – 45 °C in Kerala context) - Controlled by: chilled-water/hot-water coils, DX coils, heating/cooling thermostats. ### 2.1.2 Relative Humidity (RH) $$RH = \frac{p_v}{p_g(T)} \times 100\,\%$$ where $p_v$ = partial pressure of water vapour in moist air and $p_g(T)$ = saturation pressure at DBT. - **Why it matters:** High RH reduces sweat evaporation → discomfort even at moderate temperature. Low RH causes static, dry skin, respiratory irritation. - **Comfort band:** 40 % ≤ RH ≤ 60 % (ASHRAE Standard 55). ### 2.1.3 Air Velocity - Influences convective heat transfer coefficient: $h_c \propto V^{0.5}$ (forced regime) or $h_c \propto V^{0.8}$ (mixed regime). - **Why some air movement is desired:** Enhances convective + evaporative cooling. **Why too much is bad:** Causes local drafts, "paper-flying" effect, perceived cold. ### 2.1.4 Air Purity - Particulate matter (PM 2.5, PM 10), VOCs, CO₂, bacteria, odours. - Controlled by **pre-filters (G3/G4) → fine filters (F7/F9) → HEPA (H13/H14)** for cleanrooms. - CO₂ concentration is a good proxy for ventilation adequacy; target **< 1000 ppm** indoors. ### 2.1.5 Radiant Heat / Mean Radiant Temperature (MRT) $$MRT = \left[\sum_{i} F_i \cdot T_i^4 \right]^{0.25}$$ where $F_i$ = view factor from person to surface *i*, $T_i$ = absolute temperature of surface *i* (Kelvin). - A hot ceiling or cold window shifts the radiant balance drastically. **Operative temperature** $T_o$ accounts for this: $$T_o = A \cdot T_{DBT} + (1-A)\cdot MRT$$ where $A$ depends on air velocity (≈ 0.5 for still air, → 0.6 for $V = 0.5$ m/s). ### 2.1.6 Activity & Clothing (Occupant Factors) - **Metabolic rate** in *met* units: 1 met = 58.2 W/m² (seated quietly). - **Clothing insulation** in *clo* units: 1 clo = 0.155 m²·K/W. - Typical office: 1.2 met, 0.5 – 1.0 clo. ## 2.2 Effective Temperature (ET) – The Original Comfort Index ### 2.2.1 Definition **Effective Temperature (ET)** is the temperature of *still, saturated air* which produces the *same sensation of warmth* on the human body as the actual combination of dry-bulb temperature, humidity, and air velocity under consideration. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Equal Sensation Principle:** Any two air conditions lying on the same ET line produce *identical* thermal sensation for a clothed, sedentary occupant. This is the foundation of comfort-chart-based design. ### 2.2.2 Why ET Was Modified → ET*, ET*, SET* - Original ET (Houghton & Yaglou, 1923) was based on subjects in still saturated air — overestimated humidity effect. - **Corrected Effective Temperature (ET*)** introduced by Gagge in 1971 uses a reference of *still air at 50 % RH*. - **Standard Effective Temperature (SET*)** – modern ASHRAE 55 index, decouples clothing/activity and uses a two-node physiological model. ### 2.2.3 ASHRAE Comfort Zone (Standard 55-2023) A two-dimensional region on a psychrometric chart defined by: - Operative temperature 20 – 26 °C (winter → summer) - RH 30 – 60 % (approximately) - Air velocity up to 0.2 m/s (or up to 0.8 m/s with elevated air movement in summer) - 0.5 – 1.0 clo winter, 0.5 clo summer. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Master Reference) | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---------|-------------|-------| | 1 | $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$ | Heat transfer through building element | W | | 2 | $Q = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Sensible heat from mass flow | W | | 3 | $Q = m \cdot h_{fg}$ | Latent heat from moisture addition | W | | 4 | $Q_s = 1.08 \cdot CFM \cdot \Delta T$ | Sensible heat (English units) | Btu/h | | 5 | $Q_l = 0.68 \cdot CFM \cdot \Delta W$ | Latent heat (English units) | Btu/h | | 6 | $SHF = \dfrac{Q_s}{Q_s + Q_l}$ | Sensible Heat Factor | dimensionless | | 7 | $Q_{people} = q_s + q_l$ (per person) | Heat from occupants | W | | 8 | $Q_{vent} = \rho \cdot \dot{V} \cdot (h_o - h_i)$ | Ventilation (infiltration) load | W | | 9 | $RSHF = \dfrac{Room\,Sensible}{Room\,Total}$ | Room Sensible Heat Factor | dimensionless | | 10 | $m_a = \dfrac{Q_s}{c_p \cdot (T_i - T_s)}$ | Supply air mass flow | kg/s | | 11 | $T_o = A \cdot T_{DBT} + (1-A) \cdot MRT$ | Operative temperature | °C | | 12 | $q_{meta} = 58.2 \cdot met \cdot A_D$ | Metabolic rate (DuBois area) | W | | 13 | $A_D = 0.202 \cdot m^{0.425} \cdot h^{0.725}$ | DuBois body surface area | m² | | 14 | $CFM = \dfrac{Q_s}{1.08 \cdot \Delta T}$ | CFM from sensible heat | CFM | | 15 | $Q = K \cdot A \cdot CLTD$ | CLTD method (cooling load) | W | > [!NOTE] > **Conversion:** 1 m/s = 196.85 CFM/m², 1 kW = 3412.14 Btu/h, 1 TR = 3.517 kW = 12000 Btu/h. ## 2.4 Application Significance - **Building HVAC design:** All six factors + occupant profile determine the cooling load and hence equipment tonnage. - **Energy efficiency:** Loose control of any factor (e.g., over-humidification) inflates latent load and compressor power. - **Industrial process:** A 1 °C deviation in a cleanroom can ruin a wafer batch worth millions of rupees; a 5 % RH deviation in a textile mill reduces yarn tensile strength by ~ 8 %. --- # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Human Heat Balance Equation ### 3.1.1 First-Principles Energy Balance on a Human Body The body is an open thermodynamic system. At **steady state**, the net rate of energy storage is zero: $$Q_{meta} - Q_{loss} = 0$$ Expanding $Q_{loss}$ into the four physiological pathways: $$Q_{meta} = Q_{conv} + Q_{rad} + Q_{evap} + Q_{resp}$$ where: - $Q_{conv}$ = convective heat loss to surrounding air - $Q_{rad}$ = radiative heat exchange with surroundings - $Q_{evap}$ = evaporative loss from skin (sweat) - $Q_{resp}$ = total respiratory loss (latent + sensible) ### 3.1.2 Component Expressions **Convective loss:** $$Q_{conv} = h_c \cdot A_D \cdot (T_{skin} - T_{DBT})$$ **Radiative loss:** $$Q_{rad} = h_r \cdot A_D \cdot (T_{skin} - MRT)$$ with $h_r = 4 \cdot \varepsilon \cdot \sigma \cdot \bar{T}^3$ (linearised radiation coefficient, $\bar{T} \approx$ 305 K). **Evaporative loss from skin:** $$Q_{evap,skin} = h_e \cdot A_D \cdot (p_{skin,sat} - p_v)$$ **Respiratory loss** (latent + sensible combined): $$Q_{resp} = 0.0023 \cdot M \cdot (h_{ex} - h_{i}) \quad \text{[W]}$$ where $M$ = metabolic rate (W), $h_{ex}$ = enthalpy of exhaled air (saturated at 34 °C), $h_i$ = enthalpy of inhaled air. ### 3.1.3 Closed-Form Heat Balance Substituting back: $$Q_{meta} = A_D \left[ h_c (T_{skin}-T_{DBT}) + h_r (T_{skin}-MRT) + h_e (p_{skin,sat}-p_v) \right] + Q_{resp}$$ > [!NOTE] > At **thermal neutrality**, $T_{skin} \approx$ 33.3 °C, sweat rate = 0, and the only working term is the air-side heat balance. This is the design point for comfort AC. ## 3.2 Derivation of Cooling Load (Sensible Heat, $Q_s$) Consider a space with sensible heat gain $Q_s$ (W). Air enters at supply temperature $T_s$ and leaves at room temperature $T_i$: $$Q_s = \dot{m}_a \cdot c_p \cdot (T_i - T_s)$$ Solving for mass flow rate: $$\dot{m}_a = \frac{Q_s}{c_p \cdot (T_i - T_s)}$$ In English units (commonly used in Indian HVAC practice): $$\boxed{CFM = \frac{Q_s\,[\text{Btu/h}]}{1.08 \cdot (T_i - T_s)\,[^\circ\text{F}]}}$$ **Derivation of the 1.08 constant:** $$1.08 = 0.018 \cdot 60 = \frac{\rho \cdot c_p \cdot 60}{V_{cfm}}$$ using $\rho = 0.075$ lb/ft³, $c_p = 0.24$ Btu/lb·°F, $V_{cfm} = 1$ ft³/min: $$1.08 = \frac{0.075 \times 0.24 \times 60}{1}$$ ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix – CLTD Cooling Load Method | Load Component | Formula | Variable Definitions | Notes | |---|---|---|---| | **Roof/Wall conduction** | $Q = U \cdot A \cdot CLTD$ | $U$ = W/m²K, $A$ = m², $CLTD$ = °C | CLTD table = function of orientation, month, time | | **Glass conduction + radiation** | $Q = U \cdot A \cdot CLTD + SHGF \cdot A \cdot SC$ | $SHGF$ = W/m², $SC$ = shading coeff. | SHGF from ASHRAE tables | | **Partition, ceiling, floor** | $Q = U \cdot A \cdot (T_i - T_o)$ | $T_o$ = adjacent space temp | For unconditioned zones | | **People (sensible)** | $Q_{s,p} = q_s \cdot N \cdot CLF$ | $N$ = no. of people, $CLF$ = cooling load factor | Office $q_s \approx$ 75 W; restaurant 90 W | | **People (latent)** | $Q_{l,p} = q_l \cdot N$ | | Office $q_l \approx$ 55 W | | **Lights** | $Q = W \cdot F_{ul} \cdot F_{sa} \cdot CLF$ | $W$ = watts, $F_{ul}$ = use factor, $F_{sa}$ = special allowance | Inductive ballast adds 20 % | | **Equipment (sensible)** | $Q = W \cdot F_{ul} \cdot F_{sa} \cdot CLF$ | | Motor in air stream = $W \cdot 0.25$ | | **Equipment (latent)** | $Q = W \cdot F_{ul}$ (for steam, open water) | | | | **Infiltration** | $Q = \dot{V} \cdot \rho \cdot (h_o - h_i)$ | $\dot{V}$ = volume flow m³/s | Sensible + latent combined | | **Ventilation** | $Q = \dot{V} \cdot \rho \cdot (h_o - h_i)$ | Minimum $\dot{V}$ = 8.5 L/s per person (ASHRAE 62.1) | | ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU 14-Mark Style) **Problem:** A conference room has the following data: - Dimensions: 10 m × 8 m × 3 m, located in Kerala (Kochi) - Indoor design: 25 °C DBT, 50 % RH - Outdoor design: 35 °C DBT, 28 °C WBT - 30 occupants (seated, light work) - Lighting: 2 kW total, on throughout occupied period - Windows: 6 m² single glass, U = 5.8 W/m²K, SHGF = 380 W/m², SC = 0.95 - Walls + roof: combined $U \cdot A$ = 280 W/K, CLTD = 11 °C - Ventilation: 12 L/s per person of outside air **Step 1: Infiltration + Ventilation Load** $$\dot{V}_{vent} = 30 \times 0.012 = 0.36 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$ From psychrometric chart: $h_o$ (35 °C, 28 °C WBT) ≈ 84.0 kJ/kg, $h_i$ (25 °C, 50 % RH) ≈ 50.3 kJ/kg. $$Q_{vent} = \rho \dot{V} (h_o - h_i) = 1.2 \times 0.36 \times (84.0 - 50.3) \times 10^3$$ $$Q_{vent} = 1.2 \times 0.36 \times 33700 = 14563 \text{ W} \approx 14.56 \text{ kW}$$ Assume no infiltration (tight envelope) → use 14.56 kW. **Step 2: Envelope Load** $$Q_{envelope} = 280 \times 11 = 3080 \text{ W} = 3.08 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 3: Glass Load** $$Q_{glass} = (5.8 \times 6 \times 11) + (380 \times 6 \times 0.95) = 382.8 + 2166 = 2549 \text{ W} = 2.55 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 4: Occupant Load** From table: Office seated, 25 °C → $q_s = 75$ W, $q_l = 55$ W per person. $$Q_{s,p} = 30 \times 75 = 2250 \text{ W} = 2.25 \text{ kW (sensible)}$$ $$Q_{l,p} = 30 \times 55 = 1650 \text{ W} = 1.65 \text{ kW (latent)}$$ **Step 5: Lighting Load** $$Q_{light} = 2000 \times 1.0 \times 1.0 = 2000 \text{ W} = 2.00 \text{ kW (sensible)}$$ **Step 6: Total Loads** $$Q_{sensible} = 14.56_{vent,s} + 3.08 + 2.55 + 2.25 + 2.00 \approx 24.4 \text{ kW}$$ (Of the 14.56 kW ventilation, the sensible part is $\approx \rho \dot V c_p \Delta T = 1.2 \times 0.36 \times 1.005 \times 10 \times 1000 = 4.34$ kW; latent = 14.56 − 4.34 = 10.22 kW.) $$Q_{latent} = 10.22 + 1.65 = 11.87 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 7: Grand Total** $$Q_{total} = 24.4 + 11.87 = 36.27 \text{ kW} \approx 10.3 \text{ TR}$$ (Using 1 TR = 3.517 kW.) **Step 8: Room Sensible Heat Factor (RSHF)** $$RSHF = \frac{24.4}{36.27} = 0.673$$ > [!NOTE] > The **SHF (= RSHF for constant-volume single-zone systems)** selects the supply air state on the psychrometric chart. Here, RSHF = 0.673 falls between 0.7 and 0.8, indicating a need for reheat or bypass to avoid overcooling the supply air below the apparatus dew-point requirements. **Step 9: Supply Air Quantity (English Units)** Convert: $Q_s = 24.4$ kW $= 24.4 \times 3412 = 83253$ Btu/h. Assume $T_i - T_s = 10$ °C = 18 °F: $$CFM = \frac{83253}{1.08 \times 18} = 4283 \text{ CFM}$$ $$\dot{V} = \frac{4283}{2118.88} = 2.02 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 7280 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}$$ (using 1 CFM = 1.7 m³/h conversion: actually 1 CFM = 0.000472 m³/s; 4283 CFM × 0.000472 = 2.02 m³/s ✓) --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of a Comfort AC System ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Outdoor Air] --> B[Pre-Filter G3 G4] B --> C[Mixing Chamber with Return Air] C --> D[Cooling and Dehumidifying Coil] D --> E[Eliminator Plates] E --> F[Reheat Coil for SHF Control] F --> G[Humidifier or Steam Injection] G --> H[Supply Fan] H --> I[Supply Duct with Diffusers] I --> J[Conditioned Space with Occupants] J --> K[Return Air Grille] K --> L[Return Fan] L --> C D -. heat rejection .-> M[Condenser] M -. heat to atmosphere .-> N[Outside] classDef process fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc,color:#003366 classDef airside fill:#d4edda,stroke:#28a745,color:#155724 classDef reject fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,color:#721c24 class A,B,C,E,F,G,H,I,J,K,L airside class D,M process class N reject ``` ## 4.2 Human Body Heat Balance Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph INPUT[Heat Generation] META[Metabolic Heat Qmeta
100 to 400 W] end subgraph OUTPUT[Heat Rejection Pathways] CONV[Convection Qconv
about 30 percent] RAD[Radiation Qrad
about 45 percent] EVAP[Evaporation of Sweat Qevap
about 20 percent] RESP[Respiration Qresp
about 5 percent] end subgraph ENVI[Environment] AIR[Air at Tdb RH V] SURF[Surfaces at MRT] end META --> CONV META --> RAD META --> EVAP META --> RESP AIR -->|h_c dT| CONV SURF -->|h_r dT| RAD AIR -->|h_e dp| EVAP AIR -->|inhalation exhalation| RESP classDef gen fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#856404 classDef rej fill:#d1ecf1,stroke:#0c5460,color:#0c5460 classDef env fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,color:#721c24 class META gen class CONV,RAD,EVAP,RESP rej class AIR,SURF env ``` ## 4.3 Cooling Load Estimation Workflow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Step 1: Gather Building Data
Geometry, Orientation, Construction] --> B[Step 2: Set Design Conditions
Outdoor and Indoor DBT WBT RH] B --> C[Step 3: Calculate Envelope Loads
Roof, Walls, Glass using CLTD SHGF] C --> D[Step 4: Calculate Internal Loads
People, Lights, Equipment] D --> E[Step 5: Calculate Infiltration and Ventilation Loads] E --> F[Step 6: Apply CLF and Diversity Factors] F --> G[Step 7: Sum Sensible Qs and Latent Ql Separately] G --> H[Step 8: Compute RSHF and Grand Total] H --> I[Step 9: Compute Supply Air Quantity CFM] I --> J[Step 10: Locate Supply State on Psychrometric Chart] classDef step fill:#e2e3e5,stroke:#383d41,color:#383d41 classDef key fill:#d4edda,stroke:#28a745,color:#155724 class A,B,C,D,E,F step class G,H,I,J key ``` ## 4.4 Comfort Chart Mapping on Psychrometric Chart ```mermaid flowchart LR A[ASHRAE Comfort Zone 2023] --> B[Summer Zone
22 to 26 C, 30 to 60 percent RH] A --> C[Winter Zone
20 to 24 C, 30 to 60 percent RH] B --> D[Operative Temperature Axis] B --> E[Humidity Ratio Axis] D --> F[Air Velocity Lines 0.05 to 0.5 m per s] E --> G[ET and SET Star Lines] classDef zone fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#052c65 classDef axis fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107,color:#664d03 classDef param fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,color:#721c24 class A,B,C zone class D,E axis class F,G param ``` --- # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1.** Define **Effective Temperature (ET)**. Why was the *Corrected Effective Temperature (ET*)* introduced? `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Model Answer:** > **Effective Temperature** is the temperature of *still, saturated air* which produces the *same sensation of warmth* on the human body as the actual combination of dry-bulb temperature, humidity, and air velocity. > > **Why ET* was introduced:** The original ET (Houghton & Yaglou, 1923) used saturated still air as reference, which **overestimated the effect of humidity**. ET* uses a reference of *still air at 50 % RH*, giving a more realistic, physiologically correct mapping of equal sensation. ET* also incorporates clothing and activity levels (0.6 – 1.4 met and 0.5 – 1.0 clo) for greater accuracy. --- ### **Q2.** State **ASHRAE Standard 62.1** requirements for minimum outdoor air supply in an office space. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer:** ASHRAE 62.1 specifies the **minimum outdoor air flow rate** based on two components — *people-based* and *area-based* — and the design value is the **larger** of the two: $$\dot{V}_{oa} = \dot{V}_{per\,person} \cdot N_{occ} + \dot{V}_{per\,area} \cdot A_{floor}$$ For a typical office: - People component: **8.5 L/s per person** - Area component: **0.6 L/s per m² of floor area** - Target indoor CO₂: **≤ 1000 ppm** (above outdoor ≈ 400 ppm). > [!NOTE] > The **two-component** requirement ensures that even a sparsely occupied large room still receives a baseline outdoor air flush for dilution of building-emitted pollutants (VOCs, formaldehyde from furnishings). --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) – Module Internal Choice --- ### **Question A (14 Marks):** Cooling Load Estimation Problem `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` **(a)** [7 Marks] A classroom of size 8 m × 6 m × 3 m, located at Trivandrum, has the following data: - Indoor: 25 °C DBT, 50 % RH - Outdoor: 34 °C DBT, 27 °C WBT - 50 students (sedentary, light work) - 1.5 kW fluorescent lighting - 1 window 4 m², single glass, U = 5.8 W/m²K, SHGF = 360 W/m², SC = 0.95 - Walls + roof: $UA = 220$ W/K, CLTD = 10 °C - Ventilation: 10 L/s per person of outdoor air Estimate the **sensible and latent cooling loads**. **Model Solution:** **Step 1: Ventilation load** (sensible and latent combined) - $\dot{V} = 50 \times 0.010 = 0.5 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$ - From psychrometric chart: $h_o \approx 81$ kJ/kg, $h_i \approx 50$ kJ/kg - $Q_{vent,total} = 1.2 \times 0.5 \times (81-50) \times 1000 = 18600$ W = **18.6 kW** Splitting: - $Q_{vent,s} = 1.2 \times 0.5 \times 1.005 \times (34-25) \times 1000 = 5427$ W = **5.43 kW** - $Q_{vent,l} = 18.6 - 5.43 = 13.17$ kW **Step 2: Envelope load** $$Q_{env} = 220 \times 10 = 2200 \text{ W} = 2.20 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 3: Glass load** $$Q_{glass} = (5.8 \times 4 \times 10) + (360 \times 4 \times 0.95)$$ $$= 232 + 1368 = 1600 \text{ W} = 1.60 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 4: Occupant load (at 25 °C sedentary)** - $q_s = 70$ W, $q_l = 55$ W per person - $Q_{s,p} = 50 \times 70 = 3500$ W = **3.50 kW** - $Q_{l,p} = 50 \times 55 = 2750$ W = **2.75 kW** **Step 5: Lighting load (with inductive ballast 1.2 multiplier)** $$Q_{light} = 1500 \times 1.2 = 1800 \text{ W} = 1.80 \text{ kW (sensible)}$$ **Step 6: Totals** $$\boxed{Q_s = 5.43 + 2.20 + 1.60 + 3.50 + 1.80 = 14.53 \text{ kW sensible}}$$ $$\boxed{Q_l = 13.17 + 2.75 = 15.92 \text{ kW latent}}$$ $$\boxed{Q_{total} = 30.45 \text{ kW} \approx 8.66 \text{ TR}}$$ **(b)** [7 Marks] Determine the **supply air CFM** and **RSHF** for the above room, given $T_i - T_s = 10$ °C. **Model Solution:** $$Q_s = 14.53 \text{ kW} = 14.53 \times 3412 = 49576 \text{ Btu/h}$$ $$CFM = \frac{49576}{1.08 \times 18} = 2550 \text{ CFM} \approx 1.20 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$ $$\boxed{CFM \approx 2550 \text{ CFM}}$$ $$\boxed{RSHF = \frac{14.53}{30.45} = 0.477}$$ > [!NOTE] > RSHF < 0.5 means latent load dominates — typical for **humid tropical climates** like Kerala. A **higher by-pass factor coil** or **reheat coil** is recommended to handle the large latent component without overcooling the sensible load. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks):** Thermodynamics of Human Body & Effective Temperature `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` **(a)** [7 Marks] Derive the **human heat balance equation** and explain each term. For a sedentary office worker, estimate the **metabolic heat generation** given body weight = 70 kg, height = 1.72 m, and activity level = 1.2 met. **Model Solution:** **Heat Balance Equation:** $$Q_{meta} = Q_{conv} + Q_{rad} + Q_{evap,skin} + Q_{resp}$$ **Component breakdown:** | Term | Physical meaning | |------|-----------------| | $Q_{conv}$ | Convective heat loss to surrounding air, $\propto h_c (T_{skin}-T_{DBT})$ | | $Q_{rad}$ | Radiative exchange with surrounding surfaces, $\propto h_r (T_{skin}-MRT)$ | | $Q_{evap,skin}$ | Latent loss from sweat evaporation, $\propto h_e (p_{skin,sat}-p_v)$ | | $Q_{resp}$ | Latent + sensible loss via breathing | **Numerical estimation:** DuBois body surface area: $$A_D = 0.202 \cdot m^{0.425} \cdot h^{0.725} = 0.202 \cdot 70^{0.425} \cdot 1.72^{0.725}$$ $$A_D = 0.202 \times 8.103 \times 1.541 = 1.823 \text{ m}^2$$ Metabolic heat: $$Q_{meta} = 58.2 \times met \times A_D = 58.2 \times 1.2 \times 1.823$$ $$\boxed{Q_{meta} = 127.3 \text{ W}}$$ > [!NOTE] > This is consistent with ASHRAE tables (1.2 met seated work = ~ 75 W/m² × 1.82 m² ≈ 137 W). The small difference is due to rounded reference values. **(b)** [7 Marks] Discuss the **significance of Effective Temperature** and explain how **ASHRAP comfort chart 2023** differs from the original ET chart. List the **six primary factors** affecting human comfort. **Model Answer:** **Significance of ET:** 1. Combines three primary air-side variables (DBT, RH, V) into a **single index of sensation**. 2. Enables design of air conditioning systems that achieve **equal thermal comfort** across different combinations of T and RH. 3. Foundation for psychrometric chart-based system design. **Comparison:** | Aspect | Original ET (1923) | ASHRAE 55-2023 (SET*) | |--------|-------------------|----------------------| | Reference air | Still, saturated | Still, 50 % RH | | Humidity weighting | Over-estimated | Calibrated to physiology | | Personal factors | Neglected | Clo, met inputs required | | Application | Heating AC | Modern universal design | | Index range | 0 – 100 °F ET | Operative temperature + humidity ratio | **Six Comfort Factors:** 1. **Dry-bulb temperature** (air temperature) 2. **Relative humidity** (moisture content) 3. **Air velocity** (motion) 4. **Air purity** (dust, CO₂, VOCs) 5. **Radiant heat** (MRT) 6. **Activity & clothing** (metabolic and insulation) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students confuse **ET** and **SET***. The original ET is **NOT** a temperature — it is a *sensation index*. Using ET as a temperature for coil sizing is a common 2–3 mark deduction. Always state "index of thermal sensation" or "sensation-equivalent temperature" in definitions. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist (must memorise before exam):** - **Comfort factors = 6** (T, RH, V, purity, radiation, activity/clothing) — know all six by name and typical range. - **ET definition:** temperature of *still saturated air* giving the *same sensation* as actual conditions. - **ET\* reference:** *still air at 50 % RH*; SET\* is the modern ASHRAE index. - **ASHRAE comfort zone:** Operative temperature 20 – 26 °C, RH 30 – 60 %, V ≤ 0.2 m/s (still), 0.5 – 1.0 clo. - **Metabolic rate:** 1 met = 58.2 W/m²; sedentary office ≈ 1.2 met → ~ 70 W/m². - **DuBois area:** $A_D = 0.202\,m^{0.425} h^{0.725}$ (m in kg, h in m). - **Sensible heat (CFM):** $CFM = Q_s / (1.08 \cdot \Delta T)$ where $Q_s$ in Btu/h, $\Delta T$ in °F. - **Latent heat (CFM):** $CFM = Q_l / (0.68 \cdot \Delta W)$ where $\Delta W$ in grains/lb. - **RSHF:** $Q_s / (Q_s + Q_l)$; > 0.7 → sensible-dominated; < 0.5 → latent-dominated (typical of Kerala). - **CLTD method** components: roof, wall, glass, partition, ceiling, floor, people, lights, equipment, infiltration, ventilation. - **ASHRAE 62.1** fresh air: 8.5 L/s per person + 0.6 L/s per m² (office), whichever is greater. - **Industrial AC** is process-driven, not human-driven — recall textile, pharma, cleanroom examples. - **Heat balance** of the body: $Q_{meta} = Q_{conv} + Q_{rad} + Q_{evap} + Q_{resp}$ — write all four terms in derivations. - **Operative temperature:** $T_o = A \cdot T_{DBT} + (1-A) \cdot MRT$, $A \approx 0.5$ for still air. - **Solar Heat Gain Factor (SHGF)** and **Shading Coefficient (SC)** are *not* the same: $SC$ = ratio of solar gain of given glass to that of 3 mm clear glass; $SHGF$ is the maximum solar gain through that glass at a given location and time. - **Sweat evaporation** is the body's *only* variable heat loss mechanism — that is why humid climates feel hotter. - **Reheat coils** are used when RSHF < SHF (i.e., space humidity is low relative to coil latent capacity), not when humidity is high. - **Cleanroom grades:** ISO 5 / Class 100 = 3520 particles/m³ of size 0.5 µm; ISO 7 / Class 10000 = 352000 particles/m³. - **1 TR = 3.517 kW = 12000 Btu/h** — use this exact number in ton-of-refrigeration conversions. > [!WARNING] > **Most-Failed Concepts in KTU Exams:** > 1. Mixing up **CFM** and **m³/s** conversions (1 CFM = 0.0004719 m³/s). > 2. Forgetting the **1.2 multiplier for inductive lighting ballast** (loss factor). > 3. Confusing **SHF (apparatus)** with **RSHF (room)** — they are equal *only* for a single-zone CAV system with no return-air bypass. > 4. Applying **CLTD** values to floor/ceiling loads, which use **simple $\Delta T$** (no CLTD). > 5. Forgetting to convert **grains/lb to kg/kg** when using SI latent heat equations. ---

topics.title: Applications of Air Conditioning: Room Air Conditioners, Split Systems, Packaged Systems, All-Air Systems, Chilled Water Systems, Winter and Summer Air Conditioning, Heating Systems, Humidifiers, Year-Round AC System and Controls (Thermostat, Humidistat), Air Distribution Systems (Duct Design)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPET631 - REFRIGERATION AND AIR CONDITIONING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Applications of Air Conditioning: Room Air Conditioners, Split Systems, Packaged Systems, All-Air Systems, Chilled Water Systems, Winter and Summer Air Conditioning, Heating Systems, Humidifiers, Year-Round AC System and Controls (Thermostat, Humidistat), Air Distribution Systems (Duct Design) # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Air Conditioning — Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Air Conditioning (AC)** is the simultaneous control of **Temperature, Humidity, Air Motion, Air Distribution, Air Cleanliness, Sound Level, and Pressurization** within a conditioned space, to ensure the thermal comfort of occupants and/or the proper functioning of industrial processes. The acronym **THASPP** (Temperature, Humidity, Air-flow, Sound, Purity, Pressure) is the standard KTU-recommended way to remember all seven sub-functions of AC. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Human Skin Think of an air conditioning system as an **artificial skin** for a building. Just as your body sweats to cool down, shivers to warm up, and breathes to regulate oxygen and moisture, an AC system: - **Cools** the air (sweating = evaporation of refrigerant) - **Heats** the air (shivering = heating coil firing) - **Humidifies/Dehumidifies** (breathing = adding/removing moisture) - **Filters** the air (skin filters dust) - **Distributes** air evenly (blood circulation) The **thermostat** acts as the **brain's hypothalamus** (sensing temperature), and the **humidistat** acts as the **osmoreceptors** (sensing moisture). ## 1.3 Classification of Air Conditioning Systems | Category | Sub-Type | Application | |----------|----------|-------------| | **Unitary Systems** | Room AC (Window), Split AC | Single rooms, small offices | | **Central Systems** | Packaged, Chilled Water, All-Air | Large buildings, malls, hospitals | | **Process Systems** | Year-Round AC, Precision AC | Industries, data centers, pharma | ## 1.4 Detailed Sub-Topic Overview ### A. Room Air Conditioners (Window AC) A **self-contained unit** where compressor, condenser, expansion device, and evaporator are housed in a single cabinet, typically installed in a window or wall opening. Capacity: **0.75 TR to 2 TR** (1 TR = 3.517 kW). ### B. Split Air Conditioners Composed of **two units**: - **Indoor Unit (IDU):** Mounted inside the room; contains evaporator + blower. - **Outdoor Unit (ODU):** Mounted outside; contains compressor + condenser. Connected by **refrigerant copper tubing** and electrical wiring. Capacity: **0.75 TR to 5 TR**. ### C. Packaged Air Conditioners A larger self-contained unit (compressor, condenser, evaporator) usually installed on **rooftops or floors** with a network of ducts supplying air to multiple rooms. Capacity: **3 TR to 100 TR**. ### D. All-Air Systems Systems where the entire cooling/heating load is carried by **air only** (no water in coils). The air acts as the sole medium for transporting energy. Examples: **Single Duct, Dual Duct, Multi-Zone, Terminal Reheat**. ### E. Chilled Water (Water) Systems Systems where **chilled water** (typically at **6.7°C to 7.2°C** supply and **12.2°C to 12.8°C** return) circulates from a central chiller to **Air Handling Units (AHUs)** or **Fan Coil Units (FCUs)**. Preferred for large installations above **100 TR**. ### F. Summer, Winter, and Year-Round Air Conditioning - **Summer AC:** Provides cooling + dehumidification. - **Winter AC:** Provides heating + humidification. - **Year-Round AC:** Combines both via automatic switching (reversing valve in heat pumps, or separate heating/cooling coils). ### G. Humidifiers Devices that add **moisture** to the air. Types: **Pan type, Spray type, Steam type, Wetted element, and Ultrasonic**. ### H. Controls — Thermostat & Humidistat - **Thermostat:** A temperature-sensing switch (bimetallic, bellows, or electronic) that cycles the compressor/heater ON/OFF at set points. - **Humidistat:** A moisture-sensing switch that controls the humidifier/dehumidifier. ### I. Air Distribution Systems & Duct Design A network of **sheet metal ducts** distributing conditioned air through **supply outlets (diffusers/grilles)** and collecting return air via **return grilles**. Design involves selecting duct sizes based on air velocity, friction loss, and noise. > [!NOTE] > **Standard reference air velocity in main ducts:** 5–8 m/s; in branch ducts: 3–5 m/s; at supply outlets: 2–3 m/s (to minimize noise). ## 1.5 Standard Physical Constants (KTU 2024) - **1 TR (Ton of Refrigeration)** = **3.517 kW** = **3024 kcal/hr** - **Specific heat of air (cp)** = **1.005 kJ/kg·K** - **Specific volume of air** = **0.842 m³/kg** (at standard conditions) - **Latent heat of vaporization of water (hfg)** = **2501 kJ/kg** at 0°C - **Universal gas constant for air (R)** = **0.287 kJ/kg·K** - **Standard atmospheric pressure** = **101.325 kPa** # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Room Air Conditioners (Window AC) — Working Principle The window AC operates on the **vapor compression cycle**: 1. **Compression:** Low-pressure refrigerant vapor is compressed to high-pressure, high-temperature vapor. 2. **Condensation:** Heat is rejected to outdoor air by the condenser fan blowing ambient air across the condenser coil. 3. **Expansion:** High-pressure liquid passes through the capillary tube/expansion valve, dropping to low pressure and low temperature. 4. **Evaporation:** Low-temperature liquid absorbs heat from indoor air (passed by the evaporator blower) and evaporates. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Exam Tip:** The **evaporator is on the indoor side** and the **condenser is on the outdoor side**. This is the standard rule for any cooling-mode AC. ## 2.2 Split System AC — Advantages Over Window AC | Feature | Window AC | Split AC | |---------|-----------|----------| | Noise | Higher (compressor indoors) | Very low (compressor outdoors) | | Aesthetic | Bulky | Sleek, indoor unit slim | | Installation | Requires window/wall cut | Only a 3-inch hole | | Capacity range | 0.75–2 TR | 0.75–5 TR | | Energy efficiency | Lower EER | Higher EER (Inverter tech available) | ## 2.3 Packaged Air Conditioner — Components - **Hermetic/Scroll Compressor** - **Air-Cooled Condenser** (with propeller fans) - **Evaporator Coil** (DX or chilled water type) - **Centrifugal Blower** (high-static, ducted) - **Filters** (Pre-filters, EU filters) - **Controls** (Thermostat, safety cutouts) > [!NOTE] > **Variants:** (a) Roof-Top Package Unit, (b) Floor-Mounted Package Unit, (c) Water-Cooled Package Unit. ## 2.4 All-Air Systems — Sub-Classification | Type | Description | Use | |------|-------------|-----| | **Single Duct** | Single supply duct with CAV or VAV boxes | Small offices | | **Dual Duct** | Two parallel ducts (hot deck + cold deck) with mixing box | Hospitals, labs | | **Multi-Zone** | Multiple dampers in AHU serve different zones with single duct | Buildings with 2–8 zones | | **Terminal Reheat** | Cold air from main duct reheated at zone level | Hospitals, cleanrooms | ## 2.5 Chilled Water System — Components and Cycle **Components:** Chiller (compressor + condenser + evaporator), **Chilled Water Pump (CHWP)**, **Condenser Water Pump (CWP)**, **Cooling Tower (CT)**, **AHU/FCU**, **Expansion Tank**, **Strainers**, and **3-way/2-way control valves**. **Cycle path:** > Chiller → Supply CHW (6.7°C) → AHU/FCU coil → Absorbs heat → Return CHW (12.2°C) → Back to chiller **Condenser water loop:** > Chiller condenser → Hot water (35°C) → Cooling tower → Cooled water (29°C) → Back to chiller ## 2.6 Humidification — Theory The air's moisture content is measured by: - **Specific Humidity (W):** kg of water vapor per kg of dry air. - **Relative Humidity (φ):** Ratio of actual partial pressure to saturation pressure at the dry-bulb temperature. **Psychrometric relationship:** $$W = 0.622 \times \frac{p_w}{P - p_w}$$ where $p_w$ = partial pressure of water vapor and $P$ = total atmospheric pressure. For **isothermal humidification** (no temperature change), the **sensible heat factor (SHF) = 0**, and for **adiabatic humidification** (typical spray-type), the process follows the **wet-bulb line** on the psychrometric chart. ## 2.7 Heating Systems in AC | Heating Method | Energy Source | Application | |----------------|---------------|-------------| | **Electric resistance coil** | Electricity | Small split ACs | | **Hot water coil** | Boiler/heat pump | Central plants | | **Steam coil** | Boiler | Hospitals, industrial | | **Heat pump (reversing valve)** | Refrigeration cycle reversal | Year-round AC | | **Furnace (gas/oil)** | Combustion | Cold climates | **Heat added by heating coil:** $$Q_h = \dot{m} \times c_p \times \Delta T$$ where $\dot{m}$ = mass flow rate of air (kg/s) and $c_p$ = 1.005 kJ/kg·K. ## 2.8 Thermostat — Working A **bimetallic strip** thermostat uses two metals with different coefficients of thermal expansion bonded together. As temperature changes, the strip bends and makes/breaks an electrical contact. **Types of Thermostats:** 1. **Bimetallic** — Mechanical 2. **Bellows** — Uses vapor-pressure bulb (most common in AC) 3. **Electronic (NTC thermistor)** — Modern digital systems 4. **Programmable/Smart** — IoT-enabled **Thermostat Differential (D):** The gap between cut-in and cut-out temperature. Typical: **1°C to 2°C** for comfort AC. ## 2.9 Humidistat — Working Uses a **hygroscopic material** (human/animal hair, nylon, or polymer film) that expands/contracts with humidity. This movement operates a snap-action switch. ## 2.10 Duct Design — Fundamental Theory Air flowing through ducts loses energy due to **friction** (along length) and **dynamic losses** (fittings, bends, dampers, contractions, expansions). **Core Principles:** 1. **Continuity Equation:** $Q = A \times V$ (volumetric flow = area × velocity) 2. **Bernoulli's Equation:** Total pressure = Static pressure + Velocity pressure 3. **Friction Loss:** Governed by Darcy-Weisbach equation ## 2.11 KTU Formula Sheet (Duct Design + Applications) | S.No. | Formula | Description | Units | |-------|---------|-------------|-------| | 1 | $Q = A \times V$ | Air quantity | m³/s | | 2 | $D_e = 1.30 \frac{(a \times b)^{0.625}}{(a+b)^{0.25}}$ | Equivalent diameter of rectangular duct | m | | 3 | $\frac{\Delta P_f}{L} = \frac{f}{D_e} \times \frac{\rho V^2}{2}$ | Friction loss per unit length (Equal Friction Method) | Pa/m | | 4 | $V = \frac{Q}{A}$ | Air velocity | m/s | | 5 | $P_v = \frac{\rho V^2}{2}$ | Velocity pressure | Pa | | 6 | $\dot{m} = \rho \times Q$ | Mass flow rate of air | kg/s | | 7 | $Q_h = \dot{m} \times c_p \times \Delta T$ | Heating/cooling coil load | kW | | 8 | $Q_m = \dot{m} \times (W_2 - W_1) \times h_{fg}$ | Humidification/dehumidification load | kW | | 9 | $W = 0.622 \frac{p_w}{P - p_w}$ | Specific humidity | kg/kg | | 10 | $RH = \frac{p_w}{p_s} \times 100$ | Relative humidity | % | | 11 | $EER = \frac{Q_{cooling}}{W_{input}}$ | Energy Efficiency Ratio | (W/W) | | 12 | $\text{COP} = \frac{Q_L}{W_{comp}}$ | Coefficient of Performance | — | | 13 | $1 \, TR = 3.517 \, kW$ | Refrigeration ton conversion | kW | | 14 | $A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$ | Circular duct area | m² | | 15 | $\Delta P_{total} = \Delta P_{friction} + \Delta P_{dynamic}$ | Total duct pressure loss | Pa | > [!NOTE] > **CRITICAL FORMULA — Equivalent Diameter of Rectangular Duct:** > $$D_e = 1.30 \frac{(a \times b)^{0.625}}{(a+b)^{0.25}}$$ > where $a$ and $b$ are the sides of the rectangular duct in meters. This is essential for using round-duct friction charts on rectangular ducts. ## 2.12 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Duct design** is the backbone of HVAC engineering — improper design leads to **noise, poor air distribution, high power consumption, and discomfort**. - **Chilled water systems** dominate modern commercial buildings (>100 TR) due to **centralized efficiency and lower refrigerant charge**. - **Year-round AC** with **heat pump** technology is the future of sustainable HVAC, achieving **3–4× efficiency** over electric resistance heating. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Equal Friction Method (EFM) Duct Design ### Statement In the **Equal Friction Method**, the **frictional pressure drop per unit length (ΔPf/L)** is held **constant** throughout the duct system. As air flows from main to branches, the velocity decreases and the duct area is reduced accordingly. ### Derivation **Step 1: Start with the Darcy-Weisbach equation for friction loss in a duct:** $$\Delta P_f = f \times \frac{L}{D} \times \frac{\rho V^2}{2}$$ **Step 2: Frictional pressure drop per unit length:** $$\frac{\Delta P_f}{L} = \frac{f}{D} \times \frac{\rho V^2}{2}$$ **Step 3: Substituting continuity equation $V = Q/A$ and $D = \sqrt{4A/\pi}$:** $$\frac{\Delta P_f}{L} = \frac{f \times \rho V^2}{2D} = \frac{f \times \rho V^2}{2} \times \frac{1}{D}$$ **Step 4: For a given constant ΔPf/L, the velocity V must reduce as the duct diameter D reduces (in branches). This is the governing principle of EFM.** **Step 5: Typical design value:** $\frac{\Delta P_f}{L} = 0.8 \text{ to } 1.2 \, \text{Pa/m}$ for low-velocity systems. **Step 6: Procedure:** 1. Decide ΔPf/L (e.g., 1.0 Pa/m). 2. For each section, knowing Q, find D from friction chart at given ΔPf/L. 3. Verify that V is in acceptable range (5–8 m/s in mains). --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Static Regain Method (SRM) ### Statement In the **Static Regain Method**, the **static pressure is constant** throughout the duct. The reduction in velocity pressure from one section to the next is converted to static pressure, offsetting the friction loss. ### Derivation **Step 1: Apply Bernoulli's equation between two points (1 and 2) in a duct with friction:** $$P_{s1} + \frac{\rho V_1^2}{2} = P_{s2} + \frac{\rho V_2^2}{2} + \Delta P_f$$ **Step 2: For static pressure to be constant, $P_{s1} = P_{s2}$:** $$\frac{\rho V_1^2}{2} = \frac{\rho V_2^2}{2} + \Delta P_f$$ **Step 3: Therefore, the friction loss must equal the decrease in velocity pressure:** $$\Delta P_f = \frac{\rho}{2}(V_1^2 - V_2^2)$$ **Step 4: This is called "static regain" — the loss in kinetic energy is recovered as static pressure.** **Step 5: Procedure for sizing:** 1. Start at the **farthest outlet** with design V (~2.5 m/s at diffuser). 2. Calculate the velocity in each upstream section using $\Delta P_f = \frac{\rho}{2}(V_{up}^2 - V_{down}^2)$. 3. Find duct diameter from $D = \sqrt{4Q/(\pi V)}$. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Equivalent Diameter of Rectangular Duct ### Statement The equivalent diameter $D_e$ of a rectangular duct (a × b) is the diameter of a circular duct having the **same frictional resistance per unit length** at the same air velocity. ### Derivation **Step 1: For a circular duct, hydraulic diameter:** $$D = \frac{4A_c}{P_c} = \frac{4 \times \frac{\pi D^2}{4}}{\pi D} = D$$ **Step 2: For a rectangular duct (a × b), hydraulic diameter:** $$D_h = \frac{4ab}{2(a+b)} = \frac{2ab}{a+b}$$ **Step 3: For equal friction loss per unit length, equating Darcy-Weisbach for both:** $$f_c \frac{L}{D_c} \frac{\rho V^2}{2} = f_r \frac{L}{D_h} \frac{\rho V^2}{2}$$ **Step 4: Empirical relation (ASHRAE / KTU standard):** $$D_e = 1.30 \frac{(a \times b)^{0.625}}{(a+b)^{0.25}}$$ This is the standard formula used in all KTU exam problems. --- ## 3.4 Worked Example 1: Duct Sizing (Equal Friction Method) **Problem:** Size a circular main duct carrying **Q = 1.5 m³/s** using Equal Friction Method with $\Delta P_f/L = 1.0 \, \text{Pa/m}$. Take air density $\rho = 1.2 \, \text{kg/m}^3$. **Step 1: Use friction chart or iterate using Darcy-Weisbach. Assume D = 0.45 m.** $$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 0.45^2}{4} = 0.159 \, \text{m}^2$$ **Step 2: Velocity:** $$V = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{1.5}{0.159} = 9.43 \, \text{m/s}$$ **Step 3: Reynolds number (air kinematic viscosity ν = 1.5 × 10⁻⁵ m²/s):** $$Re = \frac{VD}{\nu} = \frac{9.43 \times 0.45}{1.5 \times 10^{-5}} = 2.83 \times 10^5$$ **Step 4: Friction factor (from Moody chart, smooth pipe): $f \approx 0.018$.** **Step 5: Friction loss per unit length:** $$\frac{\Delta P_f}{L} = \frac{f}{D} \times \frac{\rho V^2}{2} = \frac{0.018}{0.45} \times \frac{1.2 \times 9.43^2}{2} = 2.13 \, \text{Pa/m}$$ **Step 6: Since 2.13 > 1.0 Pa/m, increase D to 0.55 m.** $$A = \frac{\pi \times 0.55^2}{4} = 0.2376 \, \text{m}^2$$ $$V = \frac{1.5}{0.2376} = 6.31 \, \text{m/s}$$ $$\frac{\Delta P_f}{L} = \frac{0.018}{0.55} \times \frac{1.2 \times 6.31^2}{2} = 0.78 \, \text{Pa/m}$$ **Step 7: Interpolate: at D = 0.50 m → ΔPf/L ≈ 1.05 Pa/m ✓ (close to target).** **Final Answer:** D = **0.50 m (≈ 500 mm)**. --- ## 3.5 Worked Example 2: Heating Coil Load **Problem:** Air at 15°C, 60% RH enters a heating coil. Air flow rate = 2 m³/s. Air is heated to 30°C. Calculate heat added. Use $\rho = 1.2 \, \text{kg/m}^3$, $c_p = 1.005 \, \text{kJ/kg·K}$. **Step 1: Mass flow rate:** $$\dot{m} = \rho \times Q = 1.2 \times 2 = 2.4 \, \text{kg/s}$$ **Step 2: Temperature rise:** $$\Delta T = 30 - 15 = 15 \, \text{K}$$ **Step 3: Heat added (sensible heating only, since no moisture change):** $$Q_h = \dot{m} \times c_p \times \Delta T = 2.4 \times 1.005 \times 15 = 36.18 \, \text{kW}$$ **Final Answer:** $Q_h = \textbf{36.18 kW}$. --- ## 3.6 Worked Example 3: Humidification Load **Problem:** Air at 25°C, 30% RH (W₁ = 0.00595 kg/kg) is humidified to 50% RH (W₂ = 0.00988 kg/kg). Air flow = 1.0 m³/s, $\rho = 1.2$ kg/m³, $h_{fg} = 2501$ kJ/kg. **Step 1: Mass flow rate:** $$\dot{m} = 1.2 \times 1.0 = 1.2 \, \text{kg/s}$$ **Step 2: Moisture to be added:** $$\Delta W = W_2 - W_1 = 0.00988 - 0.00595 = 0.00393 \, \text{kg/kg}$$ **Step 3: Humidification load:** $$Q_m = \dot{m} \times \Delta W \times h_{fg} = 1.2 \times 0.00393 \times 2501 = 11.79 \, \text{kW}$$ **Final Answer:** $Q_m = \textbf{11.79 kW}$. --- ## 3.7 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU Board Exam Favorite) | Parameter | Window AC | Split AC | Packaged AC | Chilled Water | |-----------|-----------|----------|-------------|---------------| | Capacity | 0.75–2 TR | 0.75–5 TR | 3–100 TR | >100 TR | | Refrigerant in room | Yes (window sill) | No (outdoor unit) | No | No | | Ducts | No | No | Yes | Yes | | Cooling medium | Refrigerant | Refrigerant | Refrigerant | Chilled water | | Installation | Easy | Moderate | Complex | Complex | | Energy efficiency | Low | High (with inverter) | Moderate | Highest | | Initial cost | Lowest | Low | Medium | Highest | | Operating cost | High | Moderate | Moderate | Lowest | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1: Window Air Conditioner — Working Principle ```mermaid graph TD A[Indoor Air 27C 60%RH] --> B[Evaporator Coil
