[KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Differentiate between a Window AC and a Split AC unit.** **Model Answer:** | Feature | Window AC | Split AC | |---------|-----------|----------| | Configuration | Single unit, all components in one cabinet | Two units: Indoor (IDU) + Outdoor (ODU) | | Compressor location | Indoor (window side) | Outdoor | | Noise level | Higher (compressor noise inside) | Lower | | Installation | Fits in window opening | Requires wall mounting with refrigerant piping | | Capacity range | 0.75–2 TR | 0.75–5 TR | | Maintenance | Easy | Requires technician for refrigerant handling | *(MCO 1: Remembering — 3 marks)* --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **What is the function of a humidistat in an air conditioning system?** **Model Answer:** A **humidistat** is a control device that senses the **relative humidity** of air and switches the humidifier or dehumidifier ON/OFF to maintain the desired RH level (typically 40–60% for human comfort). It uses a **hygroscopic element** (human hair, nylon, or polymer) that changes dimension with moisture, actuating a snap-action switch. It is the **moisture equivalent of a thermostat** and is essential in year-round AC systems, hospitals, museums, and textile industries where precise humidity control is critical. *(MCO 1 & 2: Remembering & Understanding — 3 marks)* --- ## Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Model with Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024] **(a)** With a neat schematic, explain the working of a **window air conditioner**. State any **four advantages** of a split AC over a window AC. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A duct carries **0.8 m³/s** of air. The design pressure drop per unit length is **1.0 Pa/m**. Determine the **diameter** of a circular duct and the **equivalent diameter of a rectangular duct** with aspect ratio 1:1.5. Take $\rho = 1.2 \, \text{kg/m}^3$. **(7 marks)** --- #### Solution to Q.A(a): **Working of Window AC:** A window AC is a **self-contained, factory-sealed vapor-compression refrigeration unit** installed in a window or wall opening. The refrigeration cycle is: 1. **Compressor (2):** Compresses low-pressure refrigerant vapor to high-pressure, high-temperature vapor. 2. **Condenser (3):** Hot vapor rejects heat to outdoor air blown across it by the propeller fan (4). 3. **Capillary Tube / Expansion Device (5):** Reduces pressure of liquid refrigerant. 4. **Evaporator (6):** Low-temperature liquid absorbs heat from indoor air (blown by the centrifugal blower 7) and evaporates. 5. **Filters (1):** Clean the air before it passes over the evaporator. 6. **Louver / Grille:** Directs conditioned air into the room. The thermostat cycles the compressor ON/OFF to maintain set temperature. **Four Advantages of Split AC over Window AC:** 1. **Lower noise** — Compressor is in the outdoor unit. 2. **Better aesthetics** — Only a slim indoor unit is visible. 3. **Higher capacity** — Up to 5 TR vs. 2 TR. 4. **Easier installation** — No window modification; only a 3-inch wall hole. 5. **Better energy efficiency** — Inverter variants available. *(MCO 2: Understanding — 7 marks: 4 for working + 3 for advantages)* --- #### Solution to Q.A(b): **Given:** Q = 0.8 m³/s, $\Delta P_f/L$ = 1.0 Pa/m, $\rho$ = 1.2 kg/m³, aspect ratio 1:1.5 for rectangular duct. **Step 1: Find circular duct diameter using EFM. Iterate:** Assume D = 0.40 m: - $A = \pi D^2/4 = 0.1257$ m² - $V = Q/A = 0.8/0.1257 = 6.37$ m/s - $Re = VD/\nu = 6.37 \times 0.40 / 1.5 \times 10^{-5} = 1.70 \times 10^5$ - $f \approx 0.019$ (smooth pipe, from Moody chart) - $\Delta P_f/L = (f/D)(\rho V^2/2) = (0.019/0.40)(1.2 \times 6.37^2/2) = 1.16$ Pa/m Too high → increase D to 0.43 m: - $A = 0.1452$ m² - $V = 5.51$ m/s - $\Delta P_f/L = (0.019/0.43)(1.2 \times 5.51^2/2) = 0.80$ Pa/m Interpolate linearly: For 1.0 Pa/m, D ≈ 0.42 m **Final Answer:** **D ≈ 0.42 m (420 mm)** **Step 2: Equivalent diameter of circular duct = 0.42 m. For a rectangular duct with same $\Delta P_f/L$ and same Q, the equivalent diameter must also = 0.42 m.** **Step 3: For aspect ratio 1:1.5, let a = 1.5b. From equivalent diameter formula:** $$D_e = 1.30 \frac{(a \times b)^{0.625}}{(a+b)^{0.25}}$$ $$0.42 = 1.30 \frac{(1.5b \times b)^{0.625}}{(1.5b + b)^{0.25}} = 1.30 \frac{(1.5)^{0.625} \times b^{1.25}}{(2.5b)^{0.25}}$$ $$0.42 = 1.30 \times 1.30 \times b^{1.25 - 0.25} / 2.5^{0.25}$$ $$0.42 = 1.69 \times b / 1.257$$ $$b = 0.42 \times 1.257 / 1.69 = 0.312 \, \text{m} = 312 \, \text{mm}$$ $$a = 1.5 \times 0.312 = 0.468 \, \text{m} = 468 \, \text{mm}$$ **Final Answer:** **Rectangular duct = 468 mm × 312 mm** (aspect ratio 1.5:1) *(MCO 3 & 4: Applying & Analyzing — 7 marks)* --- ### Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – May 2024] — ALTERNATIVE **(a)** Explain with a block diagram the components and working of a **Chilled Water Air Conditioning System**. Compare it with a **Direct Expansion (DX) system**. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Air at **20°C, 40% RH** is to be conditioned to **25°C, 50% RH** in a year-round AC system. The air flow rate is **1.5 m³/s**. Calculate: (i) Heat to be added/removed, (ii) Moisture to be added. Use $c_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K, $h_{fg} = 2501$ kJ/kg, $\rho = 1.2$ kg/m³. **(7 marks)** --- #### Solution to Q.B(a): **Chilled Water AC System Block Diagram:** **Components:** 1. **Chiller** (vapor compression or absorption) 2. **Chilled Water Pump (CHWP)** 3. **Air Handling Unit (AHU)** with chilled water coil 4. **Condenser Water Pump (CWP)** 5. **Cooling Tower (CT)** 6. **Expansion tank** and **3-way control valves** **Working:** - Chiller cools water from 12.2°C (return) to 6.7°C (supply). - CHWP circulates chilled water to AHU coils. - In AHU, air passes over the chilled water coil, getting cooled and dehumidified. - Heat absorbed by chilled water → Chiller's evaporator. - Refrigerant in chiller evaporates → Compressed → Rejected to condenser. - Condenser water absorbs heat → Pumped to cooling tower → Heat rejected to atmosphere. - Cooled water returns to chiller condenser. **Comparison: DX vs Chilled Water** | Feature | DX System | Chilled Water | |---------|-----------|---------------| | Cooling medium | Refrigerant directly in coil | Chilled water in coil | | Capacity | < 100 TR (typically) | > 100 TR | | Refrigerant charge | Large (in every unit) | Small (only in chiller) | | Zoning | Difficult | Easy (per zone AHU/FCU) | | Energy efficiency at part load | Poor (each unit cycles) | Better (chiller modulates) | | Maintenance | Per unit | Centralized | *(MCO 2 & 5: Understanding & Evaluating — 7 marks)* --- #### Solution to Q.B(b): **Given:** State 1: T₁ = 20°C, φ₁ = 40%; State 2: T₂ = 25°C, φ₂ = 50%; Q = 1.5 m³/s; $\rho$ = 1.2 kg/m³. **Step 1: Get saturation pressures (from steam tables):** - $p_{s1}$ at 20°C = 2.339 kPa - $p_{s2}$ at 25°C = 3.169 kPa **Step 2: Partial pressures:** - $p_{w1} = 0.40 \times 2.339 = 0.9356$ kPa - $p_{w2} = 0.50 \times 3.169 = 1.5845$ kPa **Step 3: Specific humidities (P = 101.325 kPa):** - $W_1 = 0.622 \times 0.9356 / (101.325 - 0.9356) = 0.00579$ kg/kg - $W_2 = 0.622 \times 1.5845 / (101.325 - 1.5845) = 0.00988$ kg/kg **Step 4: Mass flow rate:** $$\dot{m} = 1.2 \times 1.5 = 1.8 \, \text{kg/s}$$ **Step 5: Heat to be added (sensible):** $$Q_h = \dot{m} \times c_p \times (T_2 - T_1) = 1.8 \times 1.005 \times (25 - 20) = 9.045 \, \text{kW (heating)}$$ **Step 6: Moisture to be added (humidification):** $$\Delta W = W_2 - W_1 = 0.00988 - 0.00579 = 0.00409 \, \text{kg/kg}$$ $$\dot{m}_{water} = \dot{m} \times \Delta W = 1.8 \times 0.00409 = 0.00736 \, \text{kg/s}$$ $$= 26.5 \, \text{kg/hr}$$ **Final Answers:** - Heat to be added = **9.05 kW** (sensible heating required) - Moisture to be added = **0.00736 kg/s** (≈ 26.5 kg/hr) **Note:** Since T₂ > T₁ and W₂ > W₁, this is a **heating + humidification** process (typical of winter AC mode). *(MCO 3 & 4: Applying & Analyzing — 7 marks)* --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Common Mistake #1:** Students often write **"condenser is inside"** in a window AC. **Correct:** Condenser + its fan are on the **outdoor side**; evaporator is on the indoor side. > 2. **Common Mistake #2:** Forgetting to convert $W$ from kg/kg to kg/s before multiplying by $h_{fg}$ in humidification problems. > 3. **Common Mistake #3:** Mixing up SHF and W in psychrometric problems. Always compute **sensible heat (m·cp·ΔT)** and **latent heat (m·ΔW·hfg)** **separately**. > 4. **Common Mistake #4:** Induct design, students often forget to **iterate** the duct diameter until ΔPf/L matches the design value. They just guess and skip recheck. > 5. **Common Mistake #5:** Using **dynamic pressure** instead of **static pressure** in static regain method. The regain equation uses **velocity pressure**, not dynamic. Be precise. > 6. **Common Mistake #6:** For aspect-ratio rectangular ducts, the formula has **0.625 and 0.25** powers — students often confuse the constants. Memorize: $1.30 \cdot (ab)^{0.625} / (a+b)^{0.25}$. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - [x] **THASPP** = 7 sub-functions of AC (Temp, Humidity, Air-flow, Sound, Purity, Pressure). - [x] **Window AC** → Single unit, 0.75–2 TR, both coils in one box; condenser outdoor side. - [x] **Split AC** → IDU (evaporator) + ODU (compressor + condenser), connected by refrigerant lines. - [x] **Packaged AC** → 3–100 TR, ducted, suitable for multi-room buildings. - [x] **All-Air System** → Air is sole medium. Sub-types: Single duct, Dual duct, Multi-zone, Terminal reheat. - [x] **Chilled Water System** → Water at 6.7°C supply / 12.2°C return; CHWP + CWP + Cooling Tower + AHU/FCU. - [x] **Summer AC** → Cooling + Dehumidification. **Winter AC** → Heating + Humidification. - [x] **Year-Round AC** → Combination using thermostats, humidistats, pre-heater, reheat coil. - [x] **Heating Coil Load** = $Q_h = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ (sensible only). - [x] **Humidification Load** = $Q_m = \dot{m} \cdot \Delta W \cdot h_{fg}$ (latent only). - [x] **Humidifier types** → Pan, Spray, Steam, Wetted Element, Ultrasonic. **Steam type** is cleanest. - [x] **Thermostat** = Temperature sensor (bimetallic / bellows / electronic). **Humidistat** = RH sensor (hygroscopic). - [x] **Duct Design Methods** → (1) **Equal Friction** (constant ΔPf/L), (2) **Static Regain** (constant static pressure, recovers velocity pressure as static). - [x] **Equivalent Diameter** for rectangular duct: $D_e = 1.30 \cdot (ab)^{0.625} / (a+b)^{0.25}$. - [x] **Standard velocities** → Mains 5–8 m/s, Branches 3–5 m/s, Outlets 2–3 m/s. - [x] **Friction Loss** uses **Darcy-Weisbach** equation: $\Delta P_f = f(L/D)(\rho V^2/2)$. - [x] **Total duct pressure loss** = Friction loss + Dynamic losses (fittings, bends, dampers). - [x] **1 TR = 3.517 kW = 3024 kcal/hr** (very high-yield conversion). - [x] **EER = Q_cooling / W_input (in W/W)** — High EER = Efficient AC. - [x] **Psychrometric equations** → $W = 0.622 \cdot p_w / (P - p_w)$; $RH = p_w / p_s \times 100$. - [x] **Cooling tower** rejects condenser heat to atmosphere via evaporative cooling. - [x] **Heat Pump** = Reversed refrigeration cycle for year-round AC (winter heating + summer cooling).

topics.title: Air Conditioning for Various Environments: Restaurants, Hospitals, Retail Outlets, Computer Centres, Theatres, Industrial Applications
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Fundamental Definition

> **ACADEMIC METADATA**
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET631 - REFRIGERATION AND AIR CONDITIONING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Air Conditioning for Various Environments: Restaurants, Hospitals, Retail Outlets, Computer Centres, Theatres, Industrial Applications

**Environmental Air Conditioning** is the specialized branch of HVAC engineering that tailors indoor air parameters—namely **temperature, relative humidity, air purity, air motion, and air distribution**—to the unique physiological, psychological, and process-driven requirements of a given space. Unlike general comfort AC, environmental AC considers **occupant density, contamination sources, process heat loads, noise tolerance, and safety codes** as primary design drivers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):**
> The design of air conditioning systems is **NOT a one-size-fits-all** exercise. A hospital operating theatre and a multiplex theatre may both require "comfort cooling," but the filtration class, air change rate, pressure relationship, and redundancy requirements are radically different. KTU examiners frequently test this *differentiation ability* in the 14-mark essay questions.

## 1.2 The Six Pillars of Environmental AC

Each application environment is characterized by six design pillars:

1. **Thermal Comfort (DBT & RH)** – Set-point values for dry-bulb temperature and relative humidity.
2. **Ventilation Rate (CFM/person & ACH)** – Outdoor air intake and room air changes per hour.
3. **Filtration Class (G1–H14)** – Pre-filter, fine filter, HEPA, ULPA grades.
4. **Pressure Relationship** – Positive, negative, or neutral pressurization vs. adjacent zones.
5. **Air Distribution Pattern** – Low-sidewall, ceiling diffuser, displacement, or laminar flow.
6. **Redundancy & Reliability** – N+1, 2N configurations for mission-critical spaces.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Hospital Gown vs. The Restaurant Napkin"

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:**
> Imagine a restaurant and a hospital waiting room. In a **restaurant**, you need *comfortable, fresh-smelling air* (like a clean tablecloth) — moderate filtration, good odor control, and pleasant temperature. In a **hospital**, you need *surgically sterile air* (like a surgical gown) — HEPA filtration, positive/negative pressure zoning, and precise humidity to inhibit microbial growth. The restaurant cares about *customer experience*; the hospital cares about *patient survival*. Both use "air conditioning," but the *design intent*, *equipment*, and *codes* are completely different. The KTU Module 4 question essentially asks: **"Can you tailor the AC system to the right 'gown' or 'napkin' for the application?"**

## 1.4 Standard Reference Codes (Must Cite in Answers)

| Application | Governing Standard | Issuing Body |
|---|---|---|
| Hospitals & Healthcare | **ASHRAE Standard 170** | ASHRAE |
| Cleanrooms (Pharma/Semi) | **ISO 14644-1** | ISO |
| Data Centres | **ASHRAE TC 9.9** | ASHRAE |
| Commercial Comfort | **ASHRAE Standard 55** | ASHRAE |
| Energy Efficiency | **ECBC / ISHRAE** | BEE, India |
| Ventilation Rate | **ASHRAE Standard 62.1** | ASHRAE |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Different Environments Demand Different AC Designs

The design parameters for any AC system emerge from the **balance of three competing demands**:

- **People-driven loads** (sensible + latent heat from occupants, CO₂ generation, odors)
- **Process-driven loads** (equipment heat, chemical fumes, particulate generation)
- **Safety-driven loads** (sterility, pressure control, redundancy for life safety)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Insight:** In ESE (End Semester Examination) answers, students often write *only* the temperature and humidity values for each application. To score full marks (7/7 in a 14-mark question), you MUST also discuss **air changes per hour (ACH), filtration grade, pressure relationship, and a justification (the 'why')** for each parameter.

## 2.2 Design Parameters — Environment-by-Environment Analysis

### 2.2.1 Restaurants

- **Occupancy:** High density during peak hours (dinner rush = 1.5–2.0 m²/person).
- **Heat gain sources:** Cooking hoods (latent load is dominant), dining lights, kitchen equipment, glass frontage.
- **Design Conditions:** **DBT = 23–25 °C**, **RH = 50–60 %**, **ACH = 8–12**.
- **Ventilation:** **15–20 CFM per person** of outdoor air (ASHRAE 62.1).
- **Critical Concern:** **Odor & smoke control** from kitchen — requires a dedicated exhaust hood and *make-up air unit* to prevent negative pressurization.
- **Filtration:** G4 (MERV 8) pre-filter + F7 (MERV 13) fine filter.
- **KTU 2024 Tip:** Always mention that restaurant AC must handle **high latent loads** due to cooking and high occupancy.

### 2.2.2 Hospitals

Hospitals are the *most demanding* AC environment and are subdivided into multiple zones:

| Hospital Zone | DBT (°C) | RH (%) | ACH | Pressure | Filtration |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Operation Theatre (OT)** | 21–24 | 40–60 | **20–25** | **Positive** | **HEPA H13/H14** |
| **ICU** | 21–24 | 40–60 | **12–15** | Positive | HEPA H13 |
| **General Ward** | 22–25 | 40–60 | 6–8 | Neutral | F7 (MERV 13) |
| **Isolation Room (Airborne)** | 22–25 | 40–60 | **12–15** | **Negative** | HEPA H13 (exhaust) |
| **Pharmacy / Clean Store** | 21–24 | 35–50 | 8–10 | Positive | F8–F9 |
| **Kitchen** | 26–28 | < 60 | 15–20 | Negative | G4 + Activated Carbon |

- **ASHRAE 170 mandate:** Operating theatres require **HEPA H13 ceiling coverage of ≥ 90 %** and a **ceiling-mounted laminar flow diffuser** to achieve ISO Class 7 cleanliness.
- **Humidity control in OT:** RH must be kept between **40–60 %** to inhibit bacterial growth *and* prevent static electricity buildup on surgical instruments.
- **Laminar flow velocity:** 0.25–0.5 m/s in the operating zone (unidirectional downward flow).

### 2.2.3 Retail Outlets (Shopping Malls & Showrooms)

- **DBT = 23–26 °C**, **RH = 50–60 %**, **ACH = 6–10**.
- **Ventilation:** 7.5–15 CFM/person (ASHRAE 62.1 retail category).
- **Challenge:** *Heterogeneous loads* — anchor stores (food courts) generate latent heat; electronics stores generate high sensible heat.
- **Strategy:** **VAV (Variable Air Volume)** systems with zone-level temperature control.
- **Filtration:** G4 + F7 (MERV 8 + MERV 13).
- **Energy:** Heat recovery wheels (enthalpy wheels) on exhaust air save **40–60 %** of cooling energy in 24/7 malls.

### 2.2.4 Computer Centres & Data Halls

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Design Philosophy:** Data centres are designed for **equipment, not humans.** Comfort is secondary; equipment reliability is primary.

- **ASHRAE TC 9.9 Recommended Range:**
  - **Temperature: 18–27 °C** (allowable A1 class: 18–27 °C; A2: 15–32 °C)
  - **Humidity: 40 % to 55 % RH** (dew point 5.5–15 °C)
- **Cooling Load:** Extremely **high sensible heat ratio (SHR ≈ 0.95–1.0)** — almost no latent load from occupants (because of low occupancy).
- **Load Density:** **500–2000 W/m²** (compared to 100 W/m² for an office).
- **Filtration:** G4 (MERV 8) pre-filter — to protect coil surfaces, not for occupant health.
- **Redundancy:** **N+1 minimum**, often **2N (Tier IV)** for hyperscale data centres.
- **Layout Strategy:** **Hot-Aisle / Cold-Aisle** containment to maximize ΔT and reduce energy.
- **PUE Target:** Power Usage Effectiveness ≤ 1.4 (modern efficient data centres).
- **Air Changes:** NOT the primary design driver (typically 8–12 ACH, but temperature stability is what matters).
- **Free Cooling:** Economizers used when outdoor WB < 15 °C.

### 2.2.5 Theatres & Auditoriums

- **DBT = 23–26 °C**, **RH = 50–60 %**, **ACH = 8–12**.
- **Ventilation:** 7.5–15 CFM/person (with 1.5 CFM/ft² as a floor).
- **Critical Concern #1:** **Noise (NC criterion).** Background noise must be **NC-25 to NC-30** in the auditorium.
- **Critical Concern #2:** **Draft-free air distribution.** Stratification must be minimized because audiences sit for long durations at a single level.
- **Strategy:** **Displacement ventilation** or low-velocity ceiling diffusers with high induction.
- **Load Variation:** Occupancy varies from 5 % (rehearsals) to 100 % (premiere) → **VAV with CO₂-based demand-controlled ventilation (DCV)**.

### 2.2.6 Industrial Applications

Industrial AC is *application-specific*. Sub-categories include:

| Industrial Sub-Sector | Special Requirement | Typical Conditions |
|---|---|---|
| **Pharmaceutical Manufacturing** | ISO Class 5–8 cleanrooms | 21 °C, 45 % RH, 20–600 ACH (laminar flow) |
| **Semiconductor Fab** | ISO Class 3–5 | 22 °C, 45 % RH, 600+ ACH (FFUs) |
| **Textile / Spinning Mill** | High humidification | 28–32 °C, **75–85 % RH** |
| **Tobacco Processing** | High RH for leaf flexibility | 24 °C, 70–75 % RH |
| **Printing Press** | Humidity for paper dimensional stability | 24 °C, **50–55 % RH** |
| **Automotive Paint Booth** | Make-up air + exhaust | 22 °C, 60–70 % RH, 30–40 ACH |
| **Server Farm / Telecom** | Same as data centre | 18–24 °C, 40–55 % RH |

- **Textile Mill AC:** Humidification is the *primary* function. Cooling is secondary. Uses **wetted-cell humidifiers** or **steam injection**.
- **Cleanroom AC:** Filtration and air changes dominate. HEPA/ULPA at ceiling, raised perforated floor for return. **Turbulent mixing or unidirectional laminar flow.**

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Exam-Ready Formulas:** The following equations are the **minimum** required for KTU 2024 ESE problems. Memorize the units and the conversion factor **1 TR = 3.517 kW = 12000 BTU/h**.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $$\text{ACH} = \frac{Q_{vol} \times 60}{V}$$ | Air changes per hour (Q = CFM, V = room volume ft³) | hr⁻¹ |
| 2 | $$Q_{vol} = \frac{\text{ACH} \times V}{60}$$ | CFM from ACH | CFM |
| 3 | $$Q_s = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$$ | Sensible heat (m = kg/s, c_p = 1.005 kJ/kg·K) | kW |
| 4 | $$Q_l = m \cdot h_{fg} \cdot \Delta \omega$$ | Latent heat (h_fg = 2501 kJ/kg) | kW |
| 5 | $$\text{SHR} = \frac{Q_s}{Q_s + Q_l}$$ | Sensible Heat Ratio (0 → 1) | dimensionless |
| 6 | $$Q_{people} = N \cdot q_s \cdot \text{SHGF} + N \cdot q_l \cdot \text{LHGF}$$ | Heat gain from occupants (ASHRAE fundamentals) | W or BTU/h |
| 7 | $$\text{TR} = \frac{Q_{total}}{3.517}$$ | Convert kW to Tons of Refrigeration | TR |
| 8 | $$\text{PUE} = \frac{P_{total}}{P_{IT}}$$ | Data centre efficiency metric | dimensionless |
| 9 | $$T_{dew\,point} = T_{db} - \left(\frac{100 - RH}{5}\right)$$ | Approx. dew point (Magnus formula simplification) | °C |
| 10 | $$\Delta T_{chilled\,water} = T_{out} - T_{in} = 5.5\text{–}6\,°\text{C}$$ | Chilled water ΔT design | °C |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why This Matters in the Field:** A graduate working at *L&T Construction*, *Voltas*, *Blue Star*, *Carrier*, or *Johnson Controls* will frequently be asked to size an AC system for one of these six environments. The *insight* tested by KTU is whether the engineer can **justify** the design choice — not just *list* parameters. For example: *"Why is a cleanroom designed for 600 ACH while a retail store is satisfied with 6–10 ACH?"* The answer lies in **particulate dilution** (cleanroom must remove sub-micron particles to ISO 5 = 3520 particles/m³ at 0.5 µm) versus **occupant comfort** (retail).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Required CFM for a Given Air Change Rate

This is the **most-tested derivation** in KTU Module 4. A student must be able to derive the relationship between CFM, ACH, and room volume.

### 3.1.1 Given Data
- Room volume: $V$ in cubic feet (ft³)
- Required air changes per hour: $\text{ACH}$ in hr⁻¹
- Required CFM: $Q_{vol}$ in ft³/min

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Air Changes per Hour (ACH)} &= \frac{\text{Volume of air supplied per hour}}{\text{Room volume}} \\[6pt]
\text{Volume supplied per hour} &= \text{ACH} \times V \quad \text{(ft}^3\text{/h)} \\[6pt]
\text{Volume supplied per minute} &= \frac{\text{ACH} \times V}{60} \quad \text{(ft}^3\text{/min = CFM)} \\[6pt]
\therefore \quad Q_{vol} &= \frac{\text{ACH} \times V}{60} \quad \text{(CFM)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.3 Worked Example — OT Air Change Calculation

**Problem:** An operation theatre has dimensions **7 m × 6 m × 3.5 m**. The design requires **20 ACH**. Calculate the required supply CFM in m³/h and CFM.

**Step 1 — Convert dimensions to feet (1 m = 3.281 ft):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
L &= 7 \times 3.281 = 22.97 \text{ ft} \\
W &= 6 \times 3.281 = 19.69 \text{ ft} \\
H &= 3.5 \times 3.281 = 11.48 \text{ ft}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute volume:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= L \times W \times H \\
V &= 22.97 \times 19.69 \times 11.48 \\
V &= 5192.2 \text{ ft}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply CFM formula:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{vol} &= \frac{\text{ACH} \times V}{60} \\
Q_{vol} &= \frac{20 \times 5192.2}{60} \\
Q_{vol} &= \frac{103844}{60} \\
Q_{vol} &= 1730.7 \text{ CFM}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to m³/h (1 CFM = 1.699 m³/h):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{m^3/h} &= 1730.7 \times 1.699 \\
Q_{m^3/h} &= 2940.5 \text{ m}^3\text{/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** For KTU 14-mark questions, always show the **unit conversion** steps explicitly. Examiners award partial credit for **method** even if the final number is off by 5–10 %.

## 3.2 Derivation: Sensible Cooling Load from Occupants

**Problem:** A conference room has **50 occupants** in light office work. DBT inside is **26 °C** and outdoor is **35 °C** DBT. Find sensible heat gain from people (using ASHRAE fundamentals: q_s = 75 W/person, SHGF = 0.84 at 26 °C).

**Step 1 — Identify per-person sensible heat (q_s):**
$$
q_s = 75 \text{ W/person} \quad \text{(ASHRAE office, seated, light work)}
$$

**Step 2 — Identify Sensible Heat Gain Factor (SHGF) from ASHRAE chart at 26 °C:**
$$
\text{SHGF} = 0.84
$$

**Step 3 — Apply formula:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{s,\,people} &= N \times q_s \times \text{SHGF} \\
Q_{s,\,people} &= 50 \times 75 \times 0.84 \\
Q_{s,\,people} &= 3150 \text{ W} = 3.15 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to TR (1 TR = 3.517 kW):**
$$
\text{TR}_{sensible} = \frac{3.15}{3.517} = 0.896 \text{ TR (sensible only)}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Data Centre PUE Calculation

**Problem:** A data centre has total facility power = 800 kW and IT load = 550 kW. Find PUE.

**Step 1 — Apply PUE formula:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PUE} &= \frac{P_{total}}{P_{IT}} \\
\text{PUE} &= \frac{800}{550} \\
\text{PUE} &= 1.454
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Interpret:**
- PUE = 1.0 means 100 % efficient (impossible).
- PUE = 1.454 is *acceptable* (industry average ~1.5–1.8).
- Hyperscale (Google, Amazon) achieve **PUE = 1.1–1.2**.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Summary

The matrix below is the **single most important study aid** for Module 4. Memorize it.

| Environment | DBT (°C) | RH (%) | ACH | Pressure | Filtration | Key Driver |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Restaurant | 23–25 | 50–60 | 8–12 | Neutral | G4 + F7 | Odor + Latent load |
| Hospital OT | 21–24 | 40–60 | 20–25 | **Positive** | **HEPA H13** | Sterility |
| Hospital ICU | 21–24 | 40–60 | 12–15 | Positive | HEPA H13 | Infection control |
| Isolation Room | 22–25 | 40–60 | 12–15 | **Negative** | HEPA (exhaust) | Containment |
| Retail Outlet | 23–26 | 50–60 | 6–10 | Neutral | G4 + F7 | Heterogeneous loads |
| Computer Centre | 18–27 | 40–55 | 8–12 | Neutral | G4 | Equipment reliability |
| Theatre | 23–26 | 50–60 | 8–12 | Neutral | G4 + F7 | Acoustics + draft-free |
| Textile Mill | 28–32 | **75–85** | 8–10 | Neutral | G4 | Humidification |
| Pharmaceutical Cleanroom | 21 | 45 | **20–600** | Positive | HEPA/ULPA | Particulate control |
| Paint Booth | 22 | 60–70 | 30–40 | **Slightly positive** | F7 | VOC exhaust + make-up |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Block Diagram — AC Design Decision Flow

The following functional architecture maps the **decision logic** an engineer follows when designing an AC system for any of the six environments. Read it from left to right: **Application → Load identification → Parameter selection → Equipment selection.**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    start[Application Environment] --> load{Select Load Type}
    load -->|Sensible dominant| sens[Office Retail Theatre]
    load -->|Latent dominant| lat[Restaurant Kitchen HospitalWard]
    load -->|Process driven| proc[DataCentre Cleanroom Textile]
    sens --> ach1[ACH 6 to 10]
    lat --> ach2[ACH 8 to 20]
    proc --> ach3[ACH 20 to 600]
    ach1 --> filt1[G4 plus F7]
    ach2 --> filt2[G4 plus F7 plus Carbon]
    ach3 --> filt3[HEPA H13 ULPA]
    filt1 --> ctrl1[CVC or VAV]
    filt2 --> ctrl2[VAV with DCV]
    filt3 --> ctrl3[CAV plus Redundancy]
    ctrl1 --> out1[Comfort AC]
    ctrl2 --> out2[Comfort plus Exhaust AC]
    ctrl3 --> out3[Mission Critical AC]
```

## 4.2 Hospital OT — Cleanroom Architecture

For an **Operating Theatre**, the AC architecture is the most complex. Air enters from the ceiling (HEPA-filtered, laminar flow), flows downward over the patient, and is exhausted near the floor. The OT is maintained at **positive pressure** to prevent corridor air from infiltrating.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ch[Chiller Plant] --> cw[Chilled Water Pump]
    cw --> ahu[AHU with Cooling and Heating Coils]
    outside[Outside Air] --> pre[Pre Filter G4]
    pre --> fine[Fine Filter F7]
    fine --> ahu
    ahu --> hepa[HEPA H13 Terminal Filter]
    hepa --> laminar[Laminar Flow Ceiling Diffuser]
    laminar --> ot[Operating Zone]
    ot --> return[Low Level Return Grille]
    return --> exhaust[Exhaust to Atmosphere with HEPA]
    ahu --> humid[Steam Humidifier]
    humid --> hepa
    ahu --> ctrl[Building Management System BMS]
    ctrl --> ahu
    ctrl --> ch
```

## 4.3 Data Centre — Hot Aisle Cold Aisle Architecture

In a **data centre**, the AC system does *not* follow the comfort model. It uses **Computer Room Air Conditioning (CRAC) units** or **Computer Room Air Handlers (CRAH)** that supply cool air to the cold aisle at **18–25 °C**. The cold air is drawn through the server racks, heated by the IT equipment, and discharged into the hot aisle at **35–45 °C**. The hot air returns to the CRAC unit for re-cooling.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    crac[CRAC Unit on Floor] --> caisle[Cold Aisle 18 to 25 C]
    caisle --> rack[Server Rack Front Intake]
    rack --> raisle[Hot Aisle 35 to 45 C]
    raisle --> return[Hot Air Return Plenum]
    return --> crac
    crac --> condenser[Outdoor Condenser or Cooling Tower]
    condenser --> crac
    rack --> monitor[BMS Monitoring]
    monitor --> crac
    monitor --> alarm[Fire Suppression System]
```

## 4.4 Theatre — Displacement Ventilation Architecture

For **theatres and auditoriums**, low-velocity air is supplied at floor level (or low sidewall) at **16–18 °C**, displaces warm contaminated air upward, and is exhausted near the ceiling. This keeps the **occupied zone** (seated audience at 1.0–1.2 m elevation) in clean, draft-free conditions.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    outside[Outside Air] --> ehp[Energy Recovery Wheel]
    ehp --> ahu[AHU with Chilled Water Coil]
    ahu --> heat[Hot Water Reheat Coil]
    heat --> supply[Low Sidewall Supply Diffuser at Floor]
    supply --> zone[Occupied Zone 1.0 to 1.2 m]
    zone --> rise[Buoyant Warm Air Rises]
    rise --> exhaust[Ceiling Exhaust Grille]
    exhaust --> out2[Exhaust to Atmosphere]
    ahu --> fan[Plug Fan with Variable Speed]
    fan --> ahu
```

## 4.5 Industrial Cleanroom — Pharmaceutical Architecture

For a **pharmaceutical cleanroom (ISO Class 7)**, the architecture uses **Fan Filter Units (FFUs)** in the ceiling to deliver HEPA-filtered unidirectional (laminar) flow downward. The floor is a **raised perforated panel** that allows the air to return to the plenum below, where it is recirculated through the AHU.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    outside[Outside Air] --> ahu[AHU with Pre and Fine Filters]
    ahu --> duct[Supply Duct in Ceiling Plenum]
    duct --> ffus[Fan Filter Units FFU with HEPA H14]
    ffus --> clean[Cleanroom ISO 7 at 22 C and 45 percent RH]
    clean --> perforated[Raised Perforated Floor]
    perforated --> underfloor[Underfloor Return Plenum]
    underfloor --> ahu
    clean --> operators[Personnel in Gown via Air Shower]
    operators --> clean
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Diagrams in KTU Exams:** A 14-mark question may ask: *"Sketch the AC system architecture for a hospital operation theatre and justify the air change rate."* You are **NOT required** to draw photorealistic engineering drawings (which is impossible in a Mermaid block), but you **MUST** show the *block-level functional flow* (AHU → Filter → Diffuser → Room → Return → Exhaust). The diagrams above are *model templates* for such answers.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 1, Module 4]**
**"Why is humidity control critical in hospital operating theatres, and what is the recommended range?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Humidity control in OTs is critical for **two reasons:**
1. **Infection control:** RH between **40–60 %** inhibits bacterial and viral growth on surfaces and prevents aerosol-borne pathogens from remaining suspended.
2. **Static electricity prevention:** RH < 40 % leads to static charge buildup on surgical instruments, which can ignite flammable anaesthetic gases.

**Recommended range: 40–60 % RH, ASHRAE Standard 170.**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supplementary, Module 4]**
**"What is the difference between HEPA and ULPA filters in terms of efficiency and application?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Property | HEPA | ULPA |
|---|---|---|
| **Efficiency** | ≥ **99.97 %** at 0.3 µm | ≥ **99.999 %** at 0.12 µm |
| **Standard** | EN 1822 H10–H14 | EN 1822 U15–U17 |
| **Application** | OT, ICU, pharma | Semiconductor fabs, ISO 3 cleanrooms |

HEPA is used in **hospital OTs and pharmaceutical cleanrooms**; ULPA is reserved for **semiconductor fabrication** where sub-100 nm particles must be removed.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module Choice Pattern:** Each 14-mark question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Both sub-parts are *compulsory* and must be answered within the same question slot.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks): Hospital OT Air Conditioning** [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Adapted]

**(a)** With a neat functional diagram, describe the air conditioning system for a **hospital operation theatre**. State the recommended **temperature, humidity, ACH, pressure relationship, and filtration grade**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A hospital operation theatre is a **mission-critical clean space** that requires:

**Design parameters (AS 1668.2 / ASHRAE 170):**
- DBT: **21–24 °C**
- RH: **40–60 %**
- ACH: **20–25** (≥ 25 preferred for orthopaedic OTs)
- Pressure: **Positive (≥ +12.5 Pa)** with respect to the corridor
- Filtration: **G4 (pre) + F7 (fine) + HEPA H13/H14 (terminal)**
- Airflow: **Unidirectional laminar (downward) at 0.25–0.5 m/s**

**Functional flow (refer to SECTION 4.2 diagram):**
- Outside air → pre-filter G4 → fine filter F7 → chilled water coil → heating coil → steam humidifier → supply fan → HEPA H13 terminal filter (in ceiling) → laminar flow diffuser → OT zone → low-level return grille → exhaust HEPA → atmosphere.

**Justification:**
- Positive pressure prevents corridor (contaminated) air from leaking *into* the OT.
- Laminar downward flow sweeps particulates away from the surgical wound.
- HEPA removes **≥ 99.97 %** of 0.3 µm particles, achieving **ISO Class 7** cleanliness.
- Humidity control prevents static and microbial growth.

**(b)** A hospital OT has dimensions **8 m × 6 m × 3.2 m**. Calculate the **required CFM** if the design ACH is 25. Also calculate the **sensible heat gain from 8 medical staff** (q_s = 75 W/person, SHGF = 0.85). Convert total to TR. **(7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Convert room to ft³:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= (8 \times 3.281) \times (6 \times 3.281) \times (3.2 \times 3.281) \\
V &= 26.25 \times 19.69 \times 10.50 \\
V &= 5425.6 \text{ ft}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — CFM from ACH:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{vol} &= \frac{25 \times 5425.6}{60} \\
Q_{vol} &= 2260.7 \text{ CFM}
\end{aligned}
$$

In m³/h:
$$
Q_{m^3/h} = 2260.7 \times 1.699 = 3840.9 \text{ m}^3\text{/h}
$$

**Step 3 — Sensible heat from people:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{s,people} &= N \times q_s \times \text{SHGF} \\
Q_{s,people} &= 8 \times 75 \times 0.85 \\
Q_{s,people} &= 510 \text{ W} = 0.51 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Add lighting and equipment (assume 1.5 kW total from equipment/lights):**
$$
Q_{total} = 0.51 + 1.5 = 2.01 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to TR:**
$$
\text{TR} = \frac{2.01}{3.517} = 0.572 \text{ TR}
$$

For 24-hour operation with safety factor of 1.3: **Required capacity ≈ 0.75 TR.**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): Data Centre Air Conditioning** [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]

**(a)** Explain the **principles of air conditioning for a data centre / computer room**. State the recommended conditions as per **ASHRAE TC 9.9** and explain the concept of **hot-aisle / cold-aisle containment.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A data centre houses **mission-critical IT equipment** (servers, switches, storage). The AC system must maintain **equipment reliability** rather than human comfort.

**ASHRAE TC 9.9 recommended conditions (Class A1):**
- **Temperature: 18–27 °C** (allowable)
- **Humidity: 40 % to 55 % RH** (dew point 5.5 °C to 15 °C)
- **Filtration: G4 minimum** (to protect coils, not for occupant health)
- **Redundancy: N+1 minimum; 2N for Tier IV**

**Why 18–27 °C is acceptable for equipment:**
- Servers are designed to operate up to 35 °C inlet temperature.
- Raising inlet temperature by 1 °C saves **2–4 %** on chiller energy.
- This is the principle of **"free cooling" / economization**.

**Hot-Aisle / Cold-Aisle Containment:**
- Server racks are arranged in **alternating rows:** cold aisle in front of rack intakes (18–25 °C supply), hot aisle behind (35–45 °C exhaust).
- The cold aisle is **sealed** with plastic strips or doors; the hot aisle is **sealed** at the top to prevent mixing.
- CRAC units supply cold air to the cold aisle; return air is drawn from the hot aisle.
- This raises the **return air ΔT** from ~10 °C to **15–20 °C**, reducing fan and chiller energy by **20–30 %**.

**PUE (Power Usage Effectiveness):**
$$
\text{PUE} = \frac{P_{total}}{P_{IT}} \quad \text{(Industry target: } \leq 1.4 \text{)}
$$

**(b)** A server room has total facility power = **1200 kW** and IT load = **900 kW**. The room is **20 m × 12 m × 4 m** and must be maintained at **22 °C, 45 % RH** with outside air at **34 °C DB / 28 °C WB**. The room has 30 CRAC units (each 50 kW sensible). Calculate the **PUE, the total room volume, the heat generation per m² of floor area, and comment on redundancy.** **(7 marks)**

**Step 1 — PUE calculation:**
$$
\text{PUE} = \frac{1200}{900} = 1.333
$$

**PUE = 1.33** — *excellent* (target ≤ 1.4). Indicates **~25 % overhead** for cooling, lighting, and auxiliaries.

**Step 2 — Room volume in m³ and ft³:**
$$
V = 20 \times 12 \times 4 = 960 \text{ m}^3 = 33907 \text{ ft}^3
$$

**Step 3 — Heat generation per m²:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Floor area} &= 20 \times 12 = 240 \text{ m}^2 \\
q_{per\,m^2} &= \frac{900 \text{ kW}}{240 \text{ m}^2} = 3.75 \text{ kW/m}^2 = 3750 \text{ W/m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

This is **15–25 times higher** than a typical office (100 W/m²). The room is a **high-density data hall**, requiring precision cooling.

**Step 4 — Redundancy comment:**
- Total CRAC capacity = 30 × 50 = **1500 kW** (sensible).
- IT load = 900 kW → **66 % utilization.**
- Redundancy available: (1500 − 900)/1500 = **40 % N+1 margin.** This is *good* (typical design is N+1, meaning 1 unit can fail without affecting operation). For Tier IV, **2N redundancy** is required, which means the system should be doubled — *this design only meets Tier III.*

**Step 5 — Sensible Heat Ratio:**
Since data centres have negligible latent load (low occupancy, no cooking):
$$
\text{SHR} \approx 0.95 \text{ to } 1.0
$$

The CRAC units should be selected with **high SHR coils** (no over-dehumidification).

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Lose Marks in Module 4 Essays (Common KTU Valuation Pitfalls):**
> 1. **Listing without justifying:** Writing "OT requires HEPA" without explaining *why* (sterility, ISO Class 7, 99.97 % efficiency at 0.3 µm) — **loses 2–3 marks per sub-part**.
> 2. **Confusing ACH with CFM:** Students often write the formula in reverse or skip the **× 60 conversion**. The relationship $Q = \text{ACH} \times V / 60$ **must be stated explicitly**.
> 3. **Forgetting pressure relationship:** Hospital OTs are *positive*; isolation rooms are *negative*. Mixing these up costs **2 marks**.
> 4. **Wrong units in TR conversion:** $1 \text{ TR} = 3.517 \text{ kW}$, not 3.5 or 3.6. Use 3.517 for accuracy.
> 5. **Ignoring redundancy in data centre answers:** Any 14-mark data centre question MUST discuss N+1 or 2N redundancy and PUE. Skipping this loses **at least 2 marks**.
> 6. **Not including a diagram:** For the 7-mark sub-part with "sketch / describe," a block diagram is **mandatory**. Mermaid flowchart or hand-drawn box diagram both earn credit.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Final 60-Second Revision — Print This Section Before the Exam.**

- ✅ **Restaurants:** DBT 23–25 °C, RH 50–60 %, ACH 8–12, **high latent load** from kitchen.
- ✅ **Hospital OT:** DBT 21–24 °C, RH 40–60 %, **ACH 20–25**, **positive pressure**, **HEPA H13**.
- ✅ **Hospital ICU:** ACH 12–15, HEPA, positive.
- ✅ **Isolation Room:** **Negative pressure** (airborne pathogens must NOT escape).
- ✅ **Retail Malls:** DBT 23–26 °C, RH 50–60 %, ACH 6–10, **VAV with DCV** for variable load.
- ✅ **Data Centre:** ASHRAE TC 9.9, **18–27 °C**, **40–55 % RH**, **hot-aisle/cold-aisle**, **N+1 redundancy**, **PUE ≤ 1.4**.
- ✅ **Theatres:** DBT 23–26 °C, RH 50–60 %, ACH 8–12, **NC-25 to NC-30** acoustic criterion, **displacement ventilation** for draft-free.
- ✅ **Textile Mills:** **High humidification** (75–85 % RH) is the primary function, not cooling.
- ✅ **Cleanrooms (Pharma/Semi):** **ISO 14644-1** standard, **20–600+ ACH**, **HEPA/ULPA**, raised perforated floor.
- ✅ **Critical formula:** $Q_{CFM} = \frac{\text{ACH} \times V}{60}$ with V in ft³.
- ✅ **Unit conversions:** 1 TR = 3.517 kW = 12000 BTU/h; 1 CFM = 1.699 m³/h; 1 m = 3.281 ft.
- ✅ **Code references:** ASHRAE 170 (Hospitals), ASHRAE 62.1 (Ventilation), ASHRAE 55 (Comfort), ISO 14644-1 (Cleanrooms), ASHRAE TC 9.9 (Data Centres).
- ✅ **Always include a block diagram** in 7-mark sub-parts.
- ✅ **Always justify** the "why" — examiners award 2–3 marks for reasoning, not just values.
